title: Add event budget and first-day pacing guardrails to endDay
description:
## Why
A fresh-save `End Day` can surface roughly 23 pending events at once. The current queue covers event integrity and expiry cleanup, but not first-day pacing, event-budgeting, or queue-governance. This overwhelms the opening and turns authored world beats into noise.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a fresh save on day 1.
2. Trigger `End Day` once from the dashboard.
3. Open the event modal / inspect pending events.
4. Observe that a large burst of pending events is queued immediately, overwhelming the opening flow.

## What
Add an end-of-day event scheduling policy with first-hour pacing limits, event-budget caps, and progressive disclosure so a single tick cannot flood the player with low-priority pending events.

## Why now
This is a first-session comprehension failure and a systemic quality hole. If `End Day` explodes the queue on day 1, the opening fantasy and every subsequent event chain lose clarity.

## Player impact
Ending day 1 produces a readable, prioritized set of events instead of an overwhelming dump. Important opening beats are surfaced first and minor world churn is deferred.

## System impact
Touches `endDay`, event evaluation/scheduling, possible event metadata for priority/deferability, and playthrough verification.

## Does not include
- no procedural quest generation expansion
- no broad rewrite of every authored event text

## Evidence
User report on 2026-05-17. Code evidence: `endDay.ts` chains many event-producing systems in one tick; `evaluateEvents.ts` only checks conditions/cooldown, not a day-budget. Existing first-hour playthrough does not assert event-volume limits after `End Day`.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** A fresh-save day-1 `End Day` does not dump an overwhelming stack of pending events; opening events are paced and prioritized.
2. **System / technical:** The event pipeline enforces a documented budget/governance rule (caps, deferment, or priority queue) for first-hour and generic day ticks.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add regression coverage for fresh-save `End Day` event volume and priority behavior; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F3 — Day-1 `End Day` creates ~23 pending events.

title: Define canonical house political footing and fix day-one faction seat truth
description:
## Why
`/Users/andre.dittrich/privat/projects/project_destiny/src/ui/screens/FactionsScreen.tsx` currently computes `restoredWardSeats` by summing all `councilSeats`, which surfaces `Restored ward seats 7` on day 1. That contradicts the opening fiction that House Valdris begins stripped of power.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start a fresh save on day 1.
2. Open the Factions screen.
3. Read the `House Political Footing` section.
4. Observe that `Restored ward seats` is shown as `7` even though the opening fiction says the house lost its seats.

## What
Introduce a canonical political-footing selector contract that distinguishes city/faction chamber composition from House-controlled restored seats and sponsor channels, then update the Factions screen to use the correct values and wording.

## Why now
This is a day-one fiction contradiction on a core overview screen. It breaks the core fantasy of rebuilding from ruin and makes the world state untrustworthy.

## Player impact
The factions screen truthfully communicates how weak or connected the house actually is at the start, and only shows restored seats once they are genuinely earned.

## System impact
Touches political/renown selectors, the Factions screen, initial state assumptions, and screen/selector tests.

## Does not include
- no full politics rebalance
- no new council-vote content

## Evidence
User report on 2026-05-17. Code evidence: `restoredWardSeats = Object.values(councilSeats).reduce(...)` in `src/ui/screens/FactionsScreen.tsx`; initial state in `data/runtime/initial-game-state.json` seeds other factions with seats. Existing `OverviewScreens.test.tsx` does not assert day-1 restored-seat truth.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** On day 1 the Factions screen does not claim House Valdris has restored ward seats it has not earned; sponsor access and literal seats are clearly distinct.
2. **System / technical:** A selector or equivalent application contract exposes separate values for house-controlled restored seats, sponsor channels, and faction chamber composition.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add selector/screen regression tests for fresh-save political footing; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F2 — `Restored ward seats 7` on day 1.

## Fiction contract
House Valdris begins politically diminished. The UI must not imply restored institutional power before the player earns it.

title: Fix router-safe dashboard directive navigation and session continuity
description:
## Why
The dashboard still uses raw `<a href>` directive links in `/Users/andre.dittrich/privat/projects/project_destiny/src/ui/screens/DashboardScreen.tsx` for the Work Board and recommended quest actions. In SPA play this can trigger a hard navigation/bootstrap path that feels like the game restarted instead of preserving the current session.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start from a fresh save on the dashboard.
2. Ensure there is no active quest and the first-run Work Board CTA is visible.
3. Click `→ Check the Work Board`.
4. Observe whether the app hard-navigates / reloads and appears to restart rather than preserving the current in-memory session.

## What
Replace raw directive anchors on dashboard/work-board-facing surfaces with router-safe navigation and add an explicit route-continuity contract for fresh-save and mid-session flows.

## Why now
This is a live player-facing trust break in the first hour. If the player thinks a primary CTA restarts the game, all other quest and house improvements lose credibility.

## Player impact
Clicking `Check the Work Board` or the recommended quest action preserves the current session and takes the player to the expected surface without feeling like a reset.

## System impact
Touches `DashboardScreen`, any similar directive-link surfaces, route wiring/tests in React Router screens, and possibly shared CTA components.

## Does not include
- no quest-content rewrite
- no broader dashboard visual redesign

## Evidence
User report on 2026-05-17. Code evidence: raw `href` links in `src/ui/screens/DashboardScreen.tsx`. Current tests only assert presence/copy in `src/ui/screens/DashboardScreen.test.tsx`, not route continuity.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** From a fresh save and from an in-progress save, dashboard quest/work-board CTAs navigate in-app without resetting opening state or feeling like a restart.
2. **System / technical:** Dashboard directive links use router-safe navigation rather than raw anchor reload semantics.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add route-level regression tests for dashboard -> work board and dashboard -> recommended quest route; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F4 — Dashboard Work Board quick link appears to restart the game.

title: Add missing quest-gilded-court-favor and fix district-pale-warrens ID
description: Why: two broken content references: (1) event-gilded-court-approach references questId quest-gilded-court-favor which does not exist - quest lead silently fails to create; (2) an event uses sourceDistrictId district-pale-warrens which does not exist (valid IDs are district-the-pale and district-the-warrens). These are silent failures the player cannot diagnose. What: add quest-gilded-court-favor to quests.json OR remove the questId reference. Fix district ID to district-the-pale. Acceptance: no events.json or quests.json reference references an ID that does not exist in the catalog.

title: Standardise house name across all content — Valdric or Valdris
description: narrative.md uses 'House Valdric' (10 references, canonical founding history). data/definitions/npcs.json uses 'House Valdris' (12 references). data/definitions/events.json uses 'House Valdris' (1 reference). pursuePlayerLegacy.ts:129 uses 'House Valdris'. Decision: align all sources to one spelling. Recommendation: if narrative.md is the lore bible, use Valdric everywhere. Update: npcs.json (12 entries), events.json (1 entry), pursuePlayerLegacy.ts (heir originStory). Add a grep CI check that fails if both spellings appear simultaneously. Acceptance: only one spelling exists in the entire codebase and data directory.

title: Author item effects for item-medkit-field and 14 other items with no effects
description:
**Why:** 15 items in data/definitions/items.json have no effects authored:
- item-medkit-field (Field Medkit) — the most common healing consumable has no heal effect
- 5 trade goods (iron ingots, pre-breach mechanism, tallow bricks, salt block, coal sack) — economically inert
- 5 documents (bureau ledger, ledger removal chit, margin note, sealed envelope, lane watch sketch)
- 2 materials (scrap cloth, activated charcoal bricks)
- 1 gift (unfamiliar crest ring)
- 1 stimulant-style item is likely close to done but medkit is the critical gap

**What:**
1. Author effect for item-medkit-field: restoreHealth +25 or +30.
2. Author effects for remaining consumables (dressing kit, tincture, antitoxin, salve, poultice, tonic — check each).
3. For documents: author quest-flag or reveal effects where appropriate.
4. For trade goods: mark as sell-only (no use effect — they are economy goods, not consumables).
5. For the gift: author a relationship boost effect for gifting (once gifting system exists — flag as pending that system).

**Why now:** Players trying to use a medkit in combat or post-mission get no benefit. The medkit is probably the most commonly purchased item.

**Player impact:** Healing items work. Consumables have real value. Players understand what documents do.
**System impact:** items.json content only. No schema changes needed if effects field already exists.

**Non-goals:** Do not build the gifting mechanic here. Do not build the trading mechanic here.

**Acceptance:** All 15 items have either authored effects or a documented reason for having none. pnpm test:run passes.
close_reason: Authored effects for all 15 items: heal for medkit, tradeValue for 5 trade goods and 3 materials, enableAction for 5 quest documents, affinityBonus+enableAction for crest ring. Added tradeValue and enableAction types to itemEffectSchema.

title: Add House Merrow to factionStandings in initial game state
description:
**Why:** faction-house-merrow is defined in data/definitions/factions.json and described as the primary antagonist of the Valdris succession arc. It controls Ash Quay, has a daily agenda hook, and defines three existential fears (Orven Pell's ledger, Torvald Messe's copy, Brannic Thule's testimony). But it is entirely absent from factionStandings in initial-game-state.json. No code can track or change the player's standing with House Merrow.

**What:**
1. Add `faction-house-merrow` to `factionStandings` in data/runtime/initial-game-state.json with a starting value (suggest -10 to -20: they hold Valdris assets and have adversarial alignment).
2. Verify gameStateSchema allows the new key (it should use Record<string,number>).
3. Update any faction-list selectors if they filter to a known list.
4. Add House Merrow standing display to the Factions screen.

**Why now:** The antagonist faction being invisible in the tracking system is a narrative and mechanical gap. Quest consequences that affect Merrow cannot be applied or shown.

**Player impact:** Player can now see and track their Merrow standing. Quest rewards/penalties affecting Merrow become meaningful.
**System impact:** factionStandings gains one more key. Schema and initial state must stay in sync.

**Non-goals:** Do not author full Merrow quest arc here — just add the tracking infrastructure.

**Acceptance:** Factions screen shows House Merrow with correct starting standing. pnpm test:run passes. pnpm typecheck passes.

title: Fix ESLint errors: 5 active errors blocking CI confidence
description:
**Why:** 5 ESLint errors exist in production code — prefer-const, no-unused-vars (x3), no-useless-assignment — plus 1 warning. These are active CI failures and signal accumulated dead code.

**What:** Fix all 5 errors:
1. applyRumorSpread.ts:223 — change `let next` to `const next`
2. dialogue.ts:165 — remove or use `_removed`
3. grief.ts:36 — remove or use `_relationships`
4. gameSlice.ts:837 — fix `sellPrice` dead assignment (verify sell price logic is correct)
5. ItemCard.tsx:14 — remove or use `_instanceId`
Also remove unused eslint-disable in adjustRelationship.ts:26.

**Why now:** Lint errors erode confidence in the quality gates and mask real bugs. The sellPrice dead assignment may mean players are getting wrong sell prices.

**Player impact:** Fixing sellPrice may correct sell-price calculations. Rest is developer hygiene.
**System impact:** gameSlice.ts sell logic should be verified for correctness, not just linted clean.

**Non-goals:** Do not refactor beyond the lint fixes.

**Acceptance:** pnpm lint exits 0. pnpm test:run still passes 757+ tests.

title: Make Mira and house-story progression depend on player action instead of passive day gates
description: Why: the current story progression can advance Mira's arc through elapsed days and generic quest counts, including an automatic rescue-stage transition in political/world progression that bypasses the dedicated rescue quest. That breaks player agency and collapses the emotional weight of the main story. What: rework main-quest progression so story stages advance through explicit player-triggered leads, dialogues, discoveries, and completed story actions rather than passive timers alone.
design: The main story should use timers only for urgency and world reaction. They must not substitute for rescue, revelation, or confrontation.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Mira cannot be 'rescued' without the actual rescue path being completed. 2. Passive day or politics processing cannot silently move the story into a rescued state. 3. Story stages advance through explicit in-world actions or authored story beats. 4. Passive time can add pressure, rumors, or consequences, but cannot replace the core story action itself.

title: Quest rewards: rewardMarks and standing delta never applied on completion
description: CRITICAL BUG: Quest templates define rewardMarks, rewardStandingFactionId, rewardStandingDelta but these are never read when a quest completes. Players complete investigations and combats but receive zero Marks or standing. Only renown is applied. Fix: in the combat victory path and mission success path, read the linked quest template and apply rewardMarks to state.money, rewardStandingDelta to state.factionStandings, and log the reward. Also apply penaltyStandingDelta for expired quests (currently only missions get this).

title: Main quest: no endgame after 'rescued' stage
description: Main quest progresses searching→lead-found→location-known→rescued. After 'rescued', DashboardScreen shows 'Mira is out. She is not the same. Neither are you.' and nothing more happens. No epilogue content, no endgame quests, no new faction dynamics, no win state or reflection. The campaign just… continues as normal. Need: post-rescue content (epilogue quests, faction reshuffling, narrative payoff, or a soft win screen).

title: Institutional standing: cosmetic only, never enforced
description: institutionalStanding per faction (hostile/blacklisted/watched/neutral/allied) is set and logged but never checked downstream. Being 'blacklisted' with a faction should block their shop, prevent their NPC recruits, and gate faction quests. Being 'hostile' should trigger active antagonism events. Currently the entire reputation tier system is display-only. Need enforcement points: shop access check, quest filter, recruitment filter, and event triggers at hostile/blacklisted.

title: Delivery and survival quest types: no UI or mechanics
description: Quests with objectiveType 'delivery' and 'survival' exist in quests.json but ContractBoardScreen only routes to InvestigationScreen (investigation) or CombatScreen (combat). Accepting a delivery/survival quest creates an activeQuest entry with no way to complete it — permanent quest backlog clog. Need dedicated screens or wire these types into existing mission flows. ~20% of quest types are silently broken.

title: Vault quest chain: unlock path is opaque to player
description: The Vault (Cellar/Vault room) is locked and shows 'Sealed. Requires a key or a quest.' but players have no visible quest or hint about how to unlock it. The vaultUnlocked flag is only set by the unlockVault reducer which is never called from any current quest or event. The main quest narrative pivots on the vault but the path to unlocking it is a complete dead end. Need: (a) a quest or event chain that leads to unlockVault, (b) visible hint in the UI about how to proceed.

title: Fix shop navigation dead end in districts
description: Shops show 'travel to district' but provide no navigation link — a dead end UX flow. Add navigation links from shop UI to district view. Audit all district-related navigation for similar dead ends and fix.

title: Add tooltips to all stats, dials, and standing indicators
description: Zero tooltips exist on any stat bars, renown counter, city dials, or faction standing — players cannot learn what anything means. Add hover/tap tooltips to: all NPC attribute bars, renown (what it unlocks), city dials (what each affects), faction standing (current tier, next threshold, what it gates), equipment stats.

title: Dashboard cognitive overload: split into 3 focused zones
description: Dashboard has 18 sections causing severe cognitive overload. Split into 3 distinct zones: (1) Command Center — active quests, day controls, alerts; (2) Roster — NPC management, status, assignments; (3) City Overview — faction standings, district dials, economy. Add navigation between zones. Each zone should have at most 5-6 sections.

title: Marion Vale: expand to reactive companion arc
description: Marion Vale has only 3 dialogue nodes and no state-reactive content — the game's most prominent NPC is a placeholder. Expand to 10+ dialogue events tied to game state (debt level, faction standing, roster size, combat outcomes). Add 5 branching dialogue choices with relationship tracking. She should feel like a co-protagonist, not a quest board.

title: Faction standing: add concrete gates and consequences
description: Faction standings change based on quests but gate absolutely nothing — no district access, no exclusive hires, no quest availability. Wire standings to: district access locks, exclusive NPC hire offers, quest tier unlocks, dialogue options, and passive income grants. Make faction reputation feel meaningful.

title: Debt and economic pressure rebalancing
description: 500M debt is solvable in 2-3 sessions — no sustained threat after that. Once debt resolves there is no escalating pressure. Redesign: raise debt ceiling or shorten repayment window, introduce escalating threats (debt collectors, faction hostility, district deterioration) that ramp if ignored. Pressure must not fully resolve without player action.
close_reason: Raised debt to 800M, added interest >day15, added faction warning at day20

title: Wire city dials to economic and systemic consequences
description: City dials (Prosperity/Control/Unrest/Corruption) produce numbers but nothing reads them — they have no economic consequences. Faction standings change but gate nothing. Connect dials to: quest availability, hire costs, passive income rates, district event probabilities. This root cause (systems produce state but nothing consumes it) also applies to faction standing gates.

title: Enforce renown roster gating in recruitNpc
description: Renown-based roster size limits are defined but never enforced in recruitNpc — players can hire unlimited NPCs. Enforce the gate: check renown tier before hire, show NPC rarity in hire UI, surface the cap to the player so it feels like a meaningful progression gate.

title: Fix guard spam degenerate strategy
description: Guard action is a free action (no cost) with 55% damage reduction — a dominant, low-skill strategy that trivializes combat. Rebalance: add stamina/action cost to Guard, cap guard frequency per round, or reduce damage reduction to ~25-30%.

title: Wire dead combat mechanics: stagger flag and evasionPenalty
description: The 'staggered' status flag is set on enemies but never checked anywhere — dead code. The 'evasionPenalty' field on armor is never read or applied. Both represent mechanics scaffolding that was built but never connected. Wire them into the combat resolution loop.

title: Break up endDay.ts into focused command modules
description: endDay.ts is 969 lines handling 10+ independent systems (income, events, NPC state, faction drift, etc.) making it untestable and fragile. Extract each system into its own command/service module. Also contains 16 Math.random() calls making state non-deterministic — inject a seeded RNG to enable deterministic testing.

title: State versioning and save migration system
description: No state versioning exists — schema changes silently discard player saves. Need a versioned save schema with migration path so players don't lose progress on game updates. Implement a version field in Redux state, a migration registry, and upgrade logic run on load.

title: Onboarding: first-run directive after opening screen
description:
After OpeningScreen, player lands on Operations with zero direction. No tutorial, no CTA, no highlighted action. Activity log says 'Nothing has been logged yet'. 11-item nav. Player has no idea what to do.

UX SPECIALIST (most damaging issue): 'Lost players at session start are not recoverable. Every other issue is recoverable through iteration. Fix onboarding before anything else.'
GAME DESIGNER agrees: 'No feedback loop visible. Player cannot tell if management actions matter.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Add isFirstRun flag to game state (set false after first End Day)
- On Operations, when isFirstRun=true: show a persistent directive banner
  'House Valdric has debts and one open contract. → Work Board'
- Highlight the Work Board nav item with a subtle pulse when unvisited
- Remove directive after player has accepted first quest
- Add one contextual log entry on day 1: 'Marion has reviewed the ledger. There is one contract available.'

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- New player sees a clear first action on Operations
- Directive disappears after first quest accepted
- No intrusive tutorial — one line, one arrow
close_reason: First-run directive banner renders on DashboardScreen when isFirstRun:true, links to /contracts, set false on endDay. 8 equip tests pass. Verifier finding fixed: slot buttons now show item names.

title: Implement equip gear — reducer, item-selection modal, selector export
description:
The Equip gear button in NpcDetailPanel.tsx has disabled hardcoded, no onClick, no reducer, no modal. Equipment cannot be assigned to any NPC. The loadout schema exists in game state but is completely invisible to player agency.

ALL SPECIALISTS agree this is P0.
GAME DESIGNER: 'Equipment is the primary external expression of NPC value. The player should feel that kitting out a character matters.'
UX SPECIALIST: 'False affordance — disabled button implies not-yet-unlocked or requires-items, not broken. Actively erodes trust in the UI.'

REQUIRED:
1. gameSlice.ts: Add equipItem(npcId, slot, itemId) reducer
2. selectRosterDetail: Export loadout in return value
3. NpcDetailPanel: onClick opens item-selection modal filtered by slot
4. ItemSelectionModal: lists owned weapons/armor/accessories, dispatches equipItem
5. Remove disabled attr from button

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Player can open modal, select an item, assign to primaryWeapon/armor slot
- Equipped item appears in durability panel
- Can unequip (set slot to null)
- Tests cover reducer
close_reason: ItemSelectionModal implemented with slot-filtered weapon/armor selection. Weapons/armor correctly show all catalog items (no inventory tracking for equipment yet — separate bead created). Verifier: item names now shown in slot buttons. 327 tests pass.

title: Dark fantasy typography — IM Fell English + Crimson Pro
description:
Zero web fonts. System fonts everywhere. Typography is the fabric of the entire UI — every name, header, badge, label.

ART DIRECTOR (escalated to P0): 'Font is applied to every character name, every screen title, every badge, every header. Switching takes 2 hours and transforms the aesthetic globally. The portrait problem takes months. Do the font first.'
GAME DESIGNER agrees: 'Low effort, high signal. This one is worth doing this week.'

IMPLEMENTATION (index.html + App.css):
1. Add to index.html:
   <link href='https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IM+Fell+English:ital@0;1&family=Crimson+Pro:ital,wght@0,300;0,400;0,600;1,400&display=swap' rel='stylesheet'>
2. Add CSS tokens:
   --font-display: 'IM Fell English', Georgia, serif;
   --font-body: 'Crimson Pro', Palatino, serif;
3. Apply: h1,h2 + .npc-identity-name + .sidebar-brand + .opening-house-name → display font
4. Apply: body, .stat-label, .badge, .nav-link → body font

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Display font on all headings and NPC names
- Body font replaces system font everywhere
- No regression on readability at small sizes
- Build passes
close_reason: IM Fell English + Crimson Pro applied globally

title: Wire event outcomes — all 17 events have empty outcomes array
description:
All 17 events in events.json have outcomes:[]. Every player choice evaporates. No faction standing delta, no follow-up event, no log entry.

NARRATIVE DESIGNER (P0): 'Choices exist without consequences. This actively trains players to stop caring about events. After 2-3 events that lead nowhere, players stop reading them.'
GAME DESIGNER (P1, raised to P0 in synthesis): 'Core loop feedback void. The event system is theater not agency.'

MINIMUM OUTCOME STRUCTURE per choice:
{
  factionStanding: { faction-id: +/-N },
  followUpEventId: event-id or null,
  logEntry: authored text line
}

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Every event choice writes at least one state delta (standing, flag, or log)
- At least 5 events have a followUpEventId creating a 2-step chain
- evaluateEvents can condition on flags set by prior events
- No choice silently disappears
close_reason: Event outcomes wired on 17 events with faction standing deltas and log entries. Verifier passed.

title: P0: Production build fails — CSS media query malformed in App.css
description: App.css around line 749: media query block has a bare property (grid-template-columns: 1fr) with no selector, and an extra closing brace at ~line 790. lightningcss minifier aborts. pnpm build cannot complete. Fix the responsive block structure.
close_reason: Missing .app-shell selector in media query fixed. Build passes.

title: Critical bug sprint: schema conflicts, type safety, non-deterministic reducer
description:
Four critical bugs found in architecture audit that silently corrupt game state or bypass type safety.

1. Math.random() inside resolveCouncilVote reducer (gameSlice.ts) — Redux reducers must be deterministic. Move randomness to a thunk; pass pre-rolled outcome as action payload.

2. as-any cast on institutionalStanding tier (gameSlice.ts) — payload typed as string bypasses InstitutionalTier enum. Change to typed payload.

3. politicalDials vs cityDials — same schema, conflicting values, unclear source of truth. Merge into one field or document the split. Selector references cityDials; endDay patches cityDials.

4. factionStandings (flat Record) vs factionStates[].standingWithPlayer — two conflicting faction standing representations. factionStandings is authoritative. Stop updating standingWithPlayer everywhere.

5. applyEventOutcome casts outcome.target as keyof typeof cityDials without validation — runtime error on invalid dial name.

6. Zod parse on initial-game-state has no try/catch — app crashes with cryptic error on schema violation.
close_reason: All 6 critical bugs fixed: deterministic reducer, typed payload, unified dials, unified standings, validated cast, error boundary.

title: Model direct world-NPC relationship dynamics beyond household growth
description:
## Why
The project already has soft bonds, pairing, household growth, custody, and faction politics, but there is no explicit ticket for direct world-NPC to world-NPC relationship dynamics as an independent system. Rivalry, protection, dependency, patronage, resentment, and escalation between non-player actors are still too indirect and often collapse into logs or household-level flavor.

## What
Add direct relationship dynamics between non-player actors: world NPCs can strengthen alliances, form patron-client ties, feud, protect one another, become dependent, intervene in each other's custody/household situations, and produce lasting social consequences that are not routed only through the player. Wire those dynamics into rumors, site access, quest emergence, and conflict escalation.

## Why now
The simulation substrate is now strong enough that this missing social layer is visible as a gap. Without it, the world is more alive than before, but still not fully agentic between non-player actors.

## Player impact
The city feels less player-centric and more like a place where other people make and break one another's fortunes even when the player is absent.

## System impact
Touches world NPC social simulation, site presence, household interactions, rumors, possibly quest lead generation and faction politics.

## Does not include
No full romance rewrite and no complete household economy overhaul.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. At least three direct non-player relationship dynamics exist beyond generic soft bonds, such as patronage, rivalry escalation, or protective intervention.
2. Those dynamics can alter later access, rumors, custody outcomes, or quest opportunities.
3. The effects persist across endDay ticks and do not require the player to be the initiator.

## Finding coverage
npc-dynamics-audit-2026-06-01 F1 F2

title: Make NPC and household actions materially affect district and city economy
description:
## Why
NPC actions currently touch player money, faction standing, household growth, and activity logs, but they rarely move the shared city economy in a material way. This makes the world feel socially active but economically too decoupled.

## What
Wire NPC, household, and faction actions into actual economic state changes: district scarcity or relief, city resource pressure, market or labor pressure, debt enforcement, household resource attrition, and rival extraction or disruption. The goal is that NPC-led actions can change economic conditions even when the player is passive.

## Why now
The project already has NPC agency, faction activity, title effects, bond service, world households, and household growth. Without a shared economic consequence layer, these systems remain partially parallel.

## Player impact
The economy will feel like something actors struggle over, not just a player-owned balance sheet.

## System impact
Touches NPC agency, faction activity, household growth, bond service, city resources, district pressure, and possibly shop pricing or quest generation.

## Does not include
No full market simulation or commodity-by-commodity economy.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. At least three existing NPC/faction/household action paths change economic world state beyond player money alone.
2. Those changes can be observed in city resources, district-level economic pressure, household resources, or downstream pricing/availability.
3. The effects persist across endDay ticks and can influence later systems.

## Finding coverage
npc-economy-audit-2026-06-01 F3 F4

title: Balance non-player household growth and add real inter-house competition
description:
## Why
The current world-household growth loop is too generous and mostly parallel. It lets several top-funded households grow with very high probability, without paying meaningful costs or contesting the same opportunities. That weakens the living-world goal and risks making growth feel automatic instead of political.

## What
Rebalance non-player household growth so it consumes resources, faces scarcity and conflict pressure, and can be slowed, diverted, or blocked by rival households. Translate authored `activeConflicts` into actual simulation effects such as contested expansion, sabotage pressure, legal choke points, or reputation/security tradeoffs.

## Why now
The new site runtime, household growth, and abstract custody layers are in place. Without competition and cost now, later world-household content will rest on a too-easy substrate.

## Player impact
Rival houses will no longer simply get bigger in parallel. Their expansion pace and room functions will reflect pressure, scarcity, and conflict, making the city feel more alive and less mechanically generous.

## System impact
Touches world-household growth, site runtime persistence, authored conflict data, event/rumor generation, and possibly faction or district pressure systems.

## Does not include
No full city economy overhaul. No complete rebuild of every household authoring record.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Non-player household growth consumes or commits household resources instead of being free.
2. At least one authored `activeConflict` type materially changes a rival household's growth odds or outcome.
3. Households with weak resources or active opposition no longer grow at near-automatic rates.
4. Competition outcomes can surface as visible logs, rumors, or state changes.

## Finding coverage
household-growth-audit-2026-06-01 F1 F2

title: Generate quest and event hooks from site, room, and custody state
description:
## Why
If rooms, sites, and custody become real but content generation remains district/faction-only, the new substrate will feel systemic but dramatically underused.

## What
Extend quest/event generation so site, room, and custody state can create leads and consequences.

Examples:
- hidden captive rumor
- transfer in progress
- compromised archive room
- servant corridor witness
- sanctuary under pressure
- site expansion displacing residents

## Why now
This is the narrative bridge that turns location simulation into playable RPG content.

## Player impact
The player gets rescue, protection, infiltration, and witness stories that arise from the city's physical and social layout.

## System impact
Extends quest/event generation and should coordinate with destiny-2c2q once site runtime lands.

## Does not include
No campaign rewrite. No requirement that every site generate content equally often.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Site and room state can act as quest/event causes.
2. Custody, shelter, and household pressure can create leads without bespoke one-off logic every time.
3. Resulting hooks can surface through rumors, dialogue, logs, or discovered evidence.

## Finding coverage
F3 F6
close_reason: Implemented generic site/custody hooks: abstract captivity rumors, known-room custody leads, and protected-site pressure events.

title: Define site visibility and reveal hierarchy for abstract and concrete locations
description:
## Why
A hybrid site model will fail in play if the UI reveals either too much concrete room detail too early or too little for the player to act on. The project needs a clear visibility grammar for abstract, site, and room levels.

## What
Define the player-facing visibility and reveal rules for abstract and concrete sites.

Cover:
- what district level can show about a site
- what site level can show before room entry
- what room-level occupancy and custody becomes visible and when
- rumor vs clue vs witness report vs confirmed knowledge
- how hidden custody stays believable without feeling arbitrary

## Why now
This is the comprehension guardrail for the whole hybrid-location direction.

## Player impact
The player can understand where they are, what they know, and what they still need to discover.

## System impact
Connects site runtime to rumors, quests, logs, POI presentation, and future room screens.

## Does not include
No full visual redesign. No combat UI changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract/site/room information boundaries are documented and actionable.
2. Hidden, rumored, and known occupancy/custody states are clearly distinguished.
3. Follow-up UI work can implement reveal logic without inventing ad hoc rules.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F6
close_reason: Documented site visibility and reveal hierarchy for abstract, site, and room layers; defined knowledge states, custody reveal ladder, and collapse-back visibility contract in docs/site-visibility-and-reveal-contract.md.

title: Define site visibility and UI hierarchy for abstract and concrete locations
description:
## Why
A hybrid site model will fail in play if the UI reveals either too little or far too much. The player needs a clear distinction between what is rumored, what is visible from district/site level, and what is confirmed at room level.

## What
Define the UI and visibility rules for abstract and concrete sites.

Cover:
- what district cards show about a site
- what site view shows before room entry
- what room-level occupancy becomes visible and when
- how rumors, clues, witness reports, and discovered evidence escalate knowledge
- how hidden custody can remain hidden without becoming arbitrary

## Why now
This is the player-comprehension guardrail for the whole direction.

## Player impact
The world becomes legible without dumping all room detail at the wrong level.

## System impact
- connects site runtime to quest UX, rumors, logs, and POI presentation
- prevents room/custody simulation from becoming invisible or overwhelming

## Does not include
- full visual redesign of all district and site screens
- combat UI work
- free-roam presentation

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; prior UX audits already show that hierarchy and post-action readability are fragile.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract, site, and room-level information boundaries are documented.
2. Hidden vs rumored vs known occupancy/custody is clearly defined.
3. Follow-up UI beads can implement the system without inventing ad hoc reveal logic.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F6
close_reason: Superseded by existing site-hierarchy/UX follow-up direction; kept in review doc as requirement, not separate duplicate bead.

title: Generate quest and event hooks from site, room, and custody state
description:
## Why
Once rooms, households, and custody become real, quests and events should emerge from them. Otherwise the new site model will stay systemic but under-authored, and the player will not feel the dramatic payoff.

## What
Add site-driven quest and event hooks that can originate from room and custody state.

Examples:
- hidden captive rumor
- transfer in progress
- room compromised
- witness in a service wing
- sanctuary under pressure
- household expansion causing displacement
- a guard, steward, or attendant quietly opening access

## Why now
This is the narrative bridge from site simulation into playable story content.

## Player impact
The player encounters rescue, blackmail, protection, infiltration, and shelter stories that arise from the world's physical and social layout.

## System impact
- extends quest/event generation beyond district/faction-only logic
- adapts destiny-2c2q toward room/site-aware emergence

## Does not include
- a full authored campaign rewrite
- every site generating content equally often
- free exploration scenes

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; current Mira and house audits already show the need for stronger place -> clue -> consequence funnels.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Quest/event generation can cite site and room state as causes.
2. Captivity, shelter, and household pressure can create leads without hand-authoring every instance.
3. The player can learn about site problems through rumors, dialogue, access changes, or discovered evidence.

## Finding coverage
F3 F6
close_reason: Superseded by existing site-driven quest/event work under destiny-2c2q plus custody/location substrate wave; kept as review finding, not separate duplicate bead.

title: Implement abstract offscreen site simulation with preserved depth
description:
## Why
The clarified direction explicitly chooses hybrid simulation: abstract sites may exist offscreen, but their depth must not collapse. Without a dedicated offscreen model, the project will either oversimulate every room constantly or reduce unseen sites to shallow counters.

## What
Define and implement abstract offscreen site simulation that preserves narrative depth.

Cover:
- which social/custody states continue offscreen
- food/care/security/secrecy pressure
- room-state compression rules
- captive condition changes and visit frequency
- rumor/event generation from abstract site state
- re-concretization from preserved abstract memory

## Why now
This is the technical and narrative safeguard that makes the chosen hybrid model credible.

## Player impact
Places the player is not currently visiting still feel alive, risky, and consequential.

## System impact
- ties into endDay/world simulation without requiring fully concrete citywide room simulation
- provides the bridge between site runtime and quest/event generation

## Does not include
- always-on full city room simulation
- pathfinding or visual room traversal
- authored quest content by itself

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; c3sh already simulates offscreen social ties at district depth, but not site/custody depth.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract sites preserve more than owner/security; they retain meaningful social and custody memory.
2. Concrete sites can return to abstraction without losing critical narrative state.
3. Offscreen captive and room interactions can still produce believable outcomes and rumors.

## Finding coverage
F3 F4 F5
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-d9o3 (same abstract custody/offscreen depth scope).

title: Spatialize captivity and bond custody at site and room level
description:
## Why
Captivity currently records who holds an NPC, but not where the NPC is physically kept. That is too thin for Mira, bond rescues, detention houses, hidden prisoners, or any living-world custody story.

## What
Extend captivity and bonded custody into a spatial detention model.

Cover:
- siteId and roomId for custody
- custody owner vs physical holder vs local handler
- transfer between rooms and sites
- visible vs hidden custody
- care/neglect/watch intensity as site-aware drivers
- how bondStatus and captivityState interact without collapsing into one schema

## Why now
This is the core bead that makes general captivity believable instead of purely notional.

## Player impact
Rescue, shelter, capture, and transfer stories become concrete and investigatable.

## System impact
- successor to destiny-4n2j, destiny-766c, destiny-og7t
- establishes shared custody semantics for player and non-player actors

## Does not include
- explicit scene depiction of abuse
- capture combat redesign
- pregnancy-specific content authoring

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; quest-mira-rescue uses holding-floor/tannery room language already; content-policy expects consequence-first captivity logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Captive or bonded NPCs can be associated with a site and room, not only a holder.
2. The model distinguishes legal ownership from physical custody.
3. Transfer between rooms/sites is supported as a world event primitive.

## Finding coverage
F2 F3 F6
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-d7lm (same spatial captivity scope).

title: Add shared room occupancy and access model for world sites
description:
## Why
World households already have authored rooms, but rooms are not runtime places with occupancy, access, or role meaning. If enterable buildings should all have rooms and NPCs should interact with those rooms, the project needs a general room occupancy/access model instead of decorative room lists.

## What
Design and implement the shared room runtime vocabulary for all site types.

Cover:
- room identity and room tags/functions
- occupant slots and occupant categories
- visitor vs resident vs staff vs guard vs captive vs patient vs guest
- restricted/locked/private/service access
- room assignment and eviction/transfer rules
- minimum room memory that survives re-abstraction

## Why now
Without this, spatial captivity and NPC room interaction have nowhere to attach.

## Player impact
The player can understand not only that a building has rooms, but what those rooms currently do and who is there.

## System impact
- successor to destiny-5dpf room lists
- creates the vocabulary needed by player house, world households, safehouses, detention houses, and story sites

## Does not include
- patrol/pathfinding
- free-movement exploration
- final UI polish for all site screens

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; data/definitions/worldHouseholds.json already authors room lists but not occupancy logic.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. A shared room occupancy/access model exists for all site types.
2. Captive, resident, staff, guard, and guest are represented distinctly.
3. The model explicitly states what room data persists when a site re-abstracts.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F5
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-ziyp (same occupancy/room substrate scope).

title: Define canonical hybrid site runtime contract
description:
## Why
POIs, world households, player-house rooms, and story locations currently describe the same world layer in different ways. The project needs an explicit contract for how a site becomes concrete, how it returns to abstraction, and what state survives that transition. Without this decision, future captivity, room, and quest work will fork into incompatible implementations.

## What
Define the canonical hybrid site runtime model.

The decision must specify:
- relationship between POI, world household, and site runtime
- what makes a site abstract vs concrete
- triggers for concretization (entry, active quest, custody relevance, social heat, mission target)
- triggers for re-abstraction
- minimum state that survives abstraction (occupants, custody, room tags, damage, secrets, social tension, access restrictions)
- how player-house sites fit the same model without losing bespoke flavor

## Why now
This is the foundation contract for every later room, captivity, and site-driven quest bead.

## Player impact
The world becomes coherent: buildings do not randomly behave as menus in one place and physical sites in another.

## System impact
- architecture/product/workflow docs updated with the hybrid site contract
- follow-up beads can reference one site vocabulary instead of parallel abstractions

## Does not include
- implementation of runtime site entities
- room occupancy mechanics
- captive movement logic
- UI redesign by itself

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; destiny-5dpf non-goal conflict; quest-mira-rescue already uses room language.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. One written contract defines abstract vs concrete sites and concrete->abstract return behavior.
2. The contract names which state must persist through abstraction.
3. Follow-up beads can reference this document instead of redefining site semantics.

## Finding coverage
F1 F4 F5 F6
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-m684 (same contract scope).

title: World Sites: Hybrid room, custody, and offscreen-depth simulation
description:
## Why
Project Destiny's lore, quest content, and existing worldHousehold data already imply a city of meaningful interiors, hidden custody, household pressure, and room-bound leverage. But the runtime still splits POIs, world households, captivity state, and bond ownership into parallel fragments. Enterable buildings are not yet true sites, captivity is holder-based instead of place-based, and offscreen buildings cannot preserve deep social state. This blocks believable rescue stories, house politics, bond custody, hiding, sheltering, and NPC-driven use of space.

## What
Create the umbrella epic for a hybrid world-site simulation model:
- all enterable buildings use a shared site/room vocabulary
- any NPC can be held in custody at a site and room
- NPCs can interact with rooms and captives
- offscreen abstract simulation preserves depth
- concrete sites can later return to abstract state without losing critical memory

This epic coordinates successor work to extend the current worldHousehold, captivityState, and bond systems instead of replacing them ad hoc.

## Why now
The current direction has now been clarified explicitly. The world already contains authored households, room lists, captivity states, and location-bound story language. Without a coordinated follow-up, future Mira, bond-service, and world-NPC work will become contradictory.

## Player impact
- buildings feel like institutions with interiors, not menu labels
- captives and dependents exist somewhere, under someone, for reasons the player can discover
- rescue, shelter, and infiltration stories gain credible spatial logic
- offscreen sites still generate believable consequences and rumors

## System impact
- establishes successor work for world households, rooms, captivity, bond custody, NPC co-presence, and site-driven quests/events
- aligns player-house room logic with a wider world-site model
- creates a shared contract for concrete <-> abstract site transitions

## Does not include
- free-roam movement
- always-on full simulation of every room in the city
- immediate replacement of existing POI or house systems
- Mira-specific quest scripting by itself

## Evidence
See docs/world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md. Existing support/conflict points include destiny-5dpf, destiny-4n2j, destiny-og7t, destiny-766c, destiny-c3sh, quest-mira-rescue, and worldHouseholds.json.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. A complete child-bead set exists for site contract, room occupancy/access, spatial captivity, abstract offscreen site simulation, and site-driven quest/event hooks.
2. Existing neighboring epics note the new dependency or scope interaction where relevant.
3. Finding coverage is explicit and no major gap is left unmapped.

## Finding coverage
F1 enterable houses need a general room model
F2 any NPC can be captive and captivity must be spatial
F3 NPCs must interact with rooms and captives
F4 abstract simulation must preserve depth
F5 concretized sites may later return to abstraction
F6 current quests and lore already imply place-based custody

## Success Criteria
- The project has a single agreed hybrid site model for concrete and abstract locations.
- Captivity, bond custody, and room-based NPC interaction are represented as successor work instead of fragmented follow-ups.
- Neighboring world, quest, and house epics are explicitly linked to the new site model.
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-a23y epic and destiny-m684 ADR discovered after creation during backlog reconciliation.

title: General captivity runtime: any NPC held in a specific room under a specific holder
description:
## Why
Current captivity modeling covers condition, compliance, and bond only loosely, but not where the NPC is, in which room they are held, who physically controls the site, or how that changes. That is too thin for the clarified direction.

## What
Extend captivity into a world-bound runtime model:
- any NPC may be captive, missing, transferred, hidden, or rescued
- captivity references location instance and room instance when concrete
- captivity references holder or controlling actor and custody regime when abstract
- custody state can be moved, hidden, visited, guarded, supplied, or degraded

## Why now
Mira, bond-market rescues, detention houses, prisoners, and hostile households all need the same substrate.

## Player impact
Captive NPCs become real world targets with location, holder, and rescue context.

## System impact
Expands or refactors captivity contracts and the commands/selectors that currently assume captivity is only a status on roster NPCs.

## Does not include
No full dialogue content pass. No UI panel redesign beyond what the runtime requires.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Captivity can be represented for roster NPCs, story NPCs, and world NPCs.
2. Concrete captivity stores room and location identity.
3. Abstract captivity still stores enough regime and state detail to drive deep consequences.

## Finding coverage
F2 F3 F4
close_reason: Extended captivity into a canonical world-bound runtime with siteId, roomId, regime, transfer metadata, and a global npcCaptivityStates registry; synced roster mirrors and end-of-day degradation across roster and non-roster NPCs; added schema and regression coverage.

title: ADR: hybrid abstract-to-concrete location simulation contract
description:
## Why
The current codebase has no canonical answer for when a location is content only, when it has runtime state, and how it becomes concrete or abstract again. That decision must be explicit before implementation starts.

## What
Define the authoritative architecture contract for hybrid locations:
- abstract location runtime vs concrete room runtime
- what data persists in both states
- what events cause concretization
- what data survives collapse back to abstract state
- how POIs, world households, and the player house relate
- how occupancy and captivity reference locations and rooms

## Why now
This prevents incompatible ad hoc implementations in house rooms, bond rescue, captivity, and world-NPC scheduling.

## Player impact
Indirect but critical: consistent location behavior across the whole game.

## System impact
Architecture and domain-contract decision only.

## Does not include
No reducer or UI implementation.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Abstract vs concrete location state is defined with field-level persistence rules.
2. Enterable POIs, world households, and the player house have a unified conceptual model.
3. Collapse-back rules preserve consequences but may discard transient room detail.

## Finding coverage
F1 F2 F3 F5

title: General location runtime: occupancy and room-instance substrate
description:
## Why
Current rooms are player-house only, world households are content-only, and world NPCs only support a coarse location override. There is no substrate for room occupancy across the world.

## What
Implement a general runtime substrate for locations and room instances that can serve player house rooms, enterable POI interiors, world household rooms, and future detention cells, infirmaries, archives, taverns, and safe houses.

Each concretized location must support room instances, occupancy slots, access or security state, and named room roles.

## Why now
This is the shared base for visible NPC room presence, location-bound captivity, and future enterable-house interaction.

## Player impact
NPCs can visibly be in rooms, not just in abstract menus.

## System impact
New runtime model for location instances and room occupancy. Existing player-house room state becomes a special case or adapter onto the general model.

## Does not include
No deep captivity logic yet. No location economy simulation yet.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. One runtime model can represent player-house rooms and non-player locations.
2. Occupancy can reference an NPC and a room instance.
3. Existing house-room functionality remains representable through the new model.

## Finding coverage
F1 F4
close_reason: Added canonical site runtime and NPC site presence schemas, adapters for player house/world households/POIs, selectors for site runtime and occupancy, reducer hooks for presence updates, and schema/test coverage including initial game state update.

title: Hybrid world locations, room occupancy, and general captivity simulation
description:
## Why
Project Destiny currently has three disconnected partial models: player house rooms, authored world households as content only, and person-level captivity without a concrete location. The user's clarified direction changes this materially: all enterable houses should have rooms; captivity must be fully general for any NPC; offscreen simulation should be hybrid (abstract until relevant, then concrete), but abstract simulation must still carry deep social and psychological consequences; concretized locations may later collapse back to abstract state.

Without a dedicated architecture wave, the current code will drift into incompatible special cases: player-house-only room occupancy, bond-market-only captive handling, and world households that remain static lore records.

## What
Create the architecture and implementation backlog for a general world-location substrate that supports:
- all enterable houses and establishments having room structures
- location-bound occupancy for NPCs
- fully general captivity for any NPC, including foreign NPCs and world NPCs
- deep abstract offscreen simulation for custody, care, abuse, dependency, rumor, and rescue pressure
- on-demand concretization of locations when entered or when a story/system demands detail
- later collapse of concretized locations back into abstract runtime state without losing important consequences

## Why now
This is now a product-direction decision, not optional future polish. The current captivity and house systems already create content and state that will become migration debt if this substrate is delayed.

## Player impact
Locations stop feeling like menu labels. Captivity becomes a real world state. NPCs, households, and factions can hold, move, guard, visit, feed, exploit, rescue, and hide people in actual places.

## System impact
Touches world location architecture, room contracts, occupancy runtime, captivity runtime, world household simulation, ward/household integration, and existing bond/captivity UI assumptions.

## Does not include
No code implementation by this epic alone. No free-roaming exploration commitment. No tactical map rewrite.

## Success Criteria
- a coherent architecture exists for hybrid abstract/concrete locations
- existing related beads are remapped so they do not diverge from the new substrate
- follow-up beads cover occupancy, captivity, abstract simulation, concretization/collapse, and household growth

## Finding coverage
F1 enterable places need rooms; F2 captivity must be explicit and location-bound for any NPC; F3 offscreen simulation must remain deep; F4 NPCs must interact with rooms and captives; F5 concretized places may later collapse back to abstract state.

title: Restore Codex-local verification by routing tooling through compatible system Node
description:
## Why
In the Codex desktop environment, `node` resolves to the app-bundled runtime at `/Users/andre.dittrich/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/node`. That runtime fails to load the ad-hoc signed `rolldown` native binding used by Vite/Vitest, causing test and build startup failures unrelated to app code.

## What
Provide a project-local, reproducible way to run quality gates and frontend tooling with a compatible system Node runtime instead of the Codex-bundled Node.

## Why now
This blocks basic verification inside Codex. Until fixed, agents cannot trust local test/build results even when the code is correct.

## Player impact
No direct player-facing change. Indirectly improves reliability by restoring working verification in the development environment.

## System impact
Touches project scripts/tooling docs and possibly adds helper wrappers for test/typecheck/build commands.

## Does not include
- no dependency version upgrade
- no application feature changes

## Evidence
Observed on 2026-05-28. `vitest` and `vite build` fail under the Codex-bundled Node with `ERR_DLOPEN_FAILED` / `Cannot find native binding` for `@rolldown/binding-darwin-arm64`, while the same commands run under `/opt/homebrew/bin/node`.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** none.
2. **System / technical:** Project-local commands or wrappers exist so Codex/agents can run tests, typecheck, and build with a compatible Node runtime.
3. **Tests / verification:** Fresh runs of test, typecheck, and build succeed using the documented project-local commands.

## Finding coverage
Environment/tooling issue discovered during 2026-05-28 project-state review.
notes: Root cause confirmed: Codex desktop resolves node to /Users/andre.dittrich/Applications/Codex.app/Contents/Resources/node. macOS rejects loading @rolldown/binding-darwin-arm64 under that bundled runtime with Team ID / ad-hoc signature mismatch. Added project-local wrappers under scripts/ that prepend a compatible system Node (/opt/homebrew/bin/node on this machine). Verified ./scripts/codex-test.sh, ./scripts/codex-typecheck.sh, and ./scripts/codex-build.sh all pass.

title: Clarify combat aftermath, loot, and return flow
description:
## Why
Combat resolution currently splits the exit into `Conclude encounter` and later `Return to contracts`, while loot/reward visibility is easy to miss. The code does grant Marks from defeated enemies in `src/application/commands/combat.ts`, but the player-facing aftermath contract does not clearly answer what happened, what was gained, and where the squad returns.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Start or reach a combat encounter.
2. Resolve it to victory or defeat.
3. Click `Conclude encounter`.
4. Observe whether the aftermath clearly states loot/rewards, contract consequence, party condition, and where the player returns next.

## What
Make combat aftermath explicit and readable: outcome, casualties/condition, loot/rewards, contract consequence, and return destination must be surfaced as one coherent post-combat result flow.

## Why now
This is a live player-facing ambiguity in a core RPG loop. Without a trustworthy aftermath surface, combat feels detached from the world and its consequences are hard to read.

## Player impact
After a fight, the player can clearly see the result, any loot or Marks gained, who was hurt, what happened to the contract, and how to return to operations.

## System impact
Touches combat selector/view-model, combat screen post-resolution UI, aftermath summary generation, and combat screen tests/playthroughs.

## Does not include
- no tactical combat redesign
- no enemy-content expansion

## Evidence
User report on 2026-05-17. Code evidence: `concludeCombatEncounter` grants `lootMarks` and writes `lastEncounterSummary`, but `CombatScreen` only weakly surfaces the result and uses a two-step exit. Existing `CombatScreen.test.tsx` checks button presence, not clear aftermath comprehension.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** After victory or defeat, the combat surface clearly shows outcome, loot/rewards, party condition, contract consequence, and an explicit return action/destination.
2. **System / technical:** Combat aftermath data is exposed through a stable selector/view-model contract rather than inferred ad hoc in the screen.
3. **Tests / verification:** Add screen/playthrough regression coverage for victory and defeat aftermath, including loot visibility and exit destination; `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` must pass.

## Finding coverage
F1 — `Conclude and Return` feels broken / loot unclear.

title: Restore green quality gates and add loop-level product verification
description:
## Why
The current quality gates are still too implementation-centric. We have green state tests while shipping route resets, fiction contradictions, event floods, and unclear combat aftermath. `pnpm typecheck` is also currently red due to a wage/bond fixture mismatch and an unused import in `NpcDetailPanel.tsx`, so even baseline gates are not consistently preserved.

## What
Expand Project Destiny's verification contract from first-hour-only checks to loop-level product assertions, and restore baseline green quality gates immediately.

## Why now
Without this, the same class of bugs will recur under green tests. The project needs a stronger definition of done before more content or architecture work.

## Player impact
Players benefit indirectly through fewer regressions in opening flows, navigation, event pacing, and combat aftermath.

## System impact
Touches playthrough coverage, verifier workflows/docs, and the currently failing TypeScript quality gate.

## Does not include
- no full CI platform migration
- no rewrite of all existing tests in one pass

## Evidence
User reports on 2026-05-17 plus current local quality-gate result: `pnpm test:run` passes but `pnpm typecheck` fails in `src/application/commands/applyWages.test.ts` and `src/ui/screens/NpcDetailPanel.tsx`. Closed bead `destiny-6vhh` covered first-hour checks, but not route continuity, day-1 truth, event budgets, or combat-loop aftermath.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Core loop regressions like dashboard route resets, false day-1 political claims, day-1 event floods, and unclear combat exit each gain explicit verification coverage.
2. **System / technical:** Restore `pnpm typecheck` to green and document required loop-level verification layers: state invariant, selector/view-model invariant, screen/route behavior, and at least one player-comprehension playthrough.
3. **Tests / verification:** `pnpm test:run`, `pnpm typecheck`, and `pnpm build` all pass; updated workflow docs/checklists reference the new loop-level gates.

## Finding coverage
Cross-cutting coverage for F1-F4 plus quality-gate failure discovered during 2026-05-17 review.

title: Add catalog integrity check at startup for quest and event cross-references
description: Why: contentCatalog.ts parses templates with Zod but does not validate referential integrity. Quest fields successorQuestId, enemyNpcId, rewardItemIds are all unchecked. An authored quest can reference a deleted NPC with no error. What: add post-parse validation in contentCatalog.ts that cross-references all quest and event fields. Throw in development, log error in production. Acceptance: loading a catalog with a broken cross-reference produces a clear error message naming the file, field, and missing ID.
close_reason: Added validateCatalogIntegrity() at the bottom of contentCatalog.ts. Checks event outcome npcId/questId cross-references and quest successorQuestId, successorOnFailQuestId, enemyNpcId, rewardItemIds. Throws in dev (import.meta.env.PROD=false), console.error in prod. Also fixed a pre-existing data bug: events.json event-gilded-court-approach referenced quest-gilded-court-favor which did not exist; corrected to quest-gilded-auction-guard.

title: Add outcome ID validation in applyEventOutcome
description: Why: applyEventOutcome processes outcome arrays but does not validate that referenced NPC IDs, quest IDs, or arc IDs exist in the catalog. A renamed NPC causes silent partial state update. What: add validation pass at start of applyOutcomes checking each referenced ID against contentCatalog. Log a warning for unresolvable IDs. Do not silently apply partial outcomes. Acceptance: any outcome referencing a missing ID produces a warning and is skipped cleanly.
close_reason: Added warnAndSkip helper and ID validation in applyOutcomes for adjustNpcRelationship, createQuestLead, updateQuestStage, unlockNpc, and addNpcToRoster outcome types. Missing IDs produce a console.warn and skip that outcome.

title: Extract getQuestDaysRemaining to shared utility
description: Why: ContractBoardScreen.tsx:86-88 computes days remaining inline. questLifecycle.ts:68 has equivalent expiry logic. Two independent implementations will diverge - UI will show different days than the system uses for expiration. What: create pure function getQuestDaysRemaining(runtime, template, currentDay): number. Both call sites import it. Acceptance: the formula exists in exactly one place; ContractBoardScreen has no inline date arithmetic.
close_reason: Extracted getQuestDaysRemaining and isQuestExpired into questUtils.ts. Updated ContractBoardScreen.tsx and questLifecycle.ts to use the shared utilities. Formula now lives in exactly one place.

title: Close ward promotion gap - auto-promote young_adult wards
description: Why: applyWardAgeMilestones sets ward.promotedToNpcId when a ward reaches young_adult but does NOT call promoteWardToRoster. The ward stays in state.wards indefinitely - stage shows young_adult but they never appear on the roster. This is a lifecycle dead end. What: either auto-promote in applyWardAgeMilestones after setting the flag, or add an endDay sweep. Acceptance: no ward can remain in young_adult stage for more than 1 game day without appearing on the roster or failing with a logged reason.

title: Events and Quests: Reference Integrity, Expiry Cleanup, and Procedural Generation
description: Three categories of problems: (1) content references broken IDs silently - a missing quest referenced by an event causes silent failure with no player feedback; (2) expired quest leads accumulate in state forever, never pruned; (3) all 335 events and 24 quests are fully authored with no procedural generation - quests do not emerge from world state. Player impact: invisible broken content, no dynamic quest emergence. System impact: no build-time or runtime guardrails catch bad IDs. Source: 05-cross-system-events-and-quests.md
notes:
Live gap identified on 2026-05-17: this epic covers reference integrity, expiry cleanup, and procedural generation, but does NOT cover first-day event pacing or event-budget governance. See destiny-dran for the concrete day-1 pending-event flood regression.
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: future procedural quest/event work should be site-aware, not only faction/district-aware. Follow destiny-f1pl.5 for room/custody/site-generated leads and consequences.

title: Decide canonical ward system and migrate all entry points
description: Why: two ward systems (Heir/houseHeirs vs Ward/state.wards) run in parallel with no collision guard. A ward born via tickPregnancyProgress lands in houseHeirs; event-placed wards can land in either. What: decide which is canonical (recommendation: Ward/state.wards has the better stage model). Migrate all heir entry points to use one system. Archive or remove the other. Acceptance: only one ward collection exists in GameState schema; all ward lifecycle commands operate on it.
notes: Architect review 2026-05-29: canonical ward migration must align with the general location/occupancy substrate so wards and young adults can reside in the same world-location model as other NPCs.

title: Split gameSlice.ts into domain-focused slice files
description: Why: gameSlice.ts is 1421 lines with ~56 command imports. Every new system adds to this file. Testing any reducer requires large state setup. Why now: file is already painful to navigate and causes merge conflicts. What: split into houseSlice.ts, rosterSlice.ts, resourcesSlice.ts, questSlice.ts, combatSlice.ts each under ~200 lines. Root store combines them. Acceptance: gameSlice.ts does not exist or is under 200 lines; all existing tests pass.
close_reason: gameSlice.ts reduced to 83 lines. 10 domain-focused slice files in src/application/store/slices/. All 5742 tests pass.

title: Move getDominantTraitSentences from NpcDetailPanel to selector
description: Why: NpcDetailPanel.tsx:125-134 filters and sorts traits inline using thresholds 65/35/50. These thresholds are game rules. What: create selectNpcCharacterDescription(npcId) returning string[]. Component renders the strings only. System impact: trait thresholds live in domain. Acceptance: no trait threshold logic in any UI component.
close_reason: Moved getDominantTraitSentences logic to selectNpcCharacterDescription selector in roster.ts. CharacterSection now uses useAppSelector with the new selector. Removed inline function and TRAIT_LABELS/TRAIT_LOW_LABELS from NpcDetailPanel.

title: Move NPC income formula from NpcDetailPanel to selector
description: Why: NpcDetailPanel.tsx:238-251 computes working income inline (bestSkill / 7, clamped 3-15). The same formula exists independently in roster.ts:9-12 and applyTitleEffects.ts:349-350. Three independent implementations will silently diverge. Why now: also blocks the UI/UX income breakdown work. What: export WORKING_INCOME_SKILLS and calculateWorkingIncome(skills) from selectors/roster.ts. NpcDetailPanel reads selectEstimatedNpcIncome(npcId). System impact: income rule exists in one place. Player impact: accurate income display. Acceptance: no income formula in any UI component; all three call sites use shared code.
close_reason: Consolidated income formula into computeWorkingIncome in roster.ts as single source of truth. Added selectEstimatedNpcIncome selector. NpcDetailPanel uses useAppSelector instead of inline calc. applyTitleEffects.ts imports computeWorkingIncome and drops its local copy.

title: NPC System: Merge Dual Ward Systems and Fix Lifecycle Gaps
description: Two parallel ward systems run simultaneously: Heir/houseHeirs (stages child to ward to apprentice to adult) and Ward/state.wards (stages infant to child to teenager to young_adult). Both run in endDay with no collision prevention. Additionally, wards reaching young_adult stage are never automatically promoted to the roster - they are permanently stranded. Player impact: confusing heir/ward state, silently incomplete NPC lifecycle. System impact: brittle endDay pipeline. Source: 02-cross-system-npc-consistency.md
notes:
Architect review 2026-05-29: ward lifecycle epic now depends on the general occupancy/location substrate because household presence should not remain player-house-only.
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: child/ward lifecycle work should assume households and sites as physical contexts, not abstract containers only, once destiny-f1pl lands.

title: Clean Architecture: Eliminate Layer Boundary Violations
description: Several screens compute game rules inline and import directly from commands, violating the UI to Application to Domain dependency rule. These violations make rules untestable, cause duplication, and will silently diverge as the codebase grows. Fixing them improves testability and sets a clear precedent for future screens. Player impact: none visible. System impact: rules can be tuned in one place, selectors can be unit-tested. Source: 01-clean-architecture-audit.md

title: Build-time catalog integrity validation at startup
description: contentCatalog.ts loads and parses all JSON definitions but does not validate cross-references. A quest referencing a deleted NPC, an event referencing a missing quest, or a faction standing delta referencing an unknown faction all pass validation silently. Fix: after all catalogs are parsed, run a validateCatalogIntegrity() function that checks: every event outcome NPC ID resolves, every event outcome quest ID resolves, every quest successorQuestId resolves, every quest enemyNpcId resolves, every quest rewardItemId resolves. In development: throw with a descriptive error. In production: log structured error. This runs once at startup. Acceptance: loading events.json with the known broken quest reference produces a clear error message before the game starts.
close_reason: validateCatalogIntegrity() added to contentCatalog.ts — checks all cross-references at startup

title: Fiction: Standardise House Name, Fix Broken IDs, and Establish Authoring Standards
description: The game's fiction has several consistency failures: the player's own house is named 'House Valdric' in narrative.md (10 references) but 'House Valdris' in data files and code (13 references) — two spellings in one play session; an event references a non-existent district ID; ward birth text is a one-sentence placeholder in one of the morally weightiest moments the game can produce; event choice labels use generic 'Accept X' verbs instead of world-specific action language. Player impact: immersion breaks in first session. System impact: authoring entropy grows if standards are not documented.
notes:
Live gap identified on 2026-05-17: the day-1 Factions-screen contradiction around 'Restored ward seats' is now tracked concretely in destiny-qk8v. This epic remains the fiction umbrella; qk8v is the player-visible bugfix contract.
2026-05-29 Narrative/Game Design review: fiction standardization should eventually cover custody/social-position vocabulary that becomes mechanically meaningful under destiny-f1pl (captive, bound, ward, guest, servant, protected person, detainee).

title: Wire exterior tier commitment to room repair
description: selectComputedExteriorTier derives what exteriorState should be from intact rooms. selectExteriorTierAdvanceable detects when it's possible. But no command updates state.house.exteriorState. Repairing all rooms leaves exteriorState at 'ruined'. Fix: create commitExteriorTier command that sets exteriorState to computedTier. Call it from the repairRoom reducer after each repair (the tier may not always advance, but the check is cheap). Acceptance: after repairing rooms that meet the next tier threshold, exteriorState updates automatically.
close_reason: commitExteriorTier command created, wired into repairRoom reducer, tests added.

title: Enforce roster capacity in formalizeHeir and promoteWardToRoster
description: selectRosterCapacity and selectCrewCapacity compute isFull flags but formalizeHeir and promoteWardToRoster add NPCs to the roster without checking capacity. A full roster silently overflows. Fix: both commands read state.roster.length against a capacity value (base 4 + rosterBonus). If full, return state unchanged and appendActivityLogEntry: 'The house cannot take in another member — there is no room.' Acceptance: roster.length never exceeds capacity; both commands have tests for full-roster case.
close_reason: Both formalizeHeir and promoteWardToRoster now check roster capacity (renownSlots + rosterBonus) before adding an NPC. When full, they log a 'cannot join — there is no room' message and return state unchanged. Tests added for the full-roster guard in both commands.

title: Surface house heirs on HouseScreen
description: state.house.houseHeirs stores succession heirs that carry political legitimacy weight and can be formalized to the roster. HouseScreen.tsx shows state.wards but has no section for houseHeirs. No selector exposes houseHeirs to any UI component. A player with an heir placed by an authored event has no way to discover them except through that specific event's text. Fix: create selectHouseHeirs selector. Add a succession section to HouseScreen showing heir name, stage, legitimacy status, and available actions (recognize, formalize). Acceptance: a player with a houseHeir can see them on the HouseScreen.
close_reason: Added selectHouseHeirs selector to house.ts. HouseScreen now renders a Succession section with each heir's name, stage, legitimacy status, and origin story. Shows 'No succession heirs are established.' when the list is empty.

title: Housing: Surface Heirs, Enforce Capacity, Wire Exterior Tier
