Currently a dead action — players search every room out of compulsion but gain nothing. Add random finds (5-20 Marks, item from stash pool, or activityLog lore entry) per room, scaled to room state.

title: House rooms: repair gives no mechanical benefit
description: Repairing rooms (Bureau 15Mk, Kitchen 20Mk, Study 35Mk, Master Chamber 45Mk, Garret 130Mk) only changes room state to 'intact' — no ongoing bonus, NPC slot, or gameplay effect. Players spend marks with zero return. Rooms should grant meaningful effects: Kitchen→wage reduction or morale, Study→training bonus, Bureau→governance action unlock, Master Chamber→renown passive, Garret→scout/lookout function.

title: Show NPC rarity tier and skill cap in hire UI
description: NPC rarity (Common/Uncommon/Rare) affects skill caps (70/80/90) but is never displayed in recruitment offer cards. Players cannot make informed hiring decisions. Add rarity badge and skill cap display to hire offer UI.
close_reason: Rarity badge and skill cap added to RecruitmentScreen hire offer cards

title: Responsive layout: add mobile breakpoints below 860px
description: No responsive breakpoints exist below 860px — the game is unusable on mobile and tablet. Add CSS breakpoints at 860px, 640px, and 480px. Priority: dashboard and roster views first. Use CSS Grid/Flexbox reflow for zone-based layout introduced in dashboard split bead.

title: NPC background and motivation writing for thin NPCs
description: 19 of 31 NPCs have null or minimal motivation — they are stats in human form, not characters. Write background, motivation, and 2-3 personality traits for each. Hook motivations to visible behavior: NPCs with conflicting loyalties show stress, NPCs near goals show opportunity events. Make NPC-to-NPC relationships visible via roster UI.

title: Story quest framing: Mira rescue and Orren Wex
description: The opening promises (vault, Mira rescue, Orren Wex) are not systemized into mechanics — they exist as generic quest entries. Give these quests special framing: opening cutscene text, named NPC involvement, multi-step chain structure, aftermath scenes with faction/world consequences. Distinguish story quests from contracts visually and mechanically.

title: Quest chains with world-state consequences
description: Quests are currently binary transactions (accept/complete for gold) with no world-state consequences. Convert key quests to chains: choices affect faction standing, district control dials, NPC availability. Make story quests (Mira rescue, Orren Wex) distinct from generic contracts with named NPCs, aftermath scenes, and faction ripples.

title: Surface NPC state visibly (hunger, fatigue, stress)
description: NPC hunger, fatigue, and stress are tracked in state but completely invisible to the player and ignorable with no consequence. Surface these as visible bars/icons in the roster view. Add consequences for neglect: performance penalties, morale events, eventual departure. Makes roster management feel real.
close_reason: NPC states (hunger, fatigue, stress) surfaced in roster list with mini-bars and warning indicators; StateStatRow negative polarity fixed; committed

title: Wire combat outcomes to management layer
description: Combat is currently isolated from the management game — winning or losing a fight has no ripple effect. Wire outcomes to: injury recovery days (NPC unavailable), faction contact gains/losses, district reputation shifts, loot feeding equipment economy. Combat must matter to the city layer.
close_reason: KO'd NPCs get injury+30 and health=10; non-quest victory +5 renown; defeat reduces faction standing by 5; any combat raises cityDials.unrest by 3; committed

title: Add passive income layer and faction income grants
description: Economy is 100% quest-dependent — one bad week creates debt crisis with no fallback. Add a baseline passive income layer: safe house earns minimal daily income, faction allies provide grants at standing thresholds, district investments unlock income streams. Prevents total income collapse while maintaining pressure.
close_reason: House baseline +5 Marks/day and faction income grants (3 or 6 Marks based on standing) added to endDay Step 4d/4e; committed

title: Rebalance equipment prices to real progression tiers
description: Equipment caps at 500M gold — aspirational fantasy with no real progression path in normal play. Rebalance to a tiered system: tier 1 at 150-200M, tier 2 at 500M-1B, tier 3 at end-game. Prices should be reachable within a normal session arc.
close_reason: Weapon/armor shopPrices retiered: tier1 60-100M, tier2 150-200M, tier3 350-450M

title: Combat rebalance: weapon parity, enemy AI, action variety
description: Ranged weapons are strictly better than melee in typical fight durations. Hammers deal 1.5x damage with only 15% miss rate — dominant. Enemy AI always attacks lowest HP target — trivially predictable. Only 4 actions exist with mostly auto-resolved outcomes. Rebalance weapon ranges/accuracy curves, add 2-3 enemy AI behaviors (focus debuffed, protect healer, etc.), and expand actionable player decisions.

title: Fix activityLog pop vs shift bug
description: activityLog uses .pop() where it should use .shift(), causing newest log entries to be discarded instead of oldest. Fix to maintain a FIFO capped queue. A correctness bug with immediate player-visible impact.

title: Memoize Redux selectors and refactor gameSlice
description: 17 unmemoized selectors cause unnecessary rerenders on every state change. Wrap all derived selectors with RTK createSelector. gameSlice.ts is 886 lines with mixed RTK immer patterns and duplicated logic — split into domain slices and eliminate duplication. Also fix unsafe type casts (as any, as unknown as unknown) and add null checks for contentCatalog.npcsById.get() (used 28+ places without null checks).

title: Spieler-Charakter im Kampf fehlt
description: Kein Spieler-Kombattant im CombatantState. Spieler schaut Kämpfen zu statt zu kämpfen. Stärke/Schläue/Autorität-Stats ungenutzt. Betrifft: combat.ts startCombatEncounter, CombatScreen.tsx.

title: NPC-Events feuern für nicht-angeheuerte NPCs
description: 7 NPC-Flavor-Events (Verek, Rutha, Sable, Aldric, Dara) haben keine Roster-Mitgliedschaftsbedingung. Feuern für Geister. Rutha Kael ist nirgends im Hire-Pool. Betrifft: events.json, evaluateEvents.ts.

title: Kampfsystem: Keine HP-Leisten, keine Zugübersicht
description: Combat-Critic: Kämpfe haben keine sichtbaren HP-Balken. Kein Kampfprotokoll pro Zug. Treffer/Schaden nicht kommuniziert. Betrifft: CombatScreen.tsx, App.css Kampf-Styles.

title: Combat: Questabschluss nicht verdrahtet
description: concludeCombatEncounter setzt kein objectiveMet. Spieler-Kombattant fehlt im CombatantState. Betrifft: combat.ts, combatAftermath.ts, quests.ts.

title: Dialogue ist zustandslos — Marion startet immer bei Node 1
description: Kein Gesprächsverlauf im Game State. Gesprächshistorie im State nötig, Condition-Felder auf Choices (minLoyalty, requiresEventFired). Betrifft: dialogues.json, gameSlice.ts, DialogueScreen.tsx.

title: Schulden-Default hat keine Konsequenzen
description: debtCrisisTriggered:true ändert nur ein Dashboard-Badge. Benötigt: Tag 31 Fraktionsdruck-Event, Tag 35 Abgänge von Low-Loyalty NPCs. Betrifft: endDay.ts, events.json.

title: Stash Acquire kostet keine Marks
description: addToStash Reducer zieht keine Marks ab. Preis-Tier: T1 50-100M, T2 150-300M, T3 400-800M. Verkaufen für 40-60%. Betrifft: gameSlice.ts addToStash, ShopsScreen Acquire-Button, equipmentCatalog.ts.

title: NPC agency — working NPCs cause world events
description: NPCs sent to 'work' should be world agents. Each endDay for a working NPC: small chance to (1) discover a rumor → added to next day log, (2) cause a minor incident → raises district tension by 1-3, (3) make a contact → unlocks a new contract, (4) attract faction attention → faction standing shift. Probability weighted by NPC traits (curious, reckless, charming etc.). NPCs shape the world, not just earn marks.
close_reason: Working NPCs have 8% daily chance to cause incident/rumor/contact; world shaped by roster actions

title: Living world — faction daily agendas + district tension
description: Factions act without player input. Each endDay: (1) Collectors increment warning counter → at threshold spawn 'collector visit' event in The Pale, (2) faction influence shifts per district based on recent contracts/incidents, (3) districtTension field per district (0-100) rises with failed missions/incidents, falls with successful contracts, (4) tension level shown on district cards (calm/uneasy/dangerous). World moves without player.
close_reason: District tension 0-100 per district; faction rotates daily log entry; tension shown on district cards

title: Session-start RPG audit — game designer review each wave
description: Process improvement: at the start of each session, run a game-designer specialist agent that reads the GDD and bd ready list, then answers: (1) does the planned work advance core RPG mechanics? (2) what RPG gaps are most critical right now? (3) are any planned beads misaligned with the RPG north star? Document this in docs/workflows/session-rpg-audit.md.
close_reason: Session RPG audit workflow created; AGENTS.md updated with audit step

title: World NPCs — questgivers, merchants, faction reps
description: All current NPCs are roster members. Missing: (1) npcType field in NPC schema (roster|world|story|enemy), (2) 2-3 world NPCs per district (innkeeper, guild contact, black market dealer), (3) world NPCs shown in DistrictMapScreen as interactable, (4) simple interaction: get quest lead, buy rumor, trigger event.
close_reason: 10 world NPCs added across 5 districts; displayed in DistrictMapScreen People section

title: Main quest — Mira arc: missing sister as story NPC
description: Mira (player's sister) is mentioned in lore but has no NPC record, no quest stages, no location. Implement: (1) Mira as story NPC in npcs.json with type:story, (2) mainQuest state (stage: searching|lead-found|location-known|rescued), (3) quest stage advances via specific contracts/events, (4) DashboardScreen shows main quest status. This gives the player a reason to play.
close_reason: Mira added as story NPC; main quest state with 4 stages; Dashboard widget

title: Player character — name, stats, traits, character sheet
description: The player has no identity. Implement: (1) character creation screen (name input, choose 2 starting traits from list), (2) playerCharacter in game state (name, stats: strength/cunning/authority, traits[], level:1), (3) PlayerSheet component showing stats. Player is male. This is the single most critical missing RPG mechanic.
close_reason: Player character with name/stats/traits implemented; character creation in opening screen

title: NPC motivation field and 'why are they here' visibility
description:
NPCs have background (one-line history) but no motivation field. The player cannot reason about why an NPC stays, might leave, or has personal goals.

NARRATIVE DESIGNER: 'The difference between a character and a stat block is knowing what they want.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Add optional 'motivation' field to NPC definition schema (z.string().optional())
2. Add motivation text to all starting NPCs in npcs.json:
   - Marion Vale: 'To rebuild something worth serving. She does not say this aloud.'
   - Ida Rhys: 'Coin, at least for now. Something else, maybe. She has not decided.'
   - Add for Verek, Sable, Aldric etc.
3. Display in NpcDetailPanel Character section below background text
4. Motivation text styled distinctly — smaller, italic, muted color

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- motivation field in schema and JSON for all 5 starting NPCs minimum
- Displayed in detail panel in visually distinct style from background
- Marion's motivation reflects her as emotional anchor, not just employee
close_reason: motivation field added to schema and npcs.json for all 12 starting NPCs. Displayed below background in Character section with em dash prefix.

title: NPC traits drive visible behavior — loyalty, ambition, empathy reactions
description:
10 personality trait scores (loyalty, ambition, empathy, ruthlessness, prudence, curiosity, dominance, loyalty, vanity, zeal) exist per NPC but are mechanically inert and drive no visible behavior.

GAME DESIGNER: 'Traits must bite. loyalty:52 should mean she stays under pressure. ambition:71 should create friction when not given a title. These need consequences.'
NARRATIVE DESIGNER: 'NPCs should have authored voice — their traits should shape how events involving them resolve.'

PHASE 1 (this bead — visible reactions):
1. In NPC departure evaluation (evaluateNpcDeparture): factor loyalty score — loyalty > 70 makes departure much less likely even under stress; loyalty < 30 makes it more likely
2. In endDay wage stress: high-ambition NPCs (ambition > 65) without a title assignment gain -2 morale/day extra (ambition frustration)
3. In recruitment: high-empathy NPCs (empathy > 65) refuse to be deployed against their faction affinity faction
4. Activity log entries when trait-driven behavior fires: 'Marion Vale stays despite the hardship. She has always been that way.'

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- loyalty score materially affects departure probability
- ambition > 65 with no title: morale drain visible in log
- Trait-driven events produce authored log entries
close_reason: loyalty trait modifies departure probability (>70 = -30%, <30 = +40%). ambition>65 without title drains 2 morale/day with authored log entry.

title: Show NPC background text in detail panel
description:
Every NPC has a 'background' field in their definition (e.g. Marion Vale: 'A former dock broker who learned to keep crews loyal and manifests flexible'). This text is never shown to the player anywhere. NpcDetailPanel shows only stats.

ALL SPECIALISTS AGREE:
- Narrative: 'background text is character gold sitting in JSON — completely invisible'
- UX: 'Player has no discovery path for character. Add a Character section to NpcDetailPanel.'
- Art Director: 'Portrait + name + one-line motivation is the minimum for emotional attachment.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
- NpcDetailPanel: add a 'Background' section between identity and stats
- Display: npc.background as italic prose text, styled in --font-body
- If background is empty: show nothing (no empty state label)
- Also show dominant traits in prose: find top 2 trait scores and render as e.g. 'Highly ambitious. Deeply prudent.' (threshold > 65 = highly, 50-65 = notably, < 35 = unusually low)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- background text visible in NpcDetailPanel
- Dominant traits rendered as prose not raw numbers
- Marion Vale shows her background on game start
close_reason: NPC background text visible in Character section. Dominant traits rendered as prose sentences (Highly/Unusually + adjective).

title: Progression signal: visible feedback between management decisions and outcomes
description:
Player assigns NPCs to training, work, recovery — but never sees a visible connection to outcomes. Does training improve stats? Does recovery restore HP? Game Designer (new P1): 'The management layer feels like ceremony not agency.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. After endDay, generate log entries for meaningful stat changes: 'Aldric Vane gained +1 Combat from training.'
2. Show expected daily income on working NPCs before assigning (consequence preview)
3. On Operations dashboard: show trend arrows on city dials (up/down from yesterday)
4. Training progress indicator on NPC detail panel: X/N days until next stat tick

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Player can see at least one visible outcome of each assignment type after End Day
- Training shows progress toward next improvement
- Economy shows per-NPC contribution not just total Marks
close_reason: endDay generates log entries for training skill ticks and recovery. NpcDetailPanel shows training day progress. RosterScreen shows income estimate for working NPCs.

title: Faction introduction: player should know factions before events reference them
description:
Events like event-gilded-court-approach fire before the player knows what the Gilded Court is. Player chooses between labels not positions. Narrative Designer: 'Meaningless decisions.'

IMPLEMENTATION: On first visit to each district OR on day 1 of Operations, surface a one-sentence faction primer per faction — not a lore dump, a political position. Example for Gilded Court: 'The Gilded Court controls credit, titles, and access. They do not help for free.' Could be: a faction glossary tab on the Factions screen, OR brief labels on each faction standing entry, OR a day-1 event that introduces the political landscape in Marion's voice.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Player can identify each faction's position and motivation before encountering faction events
- Factions screen shows agenda/motivation alongside standing
- No faction event fires before its subject faction has been introduced
close_reason: primer field added to schema and factions.json for all 5 factions. FactionsScreen shows primer + agenda alongside standing.

title: Player motivation and opening hook: surface Mira as active goal
description:
Opening gives the player grief and circumstances but no forward direction. Mira is mentioned as 'taken' but framed as backstory not as an active goal. Narrative Designer: 'The player has grief and circumstances but no direction. No dangling thread pulls the player forward.'

IMPLEMENTATION: In the opening lore step, after introducing Marion and Ida, add one line that frames Mira as a goal: something unresolved, someone to find, a reason beyond survival. Do not over-explain — one restrained sentence. Example: 'Mira is still out there. That is the only thing in the ledger that matters.'

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Player finishes opening screen knowing: who they are, who stayed, what they lost, what they want
- Mira referenced as active unresolved thread not closed backstory
close_reason: Mira surfaced as active unresolved thread — taken not confirmed dead. Verified.

title: Work Board to Deployment flow: visual signposting and active contract context
description: Three screens (Work Board, Deployment, Engagement) describe a funnel but are equal nav items. Player does not know the sequence. Deployment screen shows no active contract context on arrival. UX: sidebar step indicator 1-2 for Work Board and Deployment, banner showing active contract on Deployment screen, Deploy to Encounter as dominant CTA.
close_reason: Step numbers in nav for Work Board and Deployment. Active contract banner on Squad Deployment screen. Deploy CTA styled as dominant action.

title: Engagement: remove from nav, add post-combat exit CTA
description: Engagement is a primary nav item. Clicking it with no squad starts a seeded encounter. After combat resolves the player is stranded with no exit CTA. UX: Remove from nav, only reachable via Deploy button. Add Victory/Defeat panel with Return to Operations and View Roster CTAs after outcome resolves.
close_reason: Engagement removed from screenCatalog nav. Post-combat Victory/Defeat panel added with Return to Operations and View Roster CTAs.

title: Debt-claim mechanic — give the central narrative threat mechanical teeth
description:
The opening establishes the debt-claim against House Valdris as the central dramatic premise. Dashboard shows it. But the debt never accrues, never triggers, never threatens anything. Core tension is purely decorative.

GAME DESIGNER (new finding P1): 'This is the hook the opening hangs everything on. A management game must have a visible tension — a problem worsening, a goal approaching. Currently there are no stakes.'
NARRATIVE DESIGNER: 'The player has grief and circumstances but no direction. No dangling thread pulls the player forward.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Add debtAmount (starting ~500 Marks) and debtDueDay (day 30) to game state
2. Dashboard widget: 'Debt Claim Active — 480 Marks owed — Due: Day 30'
3. endDay: if day >= debtDueDay and marks < debtAmount → trigger debt-crisis event (game over or major setback)
4. Player can pay off debt early via a UI action (removes the threat, unlocks House Valdris standing)
5. Partial payments reduce the total

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Debt is visible on Operations dashboard from day 1
- Countdown creates genuine urgency
- Failing to pay has a meaningful consequence
- Paying off debt is a satisfying early milestone
close_reason: debtAmount/debtDueDay/debtPaid/debtCrisisTriggered in game state. Dashboard widget shows countdown and pay action. endDay triggers crisis if debt unpaid on due day.

title: Working assignment: show job type, income, and district in roster
description:
NPCs assigned to 'working' show only the label 'Working' in roster. No job type, daily income, district, or skill requirement visible anywhere.

GAME DESIGNER: 'If an NPC is Working, the player needs to know: doing what, earning how much, where. A black hole assignment erodes trust in the economy system.'
UX SPECIALIST: 'Feedback void for a core resource loop. The player assigned someone and has zero confirmation of outcome.'

REQUIRES a minimal job catalog first (see job-catalog bead). Once that exists:
1. RosterScreen row: show job name under NPC name when assignment=working (e.g. 'Textile Work')
2. NpcDetailPanel assignment section: show job name, daily income, district, skill requirement
3. Replace 'Earns Marks, cannot deploy or train' with specific values

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Roster row for working NPC shows job category name
- Detail panel shows: job name, estimated daily Marks, district context
- Player can see at a glance what each working NPC contributes
close_reason: jobCatalog.ts maps dominant skill to job name + district hint. Roster rows show job name + income. NpcDetailPanel shows job detail block when assignment=working.

title: UI shape language — square corners, amber borders, stamped buttons
description:
All panels use 8-12px border-radius and rgba(white,...) borders. Action buttons are rounded pills with blue CTA color. This reads as a Tailwind dashboard, not dark fantasy.

ART DIRECTOR (new finding, HIGH): 'Border radius is the single most insidious wrong-signal in the current UI. Rounded corners communicate softness and approachability. Dark fantasy communicates danger, weight, permanence. Near-square corners (2-4px) achieve this.'

CSS CHANGES:
1. --radius-sm: 2px, --radius-md: 3px, --radius-lg: 4px (replace current 4/8/12px values)
2. Replace all rgba(255,255,255,0.X) borders with: border: 1px solid rgba(215,191,130,0.25) (amber-gold)
3. .action-button: border-radius: 2px, border: 1px solid var(--gold), background: transparent, color: var(--gold), font-variant: small-caps, letter-spacing: 0.06em
4. .action-button:hover: background: rgba(215,191,130,0.1)
5. Screen panels: border-radius: 3px

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- No border-radius > 4px on any panel, card, or button
- All borders use amber-gold color token
- Action buttons look stamped/ledger style
- Build and visual regression check
close_reason: Square corners, amber-gold borders, stamped buttons

title: Rename MissionPrepScreen — fix label collision with Work Board
description:
The MissionPrepScreen h1 reads 'Contracts' — the same label as the Work Board (ContractBoardScreen). Two primary screens share an identical title. Player cannot build a mental model.

UX SPECIALIST: 'Label collision. The player cannot distinguish which Contracts screen does what.'
GAME DESIGNER: 'Symptomatic of an unresolved conceptual question — this screen is trying to be quest selection AND squad builder AND deploy button simultaneously.'

FIX:
- MissionPrepScreen h1: change to 'Squad Deployment'
- Eyebrow text: 'Prepare your unit — assign, review, deploy'
- screenCatalog: confirm nav label is 'Deployment' (already done, just screen header inconsistent)
- Consider splitting into two concerns if scope allows: mission selection vs squad builder

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- No two screens share the same h1 title
- 'Deployment' is consistent from nav label to screen header
- Player entering from Work Board CTA sees relevant active contract context
close_reason: MissionPrepScreen h1 renamed to Squad Deployment, test updated. Verified.

title: Event deduplication — per-event cooldown, not daily reset
description:
firedEventIds is cleared every new day in endDay.ts. Events with persistent conditions (unrest > 60, faction standing < -20) repeat every day until the condition is resolved. Also: 2 district-entry events have probability:1.0 — fire on every single visit.

GAME DESIGNER: 'A plague warning that fires every morning is not drama, it is spam. Events need permanent IDs tracked for life.'
UX SPECIALIST: 'Repetition collapses narrative tension into noise. Players will click through events robotically within 3-4 days.'

FIX:
- Add lastFiredDay: Record<eventId, number> to game state (replaces firedEventIds[])
- In evaluateEvents: skip event if lastFiredDay[id] exists AND event.repeatable !== true
- Add repeatable: boolean to event schema (default false)
- Set repeatable:true on market/patrol ambient events, false on story events
- Fix event-hollows-entry and event-pale-approach: set probability to 0.25, add repeatable:false

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Story events fire at most once per playthrough
- Repeatable events respect a minimum day gap (configurable per event)
- District-entry events no longer fire on every visit
close_reason: lastFiredDay replaces firedEventIds. Per-event permanent dedup, repeatable events respect cooldownDays. Verifier passed.

title: Event trigger discipline — fire only at end-of-day, not mid-action
description:
evaluateEvents() is called in 3 places: endDay, travelToDistrict, and acceptQuest. Events interrupt the player mid-travel and mid-action, breaking the management game's daily rhythm.

GAME DESIGNER: 'Events interrupting district travel break the daily loop. The management RPG rhythm (Review→Assign→Travel→Shop→Resolve→EndDay) is what separates it from an adventure game. If interrupted mid-travel, the loop is shattered.'
UX SPECIALIST: 'Modal interrupt pattern without a safe point. After dismissing, player left with no context of what state changed.'

FIX:
- Remove evaluateEvents() call from travelToDistrict
- Remove evaluateEvents() call from acceptQuest reducer
- Move district-conditional events to endDay, passing currentDistrict as context
- Add post-resolution toast: brief message summarising what state changed (standing +5, log entry written)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- No event modal fires during travel or quest acceptance
- District-conditional events still fire (at end of day after visiting that district)
- Post-event feedback tells player what changed
close_reason: evaluateEvents removed from travelToDistrict and acceptQuest. Fires at endDay only. Verifier passed + null-coalescing fix applied.

title: Fix opening narrative: wrong count and wrong gender
description:
Opening lore screen says 'One man remained loyal' but Marion Vale is female. Game also starts with 2 NPCs (Marion + Ida Rhys).

DESIGN INTENT (confirmed by user): Player is male. Marion Vale is the one loyal remaining retainer AND a potential romantic interest. She is the emotional anchor of the opening. Ida Rhys arrived separately — mercenary, pragmatic, different relationship.

FIX (OpeningScreen.tsx, lore step):
Replace: 'One man remained loyal. The ledger is yours.'
With something that: (1) names Marion specifically, (2) frames her as the singular loyal figure who CHOSE to stay, (3) introduces Ida as a later pragmatic addition, (4) preserves the romantic tension potential through restraint — not overt.

Example:
'Marion Vale remained. She had no reason to — the wages stopped, the house name meant nothing. She stayed anyway. Three days later, Ida Rhys knocked on the door. She said she heard you were hiring. The ledger is yours.'

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Marion framed as singular loyalty, not one of two equals
- Ida introduced as a distinct personality (mercenary pragmatism)
- Gender correct throughout
- Romantic subtext preserved through restraint (she stayed when she did not have to)
close_reason: Opening narrative corrected: Marion named as loyal anchor, Ida as pragmatic arrival, gender fixed. Verified by code-review agent.

title: P1: 2 factions missing factionStates runtime entry
description: faction-tallow-ring and faction-restored have no entry in factionStates array in initial-game-state.json. endDay pressure escalation loop iterates factionStates and skips these two factions entirely — activePressure never rises or decays for them regardless of standing.
close_reason: 2 missing factionStates added — tallow-ring and restored.

title: P1: 3 districts missing runtime state in initial-game-state.json
description: district-the-pale, district-the-warrens, district-the-hollows have no entry in the districts array of initial-game-state.json. selectShopOverview builds districtStateById from state.game.districts — missing entries cause isBlocked to always return false for those districts, bypassing faction-standing shop gates.
close_reason: 3 missing districts added to initial-game-state.json with runtime state.

title: UI: MoR-inspired prominent End Day button always visible
description: In MoR the day-advance button is always visible and primary. Currently End Day is in DashboardScreen only. Add End Day to GlobalStatusBar as a persistent button. It should be the most visually prominent interactive element in the shell. Use action-button--primary class. Keep it in Dashboard too but make the status bar version the primary affordance.
close_reason: End Day button in GlobalStatusBar, always visible

title: UI: MoR-inspired color-coded NPC status rows in Roster
description: MoR uses row background tinting and status color to signal NPC state at a glance. In RosterScreen, apply subtle row background colors: recovering=muted red tint, deployed=muted blue tint, working=muted green tint, assigned_title=muted gold tint. Also: group roster by assignment status with section headers (Deployed | Working | Idle | Recovering) instead of flat unsorted list.
close_reason: Implemented in Wave 10 mor-roster-agent: grouped roster sections, colored left borders, mini stat bars. 314 tests passing.

title: UI: MoR-inspired stat bars for NPC health/morale/loyalty
description: Masters of Raana shows NPC stats as colored progress bars (green/yellow/red) not raw numbers. In RosterScreen NPC rows and NpcDetailPanel, replace raw 'HP 85' / 'Mor 72' numbers with small inline bar indicators. Health bar: green >60, yellow 30-60, red <30. Morale bar: same thresholds. Loyalty bar in detail panel. This gives immediate visual readability without clicking into each NPC.
close_reason: StatBar thresholds updated to 30/60 MoR pattern in NpcDetailPanel.

title: UX: No pending events indicator in GlobalStatusBar
description: When pendingEvents.length > 0 the GlobalStatusBar shows no indicator. Player can miss events. Add a pulsing badge '● Events pending' or similar to the status bar. Use selectPendingEvents selector. Style as an amber/gold attention signal.
close_reason: Pending events pulsing badge in GlobalStatusBar

title: UX: No navigation from active contract to deployment
description: ContractBoardScreen active contracts section: non-investigation contracts show 'Resolves through deployment' with no button. Player has no clear path to MissionPrepScreen. Add a 'Prepare Deployment →' button that navigates to /missions. This completes the contract→deployment loop.

title: Bug: Closed contracts show raw quest IDs not titles
description: ContractBoardScreen.tsx line 135: completedQuestIds are shown with string manipulation (replace quest-, replace hyphens) instead of looking up the quest template title from contentCatalog. Fix: import contentCatalog.questsById and use template.title ?? fallback.
close_reason: contentCatalog lookup for closed quest titles

title: Bug: MissionPrepScreen shows rewardCredits not Marks
description: MissionPrepScreen.tsx line 66: mission.rewardCredits is a domain field name (legacy). Display should read '{mission.rewardCredits} Marks' but the label already says 'Marks' so it reads fine. However 'Credits' is wrong Valdenmoor vocabulary in any log messages. Also verify combat.ts line 628 uses the right field and it matches the schema.

title: Bug: NPC title badge renders twice in RosterScreen
description: RosterScreen.tsx lines 53-63: NPCs with assigned_title get two badges showing the same title — one from the assignment-badge block (line 53-57) and one from the activeTitle positive-badge block (lines 59-63). Remove the duplicate positive badge for title-holders.

title: Bug: factionBadge always shows Contested in DistrictMapScreen
description: DistrictMapScreen.tsx line 28-32: the factionBadge() function always falls through to 'return Contested' even for districts with no contested factions. Fix: if factionId exists return its short name; if contestedIds is non-empty return 'Contested'; else return 'Open' or 'None'.

title: Combat aftermath: injury persistence, deployed state, and recovering lifecycle
description:
Combat deals health damage to combatant objects during the fight, but this damage never transfers back to the roster NPC state. After combat ends, all NPCs are at full health again. The deployed/recovering assignment lifecycle is also entirely missing.

INJURY PERSISTENCE:
- concludeCombatEncounter should write final ally combatant health values back to roster NPC state
- NPCs who reach 0 health in combat should enter assignment: 'recovering' (not 'idle')
- Medic title should reduce recovery time: recovering NPCs gain +10 health/day (currently only applies to event-auto-npc-injury-complication)

DEPLOYED LIFECYCLE:
- addNpcToSelectedSquad should set assignment: 'deployed'  
- concludeCombatEncounter should set returning NPCs back to assignment: 'idle' (or 'recovering' if injured)
- Deployed NPCs should be visible as such in RosterScreen (badge or assignment label)

EMOTIONAL AFTERMATH:
- Combat victory: all allied NPCs gain +2 morale
- Combat defeat: all allied NPCs gain +5 stress
- High-casualty combat (any NPC below 30% health): +8 stress per affected NPC
- These use existing npcStateModifiers functions — just wire them into concludeCombatEncounter
close_reason: Combat aftermath complete: injury persistence, deployed/recovering lifecycle, morale/stress aftermath, recovery tick.

title: Economy balance: sustainable wages, item pricing, passive income paths
description:
Current economy forces bankruptcy by day 12-15 with no strategic counter.

WAGE PROBLEM: 12 roster NPCs cost ~48 Marks/day in wages. Steward title adds +15/day. Net deficit: -33 Marks/day. Starting capital: 300 Marks = 9 days runway at zero income.

ITEM PRICING: Items in data/definitions/items.json have no price/value field. purchaseItemFromShop checks state.money >= item.value but item.value is undefined — purchases likely always succeed (undefined >= number = false, blocking all purchases) or always fail. Needs investigation and fix.

MISSING PASSIVE INCOME: GDD implies production, trade, and district presence should generate income. Currently: only missions (one-time) and title-steward (+15/day) generate money.

FIXES NEEDED:
1. Add price: number to all items in items.json and update itemDefinitionSchema
2. Add price to weapons.json and armor.json (shopPrice field)
3. Rebalance wages: current mercenary=12/day is too high relative to mission rewards. Or increase mission rewards.
4. Add passive income: title-steward should generate Marks equal to 20% of daily wages (reduces cost, not adds income). Add a 'working' assignment that generates 5-15 Marks/day per NPC based on their primary skill.
5. Introduce 'small jobs' — daily auto-income from NPCs assigned to 'working' without needing combat mission.
close_reason: Implemented in Wave 7 economy-agent: wages reduced ~40% (mercenary 8, citizen 5), working assignment 3-15 Marks/day, shopPrice on all items/weapons/armor, starting Marks 500. Economy verified sustainable.

title: Recruitment offer generation: district travel spawns hire opportunities
description:
availableForHire is seeded with 4 NPCs at game start but never replenished. Once those 4 are hired or expire, the player can never recruit anyone. The game is designed around roster management as a core loop — this is a critical loop break.

Needed:
1. When player travels to a district, check if any NPC definitions have a discoverCondition matching that district. If so, add them to availableForHire with appropriate wage/signing bonus.
2. After completing a quest or mission, some NPCs should become available (e.g., grateful locals, impressed mercenaries).
3. Enemy NPCs with recruitableOnDefeat: true should be offered after being defeated in combat.
4. Offer pool should refresh every 3-5 days for common NPCs (vary by faction standing with their home faction).

Implementation notes:
- NPC definitions in npcs.json have factionAffiliation — NPCs affiliated with a faction should appear in districts that faction controls
- districtDefinitions have controllingFactionId — use this to match NPC faction to district
- Offers should respect requiredFactionStanding threshold — player needs standing to attract certain NPCs
- Current enemy-npcs.json has recruitableOnDefeat field — wire this to concludeCombatEncounter
close_reason: Recruitment generation: district travel populates offers, combat defeat offers, periodic refresh.

title: Test coverage: purchase, squad formation, event outcomes, endDay integration
description:
Four systems have zero test coverage, plus two flaky tests due to unmocked Math.random.

MISSING TEST FILES:
- purchase.ts: no tests for purchaseItemFromShop — money deduction, inventory update, insufficient funds, non-existent item
- squad.ts: no tests for addNpcToSelectedSquad/removeNpcFromSelectedSquad — squad limits, duplicates, max size (6)
- applyEventOutcome.ts: no tests for any of the 11 outcome types (adjustFactionStanding, adjustCityDial, adjustCityResource, addCredits, setCorridorStatus, etc.)
- No integration test verifying endDay → evaluateEvents fires pending events

FLAKY TESTS:
- endDay.test.ts: trainer title selects random NPC/skill via Math.random — not mocked, results vary
- endDay.test.ts: council vote template selected via Math.random — not mocked

FIXTURE ISSUE:
- relationships.test.ts makeMinimalState() missing cityDials field — Zod strict schema will reject

EDGE CASES NOT TESTED:
- endDay with empty roster (likely safe but unverified)
- assignTitle when NPC already has a title (silent replace — should it warn?)

title: Missing CSS: wire styles for durability repair, title panel, resource bars
description:
Four UI areas render without styling because CSS classes referenced in TSX don't exist in App.css.

1. ShopsScreen repair section: .repair-npc-row, .repair-item-row, .repair-section, .action-button-sm — the equipment repair UI added by durability-agent has no layout, colors, or sizing.

2. NpcDetailPanel title assignment and durability: .title-panel, .durability-panel, .title-option, .title-active, .title-list, .title-ineligible-reason — completely unstyled; title selector is unusable.

3. ResourceStatusPanel: .resource-bar, .resource-corridor, .resource-status-panel — city resource bars have no visual representation.

4. DashboardScreen: .action-button--primary — End Day button renders as plain text.

Fix: Add the missing CSS rules to App.css or create co-located component CSS files. Match the existing dark theme: dark backgrounds (#1a1a2e, #16213e), muted accent colors, no pure white text.
close_reason: All missing CSS classes added. Durability, title, repair, resource panels styled. Focus ring added. Inline styles extracted.

title: City resource system: food security, water rights, and material supply as game mechanics
description: Valdenmoor cannot feed itself. 50% of calories come via the Green Corridor. 30% from fortified fields (Bound labor, regular monster raids). 20% from inside the walls. Water: one clean river, Compact-controlled. Wood: League-run logging camps under constant monster pressure. These are not background facts — they are game mechanics. Resource levels affect: prices in shops (scarcity drives cost), NPC states (hunger rises faster in low-food periods), faction tensions (League threatens field operations, Tallow Ring smuggles through the Corridor), and city dials (low food security raises Unrest directly). The player can influence resources: expeditions bring outside materials, faction standing affects permit costs, hiring a Quartermaster title reduces supply costs. A Corridor blockade event (monster surge or faction conflict) should be the most dangerous city crisis in the game.
design: Add to game state: cityResources: { foodSecurity: 0-100, waterAccess: 0-100, materialStock: 0-100, corridorStatus: 'open'|'disrupted'|'blocked' }. Each resource affects city dial calculations on end-of-day tick. foodSecurity below 40: Unrest +5/day, NPC hunger decay doubles. waterAccess below 30: stress rises for all NPCs in lower districts. corridorStatus='blocked': foodSecurity -10/day, all shop prices +30%, Restore gains +5 influence/day. Resource events: 'field raid' (foodSecurity -8), 'logging camp attacked' (materialStock -10), 'Corridor toll raised by Ring' (shop prices +15%). Player actions that help: expedition returns with food caches, completing a field defense mission, hiring Quartermaster title.
acceptance_criteria: cityResources exists in game state. Low foodSecurity visibly increases NPC hunger in activity log. A Corridor disruption event fires and raises shop prices. Dashboard shows resource status indicators. At least one player action (expedition or mission) improves a resource level.
close_reason: cityResources in state/schema, end-of-day resource consequences, ResourceStatusPanel on Dashboard, shop price modifier selector.

title: Origin setup: protagonist name, starting state, and playstyle framing (no path selection screen)
description: The four paths (Business/Revenge/Power/Adventure) describe emergent playstyle, not a player selection at game start. Remove the origin selection screen concept. Instead: the game opens with the protagonist already in the ruined house with Marion waiting. The player enters or confirms a name. Starting state is fixed: ruined house, manufactured debt, one loyal retainer, three remaining bound workers who chose to stay. The four paths emerge from which actions the player chooses to prioritize — no UI forces a choice. The opening text grounds the player in House Valdric's situation.
design: New screen: OriginScreen — shown before Dashboard on first load only. Four path cards: icon, name, one-sentence framing, starting bonus listed. Name input field. Confirm button. On confirm: dispatch setOriginPath(path) which adjusts initial-game-state based on path (different starting credits, starting inventory item, starting faction standing modifier). Store protagonist name in game state. After selection, show a brief opening text (2-3 sentences) grounding the player in the ruined house with Marion waiting. Then navigate to Dashboard.
acceptance_criteria: First-time load shows OriginScreen. Player can enter a name and pick a path. Each path shows distinct starting bonuses. After confirm, dashboard shows the protagonist name in the header. The opening text references House Valdric, the debt claim, and Marion.
close_reason: Opening screen implemented with Valdenmoor voice, name input, protagonist name in Dashboard header and GlobalStatusBar.

title: Event system engine: reactive world events keyed off state, faction, and district
description: The GDD calls for 20-30 event templates. Currently there is no event system — the world never reacts to anything. Events are the mechanism that makes the city feel alive: a city with events feels reactive; one without feels frozen. The event system needs: (1) An event data format (JSON template with: trigger conditions, choices, outcomes, faction/district context). (2) An event evaluation engine that runs at end-of-day and checks which events fire. (3) An event presentation UI — a modal or panel that interrupts the player with a situation requiring a choice or just noting what happened. (4) Outcome application — events can change NPC states, faction standings, city dials, inventory, or trigger follow-up events. This is the connective tissue between all other systems.
design: Event template schema: { id, title, description, triggerConditions: { minUnrest?, factionId?, districtId?, npcStateThreshold? }, choices: [{ label, outcomes: [{ type, target, delta }] }], isAutoResolved: bool }. Seed 5 event templates to start: (1) unpaid wages unrest, (2) faction patrol in district, (3) recruit offer arrives, (4) market price shift, (5) NPC injury complication. Event engine: end-of-day tick evaluates all event templates, fires matching ones, queues for player presentation. Event queue in game state. EventModal component shows pending events one at a time.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/events.json exists with 5+ event templates. End-of-day tick evaluates and fires at least one event. Player sees an EventModal with a choice. Outcome is applied and logged. Event queue clears after player resolves each event.
close_reason: 8 event templates, evaluation engine, resolveEvent action, EventModal UI, selectPendingEvents selector. Wired into endDay.

title: Faction standing and political dials: track reputation and city-wide pressure
description: Faction standing and political dials are MVP must-haves per the GDD. Currently factions are lore only — the player has no standing with them that changes, and the citywide dials (control, prosperity, unrest, corruption) do not exist in game state. These are the levers that make the city feel alive and consequential. Faction standing affects: district access, shop inventory, recruit availability, mission availability, event triggers, NPC faction loyalty conflicts. Political dials affect: encounter rates, legal restrictions, item access, bribe costs, recruitment pool quality. Without these the city is static.
design: Add to game state: factionStandings: Record<factionId, number (-100 to 100)>. Add: cityDials: { control: number, prosperity: number, unrest: number, corruption: number } all 0-100. Initial values seeded from initial-game-state.json. Commands: adjustFactionStanding(factionId, delta), adjustCityDial(dial, delta). Selectors: selectFactionStandings, selectCityDials. Factions screen shows current standings with change indicators. City dials visible on dashboard. End-of-day tick applies faction drift based on player actions that day.
acceptance_criteria: Faction standings exist in Redux state and change via commands. City dials exist and are visible on the dashboard. The factions screen shows current standing for each faction. At least one game action (completing a mission) changes a standing and logs it.
close_reason: factionStandings + cityDials in state, FactionsScreen with tier labels, adjustFactionStanding/adjustCityDial actions, Dashboard dials row.

title: Title assignment system: assign, validate eligibility, and see active title effects
description: The NPC detail panel has a disabled 'Assign title' button. The biq bead covers opportunity cost but not the core assignment flow. Title assignment is an MVP must-have. What is needed: a title selection UI on the NPC detail panel that shows eligible titles (based on skill thresholds and status) and ineligible ones (with reason shown). Assigning a title sets the NPC's activeTitle in game state. Only one title per NPC. Deployed NPCs cannot hold a title actively. Title effects are visible on the dashboard or roster summary. This is the core of the management game — it is what makes the roster feel like an organization rather than a squad.
design: Extend NPC state: add activeTitleId (nullable). Seed data: add data/definitions/titles.json with 5 titles (medic, quartermaster, trainer, steward, chiefEngineer) each with: id, name, description, requiredSkill, requiredSkillThreshold, dailyEffect description. Title selector UI: dropdown or modal on NPC detail panel showing eligible/ineligible with tooltips. Command: assignTitle(npcId, titleId) — validates eligibility, sets activeTitleId. Command: revokeTitle(npcId) — clears it. Active title badge visible on roster list rows.
acceptance_criteria: Player can open a title selector on any NPC. Eligible titles are shown green, ineligible shown with reason. Assigning sets the badge on the roster. Revoking clears it. data/definitions/titles.json exists with 5 entries.
close_reason: Title assignment system implemented: 5 titles, eligibility validation, NpcDetailPanel UI, roster badges.

title: District travel screen: navigate the city and unlock district access
description: District travel is an MVP must-have. Currently districts exist only as data — the player cannot navigate between them. The city needs a district map screen where the player chooses where to go each timeslot. Travel has cost: time (timeslot consumed), sometimes coin (bribe at a checkpoint), sometimes danger (roll on entering a high-danger district). Locked districts require faction standing or a contact to unlock. The district map is the primary navigation surface of the game — everything the player does (shopping, recruiting, taking contracts, investigating events) is anchored to a district they have traveled to. Without this, the city is a concept rather than a place.
design: New screen: DistrictMapScreen. Shows all districts with: name, controlling faction badge, danger level indicator, lock state (accessible/locked/unknown). Clicking an unlocked district sets currentDistrict in game state and navigates to district actions (shop, recruit, investigate). Locked districts show unlock condition. Travel consumes a timeslot (or half-day). Danger districts: on arrival, run a lightweight danger check — high danger triggers an encounter event or cost. District access persists until end of day.
acceptance_criteria: Player can see all districts on a map screen. Clicking navigates to district actions. Locked districts show unlock conditions. Traveling to a high-danger district triggers a log entry. currentDistrict is reflected in the global status bar.
close_reason: District travel screen implemented. All 6 districts navigable with faction badges, danger indicators, and travel logging.

title: Recruitment system: discover, negotiate, onboard, and lose NPCs
description: No recruitment system exists. NPCs are currently seeded at startup and never change. The GDD calls for 12-20 recruitable or persistent NPCs and a world where the player manages a growing roster. Recruitment is not a hire-from-menu action — it is a social and economic loop: (1) DISCOVER — NPCs appear through district visits, event triggers, mission outcomes, faction contacts, or enemy defeat. Each NPC has a discoverCondition that must be met before they are visible at all. (2) ASSESS — the player sees the NPC's visible identity, background, faction, and wage demand. Hidden stats are revealed only after hire or through intel. (3) NEGOTIATE — the player offers a wage, a status tier, possibly a signing bonus. Some NPCs require a minimum faction standing, a specific existing roster member as a reference, or a title slot. (4) ONBOARD — the NPC joins with reduced starting loyalty (they don't trust you yet) and a probationary state. Early actions toward them raise or lower that loyalty. (5) DEVELOP — loyalty, relationships, and skill growth over time through assignment, titles, and events. (6) RETAIN or LOSE — if wages go unpaid, loyalty collapses, or a rival makes a better offer, the NPC leaves. Some warn first. Some just go. Some take secrets with them.
design: New game state: availableForHire[] — NPCs visible in the world but not yet hired. Each entry: npcId, discoveredInDistrict, wagePerDay, signingBonus, requiredFactionStanding, requiredRosterContactId (optional), currentEmployer (null or rival org id). New command: recruitNpc(npcId) — validates requirements, deducts signing bonus, moves NPC from availableForHire to roster with loyalty penalty (-20 from their base loyalty trait). New command: dismissNpc(npcId) — removes from roster, may trigger faction or relationship consequence. End-of-day tick checks: if wages unpaid for 2+ days, loyalty drops 15/day. At loyalty < 20, NPC triggers a departure event.
acceptance_criteria: Player can see available NPCs in at least one district. Clicking recruit deducts signing bonus and adds NPC to roster with reduced loyalty. Dismissing an NPC removes them and logs a consequence. Unpaid wages cause loyalty decay visible in the activity log.
close_reason: Recruitment system implemented: availableForHire state, recruitNpc/dismissNpc actions, RecruitmentScreen, loyalty decay wired.

title: Enemy NPC rosters: named antagonists with full character depth for each faction and rival org
description: Enemies are not abstract archetypes — they are people with names, histories, motivations, and social positions, just like the player's roster. A Civic Compact enforcer squad has a lieutenant with a name and a reason for being there. A criminal crew has a fixer who used to work the docks before things went wrong. A rival organization has operatives the player will encounter repeatedly across multiple missions — and who remember the player. Named enemy NPCs make the world feel populated by actors, not generated filler. MVP enemy roster should include: (1) Named operatives for each of the 3 established factions (at least 2 per faction — a leader type and a specialist). (2) Named operatives for the criminal underground faction. (3) Named operatives for at least 1 rival player organization — these are recurring antagonists, not one-shot encounters. (4) At least 2 named independent enemies — a corrupt official, a mercenary-turned-bandit, a desperate ideologue. Each named enemy NPC needs the same depth as a player NPC: background, faction, stats, traits, and a friction point that makes them narratively interesting if the player defeats or recruits them.
design: Deliverable: data/definitions/enemy-npcs.json with 15-20 named enemy NPC entries using the same schema as npcs.json plus: isRecurring (bool), organizationId (which rival org or faction they belong to), encounterRole (leader/specialist/muscle/schemer), recruitableOnDefeat (bool — some enemies can be flipped to the player's roster). Also define 2 rival organizations in data/definitions/rival-orgs.json with name, faction alignment, district presence, current priority, and a roster of 3-4 named operatives each. Add a section to docs/narrative.md: Antagonist Roster — who the player is up against and what makes each memorable.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/enemy-npcs.json contains 15+ named entries. data/definitions/rival-orgs.json contains 2 rival organizations each with 3-4 named operatives. At least 3 enemy NPCs are flagged as recruitable. Recurring enemy NPCs have encounter history hooks in their description. All entries use full NPC schema fields.
notes: Recruitable enemies are a high-value feature: defeating a named antagonist and offering them a place in your org is a classic power fantasy moment that also rewards tactical performance. Design at least 3 enemy NPCs as recruitable — with a condition (e.g. defeated with morale intact, not killed) and a loyalty penalty on recruit (they don't trust you yet).
close_reason: 17 named enemy NPCs, 2 rival orgs (Gilded Hand, Ashen Compact), antagonist roster in narrative.md. 3 recruitable enemies.

title: NPC roster expansion: 10 additional characters with full lore and stats
description: The MVP calls for 12-20 NPCs. We have 2. Marion Vale and Ida Rhys are good templates — one per faction, grounded backgrounds, readable friction points. We need 10 more to make squad management feel like actual choices. Each NPC needs a distinct social class, faction tie, skill profile, and personal friction that makes them memorable in 2-3 sentences. The roster should cover: the full range of social statuses (citizen, mercenary, servant, retainer, noble, criminal), different faction loyalties including the new factions, different archetypes (a fighter, a healer, a schemer, a soldier, a tradesperson, a criminal contact, a noble in reduced circumstances), and at least one NPC who is morally complicated in a way that makes the player feel something.
design: Deliverable: 10 new NPC entries in data/definitions/npcs.json. Each entry must include: identity (name, origin, background, status, factionAffinityId, rarity), baseAttributes (all 7), startingSkills (all 12), startingTraits (all 10), allowedTitleIds. Background text should reveal role, competence, and friction in 1-2 sentences. Avoid generic fantasy archetypes. Ground each NPC in the city's specific social texture — a former Compact clerk who knows where bodies are buried, a League worker with a disability that changed their career, a Court hanger-on who burned their connections.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/npcs.json contains 12 NPC entries total. Each covers all required fields. The roster spans at least 4 different factions, 3 different statuses, and includes both combat-primary and non-combat-primary characters.
close_reason: 12 NPCs total — 10 new characters covering all factions, 3 status tiers, combat and non-combat primaries. All grounded in Valdenmoor lore.

title: Faction lore: criminal underground, underclass, zealots, and foreign interest
description: We have 3 establishment factions. The GDD calls for 5-8. More critically, all 3 current factions compete at the top — there is no opposition from below. A living city needs: (1) A criminal underground — the people the Civic Compact is trying to suppress. Smugglers, information brokers, protection rackets, black-market suppliers. They are not evil, they are the underclass economy. (2) A dispossessed or underclass faction — the people the Foundry League exploits or the Compact ignores. Refugees, displaced workers, the informal city. (3) A religious, ideological, or moralist faction — someone who claims moral legitimacy, not just material power. They pressure marriage norms, lineage choices, and public conduct. Creates friction with relationship and household systems. (4) A foreign interest — a trading company, an embassy, a mercenary charter from outside the city that has its own agenda and uses the player as a local asset.
design: Deliverable: faction bible entries in docs/narrative.md for 2-3 new factions (pick the highest-leverage ones first). Also add contested territory rules: which districts are disputed between which factions, and what the grey zones feel like. Each new faction needs: core identity, operating logic, social base, method of power, public image, hidden flaw, conflict posture, writing note.
acceptance_criteria: docs/narrative.md contains at least 2 new faction bibles. data/definitions/factions.json contains their seed entries. At least one grey-zone district or contested street is documented. Faction count reaches at least 5.
close_reason: Tallow Ring and The Restored fully documented in world bible and factions.json. Grey zones will be handled as part of district expansion (destiny-9up).

title: World lore: city name, history, wound, and external context
description: The city has no name, no history, and no precipitating crisis. Every faction, district, event, and NPC background floats without grounding. We need: (1) A city name — should evoke dark medieval density and political age. (2) A founding myth or origin — how did this city become what it is? (3) The city's wound — the specific historical break that created the current tension. A failed conquest? A collapsed ruling house? A plague that wiped out the old order? A catastrophic industrial accident? (4) The current crisis — what is everyone trying to hold together or tear apart right now, at the moment the player arrives? (5) External world context — is there a kingdom? An empire? A war somewhere that floods the city with refugees, disrupts trade, or gives factions outside leverage to exploit? This is the bedrock everything else is built on.
design: Deliverable: an expansion to docs/narrative.md with a World Bible section. Sections needed: City Name and Identity, The Wound (historical break), The Current Moment (what is happening when the player arrives), The World Outside (what presses in from beyond the city walls), and a one-paragraph tone-setter that writers can use as a north star. The world should not be generic fantasy — it should have a specific political texture that makes its factions feel inevitable rather than arbitrary.
acceptance_criteria: docs/narrative.md contains: city name, 2-3 paragraph history, the named wound event, a current-state paragraph, and a note on the external world. All faction and district entries in the doc are grounded in that history.
close_reason: World bible written: city of Valdenmoor, The Breach, House Valdric, five factions (Civic Compact, Gilded Court, Foundry League, The Tallow Ring, The Restored), four protagonist paths, outside world, naming conventions, voice north star.

title: End-of-day resolution loop: wages, state decay, passive income, triggered events
description: There is no end-of-day loop. The game has no time pressure, no income, no upkeep, and no passive state changes. This means credits, morale, stress, hunger, fatigue, and loyalty are all decorative. The daily loop is the core engine that connects roster → economy → world state → player decision. Without it the game has no heartbeat. Each day end should: deduct wages per NPC by status/title, apply state decay (hunger rises, fatigue rises, stress decays with rest), apply passive title outputs (medic heals, quartermaster reduces costs, trainer adds skill xp), apply passive income from assigned jobs, trigger any pending events keyed off state thresholds, update faction standings from player actions taken that day, produce a day-end summary the player sees before advancing.
design: Implement as a Redux thunk dispatched when player clicks End Day. Process order: (1) state decay tick, (2) title effects, (3) job outputs, (4) wage deductions, (5) event evaluation, (6) faction drift, (7) log summary. Each step should produce activity log entries. Wage default: 5cr/day for free NPCs, 0 for prisoners/family. Title output examples: medic title → heal 5hp to most injured NPC. trainer title → +1 skill xp to one idle NPC per day.
acceptance_criteria: End Day button exists. Clicking it deducts wages, applies at least hunger/fatigue decay, applies medic heal if assigned, adds summary to activity log, and advances day counter. Credits can hit 0 and trigger a warning event.
close_reason: End Day loop implemented: wage deduction, hunger/fatigue decay, title effects (medic/steward/trainer), day counter, activity log separator.

title: Narrative identity: who is the player and what is their organization
description: The GDD describes the player as 'the leader of a small organization inside a living city' but leaves the framing open. Right now there is no answer to: Who am I? Why does this organization exist? What do I want? What is my name or title? This gap means every mechanic floats without emotional grounding. Defining narrative identity is prerequisite to writing any event text, faction dialogue, or quest framing with consistent voice.
design: Options to decide: (1) Fixed framing — e.g. player is always a displaced mercenary captain rebuilding a broken house. (2) Soft framing — player picks from 2-3 origin archetypes at start (mercenary house, trading household, criminal syndicate). (3) Pure emergent — framing is implied by faction alignment and early choices, never stated. Recommendation: soft framing with 2-3 origins. Each origin sets: starting money, starting roster composition, starting faction standing, starting district access, and the opening narrative hook.
acceptance_criteria: Player sees an origin selection or receives a narrative hook on first load. All UI chrome and event text uses a consistent organizational identity. The dashboard greeting references who the player is.
close_reason: Opening screen with name input and House Valdric framing. protagonist name in game state and dashboard. Marks replaces credits in status bar.

title: Define portrait style guide
description: Why: Portrait generation cannot begin at volume without a style guide — inconsistent portrait quality breaks roster coherence and weakens the relationship system. Per the MoR analysis, portrait inconsistency is one of the most damaging visual anti-patterns. What: produce a portrait style guide document covering framing (bust or 3/4), fixed aspect ratio, background treatment options, lighting language, rendering/stylization level, and rules for character-consistent sensual presentation. Include at least one worked example prompt per NPC class (combat, administrative, elite, working class, intimate). Output belongs in docs/portrait-style-guide.md. Owner: Art Direction role.
close_reason: Portrait style guide created at docs/portrait-style-guide.md covering framing, aspect ratio, lighting, rendering consistency, sensual presentation tiers (3), per-class prompt templates, anti-patterns, and file conventions.

title: UI: add save and load controls
description: Add explicit save and load controls to the application shell or dashboard so the current game session can be persisted and restored through the application layer. The UI should show clear success feedback.
acceptance_criteria:
- save control exists
- load control exists
- visible feedback is shown
- UI delegates persistence to application/infrastructure
notes: Added explicit save and load controls to the dashboard using the application session-persistence services and the browser save adapter. The UI now lets the player persist and restore the current session with visible feedback, while tests cover the full save-modify-load loop. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems/Infrastructure: wire save and load use cases
description: Add application-level save and load orchestration around the existing snapshot adapter. Loading should replace the runtime game state through validated data, and saving should persist the current state without leaking storage concerns into the UI.
acceptance_criteria:
- save and load orchestration exists
- load path replaces store state
- tests cover save and restore behavior
notes: Added application-level save and load orchestration in src/application/services/sessionPersistence.ts. Saving now persists the current runtime game state through the existing save-store port, and loading restores validated state back into the store via replaceGameState. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 5: Save and session flow
description:
Wire the existing save snapshot boundary into the application and UI so the player can persist and restore the current game state. This milestone focuses on save/load orchestration, explicit UI controls, and user-visible confirmation that the current management loops are durable.

Sections:
- application save/load orchestration
- UI controls for save and load
- visible session feedback and validation

title: UI: implement shop screen and purchase interaction
description: Replace the shop placeholder with a real shop screen backed by application selectors and purchase actions. The screen should let the player browse district-specific offers, inspect items, and buy them through the application flow.
acceptance_criteria:
- shops route renders seeded shops and offers
- player can buy an item through the application layer
- money and inventory feedback is visible
notes: Implemented the shops route using application selectors and purchase actions. The UI now renders seeded district-specific shops and offers, dispatches purchases through the application layer, and shows live money and owned-quantity feedback. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems/Data: define shop contracts and purchase flow
description: Define shop contracts, seed shop data, and implement the first purchase flow through the application layer. Buying should validate item availability, enforce sufficient funds, deduct money, and add inventory entries without leaking rules into the UI.
acceptance_criteria:
- shop contracts exist
- seeded shops exist
- purchase flow updates money and inventory
- tests cover success and failure cases
notes: Added shop contracts, seeded shop definitions, and the first purchase flow through the application layer. Buying now validates offers via content definitions, enforces sufficient funds, deducts money, and adds inventory entries without placing the rules in the UI. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 4: Economy and shop loop
description:
Build the first real economy loop around district shops and purchases. This milestone focuses on shop definitions, purchase rules, inventory and money updates, and a UI flow that lets the player buy items through the application boundary.

Sections:
- shop contracts and seed data
- purchase application flow
- shop UI and feedback
- validation of economy state changes

title: UI: make mission prep interactive
description: Update the mission prep screen so the user can add and remove squad members through the application command flow. The screen should reflect current squad composition and preserve the six-member limit.
acceptance_criteria:
- mission prep supports add/remove actions
- six-member limit is visible or enforced
- UI delegates rules to the application layer
notes: Updated the mission prep route to dispatch the application-layer squad add/remove actions and reflect current squad composition through selectors. The screen now supports interactive squad changes while preserving the rule ownership in the application layer. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems: implement squad assignment commands and reducer wiring
description: Add the first state-changing application flow for squad management. Implement typed actions or use-case-style reducer wiring for adding and removing NPCs from the selected squad, enforce a maximum squad size of six, and cover the behavior with tests.
acceptance_criteria:
- squad mutation flow exists in application layer
- max squad size is enforced
- tests cover add, remove, and overflow behavior
- UI can dispatch without owning the rules
notes: Implemented the first state-changing application flow for squad management. Added pure squad command functions, reducer wiring through the game slice, and tests covering add, remove, and six-member overflow behavior. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 2: Interactive squad management
description:
Introduce the first state-changing loop through the application boundary so the UI can move beyond read-only shells. This milestone focuses on squad assignment, mission-prep interaction, and persistence-safe mutations that preserve the clean architecture constraints.

Sections:
- application commands for squad changes
- interactive mission-prep UI
- validation of state transitions
- follow-on save and mission workflow hooks

title: UI: implement roster screen from application selectors
description: Replace the roster placeholder with a real roster screen backed by application selectors. Show roster entries, assignment, status, and a compact trait or state summary with a selected NPC detail panel.
acceptance_criteria:
- roster route renders seeded NPCs
- selected NPC detail is visible
- UI reads through application selectors only
notes: Implemented the roster route using application selectors only. Added a content-catalog lookup in the application layer so UI components can render seeded NPC names, statuses, and backgrounds without importing JSON files directly, and added a selected-detail panel plus UI tests. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Systems/UI: add application store and seed-backed selectors
description: Create the first application-level game state integration using the seeded runtime data. The work should introduce a store boundary, typed selectors or queries, and a minimal mechanism for the UI to consume dashboard, roster, district, and faction views without embedding business rules in components.
acceptance_criteria:
- application store exists
- seed-backed selectors or queries exist
- UI can consume state without importing raw JSON directly
- tests cover the seed-backed state integration
notes: Added the first application store boundary in src/application with a parsed seed-backed game state, typed selectors for dashboard, roster, districts, and factions, and provider wiring so the UI consumes application state rather than importing raw JSON. Added tests for the store integration and dashboard rendering. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Milestone 1: Playable systemic slice
description:
Build the first playable vertical slice that proves the game identity through roster management, district visibility, faction context, one mission-prep flow, and stateful UI driven by the existing domain contracts and seed data.

Sections:
- application state and seed loading
- roster and NPC inspection
- district and faction overview
- mission-prep shell
- first round of UI integration verification

title: Verifier: define review checklist for architecture and TDD compliance
description: Create a concise verifier checklist for reviewing architecture boundaries, dependency direction, test coverage quality, and TDD compliance. This should be usable across implementation tasks.
acceptance_criteria:
- checklist exists in docs
- review priorities are explicit
- checklist is short enough for repeated use
notes: Added docs/review-checklist.md as a concise reusable verifier checklist covering correctness, architecture boundaries, TDD/tests, contracts/coupling, validation gates, and scope discipline.

title: Systems: define initial core domain contracts
description: Define the first core game contracts for NPCs, items, equipment, factions, and runtime game state. Contracts should support future schema validation and stay independent from UI and framework concerns.
acceptance_criteria:
- core contracts are explicit
- invariants and boundaries are documented or tested
- no UI or persistence leakage into domain contracts
notes: Defined the first core domain contracts in src/domain using Zod-backed schemas and exported types for NPCs, items, equipment, factions, political dials, the content catalog, and runtime game state. Added focused tests for weapon invariants, relationship-axis bounds, and runtime state validity. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Chore: scaffold project workspace and validation commands
description: Scaffold the initial project workspace and make the validation loop executable. Deliver the first working scripts for typecheck, tests, lint, and build so later agents have fast quality gates.
acceptance_criteria:
- project scaffold exists
- validation commands are documented and runnable
- baseline test setup works in CI-friendly mode
notes: Scaffolded the initial React + TypeScript + Vite workspace under the clean-architecture folder shape. Added pnpm-based scripts for lint, format, test, test:run, typecheck, and build; installed the initial stack dependencies; added Vitest + Testing Library setup; added a baseline app shell and smoke test; documented setup and validation in README.md and CLAUDE.md. Validation passed: pnpm lint, pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build.

title: Decision: choose initial web stack and quality gates
description:
Decide the initial implementation stack and required validation commands for the project. This should cover frontend framework, typing, schema validation, test runner, linting, formatting, and core scripts expected for agentic work.

Document the rationale and non-goals.
acceptance_criteria:
- selected stack is documented
- required validation commands are explicit
- decision supports clean architecture and TDD
notes: Documented the initial stack and quality-gate decision in docs/decisions/0001-initial-stack-and-quality-gates.md. Selected pnpm, Node 22 LTS, React, TypeScript, Vite, Redux Toolkit, Zod, Vitest, Testing Library, ESLint, and Prettier. Defined required validation commands and quality-gate policy, and linked the decision from docs/architecture.md.

title: Architect: define initial clean architecture
description:
Create docs/architecture.md with layer boundaries, dependency direction, module ownership, state-management constraints, persistence boundaries, and testing strategy.

The architecture must support clean architecture, extensibility, and TDD-first domain work.
acceptance_criteria:
- docs/architecture.md defines layers and dependency rules
- ownership boundaries are implementable by multiple agents
- testing strategy is compatible with TDD
notes: Expanded docs/architecture.md into the initial architecture source of truth. Defined clean architecture layers, dependency direction, module boundaries, state-management constraints, persistence boundaries, content architecture, testing strategy, ownership boundaries, and deferred technical decisions for the next Bead.

title: Architect: expand product source of truth
description:
Create and refine docs/product.md from the existing game design document. Define player promise, MVP scope, major systems, and milestone acceptance criteria.

Use the task contract template and keep the result implementation-facing.
acceptance_criteria:
- docs/product.md captures MVP scope and major system goals
- milestone acceptance criteria are explicit
- document resolves ambiguity left in GAME_DESIGN_DOCUMENT.md
notes: Expanded docs/product.md into the initial product source of truth. Defined player promise, design spine, MVP scope, major system requirements, milestone goals, milestone acceptance criteria, prioritization rules, and current open questions.

title: Milestone 0: Agentic foundation and technical baseline
description:
Establish the project source-of-truth documents, technical direction, and initial implementation baseline so agentic work can proceed with clear architecture, TDD expectations, and dependency-managed execution.

Sections:
- Product and architecture source of truth
- Technical stack decision
- Initial scaffold and quality gates
- First domain contracts

title: Economics: align existing hired merc wages with market contract rates
description:
## Why
The project already computes skill-based market wages for hire offers, but existing hired mercenary fixtures and some wage fallbacks still behave like flat status-rate labor. That leaves the economics model split between market contracts and runtime payroll.

## What
Align existing hired mercenary runtime entries with the same contract-rate logic used by recruitment offers, without rewriting every status-based wage in one pass.

## Why now
This is the safest next slice of destiny-k988 after wage-debt consequences. It reduces the largest live mismatch without forcing a full economy rebalance.

## Player impact
Hired mercenaries behave more consistently: the rate you hire them at is the rate they keep costing.

## System impact
Touches shared hire/wage calculation, hire fixtures, wage application, and end-day economy tests.

## Does not include
No full wage redesign for retainers, servants, nobles, or family.
