No ledger UI redesign.

## Evidence
Epic destiny-k988, generateDistrictHireOffers skill-based wages, applyWages status fallback, and current hired-merc fixtures.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible acceptance: hired mercenary wages remain consistent between offer generation and ongoing payroll.
2. System / technical acceptance: one shared contract-rate path exists for the targeted mercenary slice.
3. Tests / verification acceptance: recruitment, wages, and endDay economy tests stay green.

## Finding coverage
k988 F2

title: Economics: unpaid wages cause real roster consequences
description:
## Why
NPCs can currently remain unpaid far too long without believable intermediate fallout, which makes wage debt feel gamey instead of social and economic.

## What
Add a thin slice of the economics epic: unpaid wages create escalating roster consequences before final departure, including assignment refusal or morale collapse, and the house receives visible warnings.

## Why now
This is the clearest exploit inside the economics epic and the safest piece to land independently.

## Player impact
The player can no longer ignore wage debt as a harmless hidden counter. Unpaid staff will visibly sour, refuse work, or leave.

## System impact
Touches wage application, end-of-day roster consequences, activity log messaging, and relevant tests.

## Does not include
No hire-rate unification. No ledger income redesign. No full economy UI pass.

## Evidence
Epic destiny-k988, current applyWages behavior, and session retro on thin-slice economics fixes.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible acceptance: unpaid wages produce visible escalating warnings and at least one consequence before final departure.
2. System / technical acceptance: roster state changes in a deterministic way based on wage arrears and NPC context.
3. Tests / verification acceptance: targeted wage/endDay tests cover the escalation path and full project gates stay green.

## Finding coverage
k988 F1

title: Let NPC households expand and repurpose world sites over time
description:
## Why
The clarified direction explicitly requires non-player households and NPCs to expand, repurpose, and improve their own buildings when they have the means. Without that, only the player meaningfully changes space and the city stops feeling alive.

## What
Add NPC and household site expansion/renovation logic.

Cover:
- when NPC households can afford expansion or repair
- how rooms are added, repurposed, or hardened
- security, amenity, and custody implications
- how new site capacity changes social and quest pressure
- what changes remain visible after the site returns to abstraction

## Why now
This is required if buildings are meant to belong to all actors, not only the player.

## Player impact
The player sees rival houses, safehouses, shrines, and faction buildings evolve over time instead of remaining static set dressing.

## System impact
- extends worldHousehold data from authored snapshot toward living institutions
- ties economy and world simulation into physical site change

## Does not include
- decorative-only map art refreshes
- full construction minigames
- player base-building overhaul by itself

## Evidence
world-sites-captivity-narrative-review-2026-05-29.md; current world households already imply rooms, debt pressure, and hidden custody but remain structurally static.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. NPC-controlled sites can gain, lose, or repurpose room capacity based on world conditions.
2. These changes affect security, custody, or social outcomes.
3. Abstracted sites still remember the structural changes when reconcretized.

## Finding coverage
F1 F3 F4
close_reason: Superseded by pre-existing destiny-v1tx (same non-player household growth scope).

title: NPC room interaction and captive-interaction rules
description:
## Why
The user wants NPCs to interact with rooms and with captives in those rooms. Occupancy alone only says where someone is; it does not define what they do there.

## What
Define and implement interaction rules for NPCs in concrete locations: dwell, work, guard, visit, question, tend, smuggle supplies, comfort, abuse, conspire, and observe. These interactions must also feed abstract state when the location collapses back.

## Why now
Without this layer, concretized locations remain visually explicit but behaviorally shallow.

## Player impact
Rooms become places where relationships and consequences are produced, not just labels.

## System impact
Builds on occupancy substrate and captivity runtime. Must integrate with relationships, events, rumors, and content policy.

## Does not include
No cinematic scene system. No explicit exploitation mechanics.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Concrete locations support meaningful room-level NPC actions.
2. Captive interactions can change condition, compliance, trust, fear, and clue state within content-policy limits.
3. Interaction outcomes can survive collapse back to abstract runtime state.

## Finding coverage
F3 F4 F5
close_reason: Implemented concrete-site room interactions: guard pressure, protective care, and observed custody clues that persist through collapse-back.

title: Author room blueprints for all enterable houses and establishments
description:
## Why
The new direction requires that all enterable houses and similar interiors have rooms. A runtime substrate alone will not guarantee authored room coverage for the actual places the player can enter.

## What
Audit enterable POIs, residences, safe houses, taverns, detention sites, and other interior locations, then author room blueprints for each relevant place. Blueprint scope should include named rooms, room roles, basic capacity, and any special security or clue tags.

## Why now
Without this content pass, the architecture can land while the world still behaves like a partial menu shell.

## Player impact
Enterable places feel spatially real and support room-specific discovery, rescue, hiding, and interaction.

## System impact
Content and schema alignment across POIs and world households.

## Does not include
No full art pass. No tactical maps.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Every enterable house or establishment has an authored room blueprint.
2. Blueprint naming and role tags are consistent with the general location runtime.
3. Key story locations such as Mira's holding site can be represented without bespoke exceptions.

## Finding coverage
F1 F2
close_reason: Authored POI room blueprints, added site tags for safehouse/holding-site coverage, and modeled the old tannery as a real location with rooms.

title: Location concretization and collapse-back lifecycle
description:
## Why
The clarified direction explicitly requires that locations may later fall back from concrete to abstract state. Without an explicit lifecycle, concretized places will either bloat permanently or lose important state when collapsed.

## What
Define and implement the lifecycle rules for when a location becomes concrete, when it may collapse back to abstract state, what transient room data is discarded, what persistent consequences must remain, and how quests, captives, occupancy, clues, and repairs survive collapse.

## Why now
This is the core mechanism that makes the hybrid model operational rather than just aspirational.

## Player impact
The world can stay broad without feeling fake or memoryless.

## System impact
Touches persistence, selectors, and any future location runtime.

## Does not include
No new authored content.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Collapse-back preserves durable consequences and references.
2. Concretization is deterministic from abstract state plus authored definition.
3. Quest and captive references do not break when locations collapse.

## Finding coverage
F5
close_reason: Added persisted siteRuntimes plus deterministic materialization, concretize/collapse lifecycle commands, reducer hooks, known-room retention, and regression coverage ensuring collapse-back preserves captivity and room knowledge.

title: NPC household growth and room expansion for non-player actors
description:
## Why
The user wants NPCs and foreign houses to expand their own buildings when they have money, and to use those rooms socially and politically. Current world households are authored static records.

## What
Add household growth rules for non-player actors: NPC or world households can invest in repairs, added rooms, or upgraded room functions; resources and wealth can change location capability; new rooms can affect security, concealment, care capacity, and social influence.

## Why now
A general location substrate without non-player household growth would keep the world reactive only for the player.

## Player impact
Rival houses and institutions evolve materially over time, not just in text.

## System impact
Builds on worldHouseholds plus new location runtime. Must integrate with faction resources and world simulation.

## Does not include
No city-scale construction economy overhaul.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Non-player households can change room capacity or function over time.
2. Growth affects later occupancy, captivity, and infiltration conditions.
3. Changes persist whether the location is abstract or concrete.

## Finding coverage
F1 F4
close_reason: Non-player households now invest in growth, add or repurpose rooms, deepen capacity over time, and persist those changes across abstract/concrete site lifecycle.

title: Abstract custody simulation: offscreen care, coercion, visits, and psychological consequences
description:
## Why
The user explicitly rejected shallow offscreen simulation. Hybrid only works if abstract locations still preserve depth.

## What
Model offscreen custody and room ecology at a deep abstract level: care or neglect, food, water, sanitation, guard pressure, movement restriction, interrogation, visits by other NPCs, relationship and trauma shifts, dependency, fear, rumor, and survival-bond development within content-policy constraints, plus escape, rescue window, illness, death, transfer, and clue generation.

## Why now
Without this, abstract locations become narrative dead zones and the hybrid model loses the project's intended depth.

## Player impact
Even unseen places continue to matter and generate consequences.

## System impact
New abstract simulation rules layered on top of captivity and location runtime. Must integrate with events, rumors, relationships, and world NPC systems.

## Does not include
No exploitation framing. Must follow content policy and consequence-first tone.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Offscreen captivity can evolve meaningfully without concrete room-by-room ticking.
2. Simulation outputs can surface as events, rumors, quest leads, and state shifts.
3. The same NPC can later be concretized without losing the meaningful abstract history.

## Finding coverage
F3 F4 F5
close_reason: Abstract custody now simulates harsh and protective offscreen regimes, emits rumors and custody alerts, and preserves those consequences when sites later become concrete.

title: Bond management UI: BondStatusPanel in NpcDetailPanel for sale listing and rescue actions
description:
## Why this issue exists
The bond market backend (destiny-og7t) is complete: markNpcForSale reducer, checkBondAcquisitionOffers, transferBondedNpc, three rescue commands, and three selectors. But there is zero UI surface. The player cannot:
- See a bonded NPC's bond details
- Toggle 'for sale' or set a market value
- Free an NPC voluntarily (freeNpc reducer exists, no button)
- See which NPCs are currently NPC-held (assignment='transferred')
- Trigger any of the three rescue paths (legal/extraction/force)

The EventModal correctly handles bond-acquisition-offer events when they fire — that part works. What's missing is everything the player does before and after.

## What needs to be done
Add a BondStatusPanel to NpcDetailPanel (src/ui/screens/NpcDetailPanel.tsx). The panel is conditional — it renders only when detail.bondStatus is non-null. Two view modes:

**Player-held bond (ownerType='player', assignment != 'transferred'):**
- Bond metadata: entry reason, contract value, term days, days elapsed since bondStartDay
- Free NPC action: button dispatching gameActions.freeNpc — show cost (contractValue), disabled if insufficient funds
- List for sale section: forSale toggle + market value number input dispatching gameActions.markNpcForSale
- If forSale=true: show current marketValue, 'Unlist' button

**NPC-held bond (ownerType='npc', assignment='transferred'):**
- Show current holder name (look up bondBuyersById or fallback to holderId)
- Show health decay indicator (derived from buyer specialization)
- Three rescue buttons: Legal (cost: marketValue×1.5), Extraction (−20 health, −15 Tallow Ring standing), Force (−15 health)
- Disable legal rescue if insufficient funds

Also: NpcDetailPanel currently has no path to show 'transferred' NPCs — confirm RosterScreen selector includes them or add a separate 'Bonded Persons' section.

## Why now
The bond market exists in the engine but is invisible to the player. Without this panel, forSale can never be set organically, rescue is impossible, and the feature is dead on arrival.

## Player impact
Players can manage bonded NPC contracts directly from the NPC detail view. Bond sale listing, rescue, and voluntary release all become first-class actions.

## System impact
UI only. Touches NpcDetailPanel.tsx (new panel component), possibly RosterScreen.tsx (ensure transferred NPCs are listed). Reads from existing selectors (selectForSaleNpcs, selectBondedPersonsRegistry) and dispatches existing actions. No new commands or selectors required unless RosterScreen filtering needs extension.

## Non-goals
- Not building a dedicated 'Bond Market' screen (the panel-in-panel approach keeps scope small)
- Not adding faction standing indicators beyond Tallow Ring (that's cosmetic polish)
- Not building tooltips or animations

## Acceptance
- [ ] BondStatusPanel renders in NpcDetailPanel when bondStatus is non-null
- [ ] Player-held view shows entry reason, contract value, term, free button, and for-sale toggle+value
- [ ] Free NPC button dispatches freeNpc and is disabled when money < contractValue
- [ ] markNpcForSale dispatches with correct npcId, marketValue, forSale
- [ ] NPC-held view shows holder, decay hint, and three rescue buttons
- [ ] Legal rescue disabled when player cannot afford ransom
- [ ] Transferred NPCs are visible in RosterScreen (not filtered out)
- [ ] NpcDetailPanel.test.tsx covers player-held and NPC-held bond panel states
- [ ] Typecheck and test:run pass
notes: Architect review 2026-05-29: bond management UI must eventually surface location-bound captivity and holder/location context from destiny-d7lm, not only ownerType and assignment=transferred.

title: Wire bond entry reasons: add createBond outcome type and hook the five entry paths
description:
## Why this issue exists
bondEntryReasonSchema defines five values (compact-assessment, debt-settlement, voluntary, combat-capture, inherited) but not a single application command creates a bondStatus object. Every NPC enters the roster with bondStatus: null. The enum is aspirational schema with no execution path behind it.

## What needs to be done
1. Add a 'createBond' outcome type to eventOutcomeTypeSchema (domain/events/contracts.ts) alongside the existing transferBondedNpc type.
2. Extend eventOutcomeSchema with the fields needed to construct a BondStatus: contractValue, termDays, entryReason (typed to BondEntryReason).
3. Implement the case in applyEventOutcome.ts — find the sourceNpcId, construct bondStatus with the correct entryReason, patch the roster entry.
4. Wire the five entry paths via event outcomes or direct commands:
   - compact-assessment: fired by the Compact ward-bond event (Compact assesses your ward and places them under bond)
   - debt-settlement: fired when a debt event resolves with an NPC pledged as settlement
   - voluntary: fired when an NPC voluntarily agrees to bond service (quest or conversation choice)
   - combat-capture: triggered after combat where a captive is converted to a bonded prisoner
   - inherited: seeded in initial-game-state.json for NPCs whose bond pre-dates the game's start

## Why now
Without bond creation paths, the bond market feature (destiny-og7t) is mechanically complete but unreachable in real play — no NPC will ever have a bondStatus unless manually injected. This is a required pre-condition for any organic bond market activity.

## Player impact
Players will encounter bonded NPCs through natural game events rather than only test fixtures. Bond entry reason will be visible in UI and can gate future mechanics (e.g. combat-capture NPCs may have different morale/fear modifiers).

## System impact
Touches domain/events/contracts.ts (new outcome type), applyEventOutcome.ts (new case), and 5 event or command files for each entry path. Does not change existing bondStatus field names or BondStatus type — only adds construction paths.

## Non-goals
- Not changing bondStatus field layout
- Not adding UI for bond display (separate ticket destiny-og7t-ui)
- Not implementing game-balance tuning for which events fire each reason

## Acceptance
- [ ] createBond outcome type exists in schema, validated by Zod
- [ ] applyEventOutcome handles createBond and sets bondStatus with correct entryReason
- [ ] At minimum compact-assessment and debt-settlement paths are wired to real events/commands
- [ ] Tests cover applyEventOutcome createBond case for each wired path
- [ ] Typecheck and test:run pass

title: Player-initiated council votes via houseWardSeats or faction sponsorship
description:
## Why this exists
House Valdric starts politically stripped — houseWardSeats = 0. Restoring political standing is a core progression arc. Once the player regains a ward seat OR builds standing >= 50 with an allied faction, they should be able to put something on the council table. This is the payoff for the political systems the player has been navigating.

## What needs to be done
Two access paths to initiate a vote:

Path A — Direct (houseWardSeats >= 1):
- Player selects a vote template from a curated list of 'house proposals' (authored separately from faction templates)
- Proposal fires immediately into activeCouncilVotes with proposingFactionId = 'house-valdric'
- No sponsorship needed

Path B — Sponsored (faction standing >= 50 with any faction):
- Player can attach their proposal to an allied faction's name
- Faction standing >= 50 required
- Allied faction's leader approves or refuses based on their traits (ambitious leader more likely to co-sponsor if it aligns with their values)
- If approved, vote fires under that faction's name

New game state field: 'houseProposalCooldown' — player can only propose once every 10 days to prevent spam.

UI: Add 'Propose a Vote' button to FactionsScreen, gated by access conditions. Show available templates, sponsorship options.

## Why now
Depends on destiny-vy9e (agenda scoring working). houseWardSeats field already exists in state. This is the player-facing payoff for the political progression arc.

## Player impact
Political agency. The council is no longer something that happens to the player.

## System impact
New gameSlice action (proposeCouncilVote), new state field (houseProposalCooldown), new house-proposals content file, FactionsScreen UI addition.

## Non-goals
- House Valdric having faction members vote on their behalf (that requires allied NPC voting blocks)
- Vote campaigning mechanics

## Acceptance Criteria
- Player with houseWardSeats >= 1 can initiate a vote
- Player with faction standing >= 50 can sponsor a vote through that faction
- Cooldown prevents more than one proposal per 10 days
- Faction leader refusal is possible and logged
- Test: proposeCouncilVote adds to activeCouncilVotes with correct proposer

title: Rewrite applyPolitics vote selection to use faction agenda scoring
description:
## Why this exists
applyPolitics currently picks a vote template at random. The Tallow Ring can propose Compact patrol expansions. This breaks faction coherence — each faction has a defined agenda but the engine ignores it. The agendaAxes data (authored in the prerequisite task) exists to drive this.

## What needs to be done
Replace Math.random() template selection in applyPolitics with a scoring function:
1. For each faction, score their eligibility to propose: activePressure + trigger conditions from agendaAxes.proposesWhen
2. Pick the faction with highest score as proposer
3. From that faction's vote templates (filtered by agendaAxes.values tag match), pick the most contextually relevant one
4. If no faction meets threshold, no vote fires that cycle (silence is also meaningful)

Pressure relief: a faction that successfully passes a vote has its activePressure reduced.

## Why now
Depends on destiny-2lg6 (agendaAxes data). This is the core logic change that makes politics feel intentional.

## Player impact
Players can anticipate what the Compact will push when unrest is high. The Gilded Court proposing licensing fees after prosperity drops makes narrative sense.

## System impact
applyPolitics.ts rewrite of step 7. No schema changes — reads from agendaAxes already in factions.json.

## Non-goals
- Faction leader traits (separate layer)
- Player-initiated votes (separate task)

## Acceptance Criteria
- A state with Compact activePressure=80 and unrest=70 reliably produces a Compact order/patrol vote
- No faction proposes a vote that contradicts their agendaAxes.values
- Test: three different world states produce three different proposing factions

title: Fix: council vote effects are text-only — mechanical outcomes never applied
description:
## Why this exists
Every council-votes.json entry has an 'effect' field describing mechanical consequences (shop prices +5%, standing deltas, district access changes). None of these are applied. applyPolitics logs the text but dispatches no state changes. Players see 'Compact Patrol Expansion passed — Tallow Ring standing decreases by 10' but nothing actually changes.

## What needs to be done
Parse the effect field into structured data (or add a separate 'mechanicalEffects' array to each vote template). On resolution, apply the effects: faction standing deltas, city dial changes, temporary district access flags, market pressure changes. Each effect type needs a handler in applyPolitics or a new applyVoteEffects command.

## Why now
Without this, council votes are purely cosmetic. The fix is the prerequisite for players ever caring about vote outcomes.

## Player impact
Vote outcomes now actually change the world. Supporting or blocking a proposal has real consequences.

## System impact
council-votes.json schema change (add structured effects), new applyVoteEffects command, applyPolitics resolution step updated.

## Non-goals
- Temp-effect duration tracking (effects can start as permanent deltas)

## Acceptance Criteria
- At least 3 vote templates have structured effects applied on resolution
- Standing deltas in effect field are applied to the correct factions on pass/fail
- Existing tests extended to verify state changes after vote resolution

title: Author agendaAxes data for all 6 factions
description:
## Why this exists
factions.json has a prose 'agenda' field per faction. It's display text only. The vote selection logic in applyPolitics cannot read intent from prose. For agenda-driven vote proposals to work, each faction needs a machine-readable agendaAxes block that encodes what they value and when they act.

## What needs to be done
Add 'agendaAxes' to each faction in factions.json:
- 'values': array of policy tags matching vote template tags (e.g. ['order', 'suppression', 'trade'])
- 'proposesWhen': conditions object — cityUnrestAbove, factionPressureAbove, standingWithTargetBelow, prosperityBelow
- 'blocksWhen': conditions under which they would spend pressure to oppose a vote

Also add a 'tags' array to each council-votes.json template so the matching can work.

Faction values to author:
- Compact: order, suppression, trade, patrol
- Gilded Court: prestige, debt, elite-access, licensing
- Foundry League: labor, industrial, safety, leverage
- Tallow Ring: contraband, exemption, profit, opacity
- The Restored: abolition, welfare, community, anti-compact
- House Merrow: customs, tolls, succession, harbor

## Why now
All subsequent agenda-driven logic depends on this data existing. Pure authoring work — no code change.

## Player impact
Indirect — factions begin proposing votes that fit their stated purpose.

## System impact
factions.json and council-votes.json schema changes only.

## Non-goals
- Code to read agendaAxes (separate task)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 6 factions have agendaAxes with values and proposesWhen
- All 8 vote templates have tags array
- Domain schema (factions/contracts.ts) updated to include the new fields
- TypeScript compiles without error

title: [epic] Council Politics: Agenda-Driven Votes, Faction Leaders, and Player Proposals
description:
## Why this exists
The current council vote system picks templates at random. The Tallow Ring can propose a Compact patrol expansion. The Gilded Court can push welfare spending. This breaks the internal logic of a world where every faction has a stated agenda and motivation.

## What needs to be done
Replace random vote selection with agenda-driven proposal logic. Give each faction a machine-readable agenda (agendaAxes) that determines when and why they propose. Add named faction leaders as world NPCs whose traits skew proposal tendency. Allow the player to initiate votes once houseWardSeats >= 1 or via faction sponsorship at standing >= 50. Add agenda trees: multi-step vote chains where outcomes unlock or close subsequent proposals.

## Why now
The council vote system exists and fires every 5 days. Players already see it. Right now it teaches the wrong model — that factions are interchangeable random actors. Every day it runs it erodes world coherence.

## Player impact
Council politics becomes legible and predictable. Players can read faction pressure and anticipate what will be proposed. Eventually they can initiate their own agenda.

## System impact
applyPolitics, factions.json, gameSlice castVote, FactionsScreen. New world NPC entries. New schema fields on factions and gameState.

## Non-goals
- Full NPC dialogue for faction leaders in this epic
- Mechanical implementation of vote effect text (separate fix)

title: Add selectShopPricingBreakdown selector for price transparency
description: Why: ShopsScreen.tsx:121-139 formats market pressure labels inline (boundary violation). Player sees 15% discount but cannot understand how faction standing, corridor status, and market pressure combine. What: create selectShopPricingBreakdown(shopId, itemId) returning basePrice, marketMod, factionMod, corridorMod, finalPrice. Show breakdown as tooltip or collapsed section. Acceptance: no price formatting logic in ShopsScreen component; player can see what drives price changes.

title: Unify NPC status labels and money format across all screens
description: Why: NPC assignment state is displayed differently per screen (group label Available, badge idle, filter by readiness.blocked). Money shows as X Mk, X Marks, X marks depending on location. What: (1) create ASSIGNMENT_DISPLAY_LABELS map used everywhere; (2) use formatMarks/formatMarksAbbrev helpers across all screens; (3) audit action buttons - standardise Repair cost Mk, Pay debt amount Mk, Accept contract. Acceptance: NPC status label is identical in all contexts.

title: Add post-combat result screen
description: Why: combat ends and player returns to previous screen with no summary. No screen shows casualties, loot, faction changes, or quest progress. What: add CombatResultScreen or modal after combat resolution showing: outcome label, squad losses and injuries, items acquired, Marks gained, faction standing changes, quest progress triggered. Acceptance: every combat encounter shows a result summary before navigation.

title: Add per-operative investigation result breakdown
description: Why: investigation resolves to success/partial/failure with no per-operative detail. Player cannot tell which skill check passed or why - results feel random. What: create selectLastInvestigationResult() returning per-NPC roll details. Show breakdown on result screen: operative name, skill used, roll value, outcome. Acceptance: investigation result screen shows per-operative contributions.

title: Make purchase feedback persistent via activity log
description: Why: ShopsScreen sets lastPurchaseMessage in local React state which disappears on unmount. If player navigates away immediately, no record exists. What: dispatch appendActivityLogEntry at purchase time: Purchased [item] from [shop] for [amount] Marks. Acceptance: every purchase produces an activity log entry visible in Journal screen.

title: Create ConfirmationModal component for irreversible actions
description: Why: title revocation, item sale, room search, assignment changes dispatch immediately with no confirmation. Accidental clicks cause permanent state changes. What: create ConfirmationModal component showing action description, consequence, and confirm/cancel. Wrap dispatches for: title revocation (show what bonus is lost), item sale (show price), room search (show it cannot be undone). Acceptance: all destructive actions require explicit confirmation; modal shows the consequence.

title: Prune expired quest leads in endDay pipeline
description: Why: state.availableQuestLeads accumulates expired leads forever. Expiry enforced only in selectAvailableQuests at display time. Any code accessing state.game.availableQuestLeads directly sees stale data. State grows unboundedly. What: add pruneExpiredQuestLeads step in endDay. Acceptance: state.availableQuestLeads contains no expired leads after endDay runs.
close_reason: Added pruneExpiredQuestLeads(state) exported from endDay.ts, using isQuestLeadExpired from domain. Integrated into the endDay pipeline in Step 8 before evaluateEvents. State now contains no expired leads after endDay runs.

title: Create selectFullNpcProfile canonical merge selector
description: Why: a roster NPC's complete picture is split across 7 locations: state.roster, state.relationships, state.house.houseHeirs, state.wards, state.combat.combatants, contentCatalog.npcsById, contentCatalog.dialogueTreesById. To reconstruct one NPC a selector must merge all 7. What: create selectFullNpcProfile(npcId) as the single canonical merge point. All UI components use this. Acceptance: no UI component assembles NPC data from more than one selector call.

title: Rename bond terminology to eliminate three-meaning ambiguity
description: Why: 'bond' means three different things: (1) lore personality notes on NpcDefinition.bonds, (2) runtime relationship axes in state.relationships, (3) labor contracts in state.activeContracts. Code searching for 'bond' returns all three. What: rename NpcDefinition.bonds to loyalties or characterNotes. Labor bonds are already contracts. Reserve 'bond' or 'relationship' exclusively for the runtime axis system. Acceptance: 'bond' is unambiguous in all code search results.

title: UI/UX: Confirmation Modals, Feedback Persistence, and Missing Result Screens
description: Several categories of player-facing gaps: (1) irreversible actions (title revocation, item sale, room search) have no confirmation dialog; (2) purchase feedback is local React state that disappears on navigation; (3) investigation and combat have no detailed result screens showing per-operative outcomes or loot; (4) labels and formats (money, NPC status, button text) vary inconsistently across screens. Player impact: accidental irreversible actions, unclear outcomes, inconsistent language. Source: 08-cross-system-ui-ux.md

title: Centralise game rule constants in domain rules files
description: Why: prestige thresholds, defense tier scores, trait thresholds (65/35/50), exterior tier thresholds duplicated across selectors and UI components. A designer must hunt across 10+ files to tune one threshold. What: create src/domain/npc/traitThresholds.ts and src/domain/game/gameRules.ts exporting all threshold constants. All selectors and commands import from there. Acceptance: no magic threshold numbers in selector or UI files.
close_reason: Created domain/npc/traitThresholds.ts and domain/game/gameRules.ts. Updated applyNpcAgency, applyPersonalityFriction, applyNpcConsequences to use named trait constants. Updated selectors/house.ts to import prestige, exterior, and defense constants from gameRules.ts. Updated NpcDetailPanel.tsx to use TRAIT_HIGH and TRAIT_NOTABLY_LOW.

title: Convert void-mutation functions in adjustRelationship.ts to pure returns
description: Why: applyPassiveDrift and applyProximityGains mutate state in place and return void. Pure-function pattern requires returning a new GameState. What: change both to return GameState. applyNpcConsequences.ts already reassigns result. Acceptance: no void-return mutation functions in commands/; all tests pass.
close_reason: Converted applyPassiveDrift and applyProximityGains to pure returns. Updated call sites and tests.

title: Wrap InvestigationScreen and ShopsScreen command imports in selectors
description: Why: InvestigationScreen.tsx:10 imports INVESTIGATION_APPROACHES from commands/investigation. ShopsScreen.tsx:7 imports getDurabilityTier from commands/durability. Both are boundary violations. What: create selectInvestigationApproaches() and selectEquippedItemDurabilityStatus(npcId, slot). Acceptance: neither screen imports from application/commands/.
close_reason: Created selectors/investigation.ts with selectInvestigationApproaches(). Added getDurabilityTier re-export to selectors/durability.ts. Updated InvestigationScreen.tsx and ShopsScreen.tsx to import from selectors instead of commands.

title: Wrap DialogueScreen command import in selector
description: Why: DialogueScreen.tsx:5 imports isDialogueChoiceAvailable from commands/dialogue. UI must not import from commands. What: create selectVisibleDialogueChoices(nodeId) returning pre-filtered choices. Component renders what the selector returns. Acceptance: DialogueScreen has zero imports from application/commands/.
close_reason: Added selectVisibleDialogueChoices(nodeId) to selectors/dialogue.ts. Updated DialogueScreen.tsx to use useAppSelector with the new selector instead of directly importing and calling isDialogueChoiceAvailable from commands/dialogue.

title: Add ID constants files to eliminate string literals in content
description: Event and quest JSON files reference IDs like 'quest-gilded-court-favor' and 'district-pale-warrens' as raw strings. Typos and renames are invisible until runtime. For TypeScript code (commands, selectors), fix: create src/application/content/ids/ directory with files like questIds.ts (export const QUEST_IDS = { GILDED_COURT_FAVOR: 'quest-gilded-court-favor' } as const), npcIds.ts, factionIds.ts, districtIds.ts. All command and selector code uses constants, never string literals. JSON content cannot use TypeScript constants — for JSON, rely on the build-time integrity check (separate task). Acceptance: no command or selector file contains a hardcoded entity ID string literal.

title: Add TypeScript branded types for all entity IDs
description: Entity IDs (quest IDs, NPC IDs, faction IDs, district IDs, event IDs, item IDs) are plain strings throughout the codebase. Passing a faction ID where a district ID is expected causes a runtime bug, not a compile-time error. Fix: define branded types: type QuestId = string & { readonly _brand: 'QuestId' }. Apply to all ID fields in domain schemas. Update contentCatalog lookup maps to use typed keys. This catches category mistakes at compile time with zero runtime cost. Acceptance: passing an NpcId where a QuestId is expected produces a TypeScript error; existing tests continue to pass.

title: Model the Gilded Court as named creditor for state.debt
description: state.game.debtAmount is an abstract number. The narrative establishes the debt as a forged promissory note filed by the Gilded Court — it is not a real debt but a manufactured legal weapon. The creditor is not modeled as a faction or NPC connection. Fix: add debtCreditorFactionId to GameState (default: 'faction-gilded-court'). applyPolitics uses this to apply faction-specific interest rates (worse Gilded Court standing = higher daily interest). Paying off the debt improves Gilded Court standing slightly (not forgiveness — acknowledgment). Player impact: the debt feels like confronting the enemy, not reducing a number. Acceptance: debt interest scales with Gilded Court faction standing; paying debt changes faction standing.

title: Audit and replace 'Accept X' event choice labels
description: At least 4 event choice labels in events.json use the generic passive verb 'Accept': 'Accept the meeting', 'Accept the supplies', 'Accept the treatment', 'Accept the arrangement'. The narrative voice guide demands motive-driven, concrete language. 'Accept' describes what happens to the player, not what they do. Fix: audit all event choice labels. Replace passive 'Accept X' with active context-specific verbs: 'Attend the meeting', 'Take the supplies', 'Submit to treatment', 'Agree to the arrangement'. Extend the standard to all future choices. Acceptance: no event choice label uses 'Accept' as a standalone verb; all labels use first-person active verbs.

title: Author birth originStory templates with narrative weight
description: Biological heirs born via tickPregnancyProgress receive a generated originStory: 'Born to ${npc.name} within House Valdris.' This is a placeholder. The setting's treatment of birth, legitimacy, and the Breeding Register is one of its most morally charged areas. A one-sentence location stamp does not match the fiction's weight. Fix: author 3–5 originStory templates keyed to relationship context: committed relationship (private will vs public obligation), unacknowledged (registered but unnamed), contested legitimacy (Merrow Registry notified), ward-born (different social position). tickPregnancyProgress selects based on relationship intimacyStage and legitimacyStatus. Acceptance: no heir has a placeholder originStory; each template passes the voice guide's 'concrete and consequential' test.

title: Tech Infrastructure: Branded ID Types, Build-Time Catalog Checks, Constants Files
description: The audit found multiple broken content references (missing quests, wrong district IDs) that are only discovered at runtime when a feature silently fails. There are no compile-time or build-time guardrails. String IDs used in events, quests, and NPC definitions are untyped — a typo or rename causes silent failure. This epic adds three layers of protection: TypeScript branded types for IDs, build-time catalog integrity validation, and ID constants files to eliminate string literals.

title: Add pairing policy UI to HouseScreen
description: npcPairingPolicy is enforced in applyNpcPairing but the player has no way to view or change it. The house defaults to 'open' and stays there. Fix: add a household policy section to HouseScreen with a selector showing current policy and a control to dispatch setNpcPairingPolicy. Policy labels should use the fiction-appropriate framing already established (not clinical language). Acceptance: player can view and change pairing policy from the house screen.

title: Implement effects for unused room function types or remove them
description: Room function types 'workshop', 'archive', 'infirmary', 'reception' are defined in the schema and assignable via assignRoomFunction, but have no mechanical effect. Assigning a room to 'archive' changes state but nothing happens. This invites authoring confusion. Fix decision: either implement effects for each (archive: +1 rumor/day; infirmary: +1 recovery rate; workshop: +crafting skill gain; reception: +hire offer quality), or remove them from the schema. Acceptance: every assignable room function type has a documented, implemented effect.
notes: Architect review 2026-05-29: room function effects should be reconsidered against the broader location model; avoid implementing player-house-only semantics that later conflict with world households.

title: Unify roster capacity selectors into one canonical source
description: selectRosterCapacity (selectors/household.ts) reads state.house.rosterBonus directly. selectCrewCapacity (selectors/house.ts) recomputes from room function bonuses. Two independent calculations can produce different values. If rosterBonus is stale after migration, they disagree. Fix: pick one as canonical (recommended: selectRosterCapacity reading stored bonus). The other imports and delegates to it. Acceptance: only one capacity calculation exists; grep finds no duplicate implementations.
close_reason: Removed selectCrewCapacity from house.ts (was unused). selectRosterCapacity in household.ts is now the sole canonical roster capacity selector.

title: Log corruption reduction on quest payout
description: questSettlement.ts:221–227 reduces quest rewards by 10% when corruption >= 70. No activity log entry explains the deduction. The player sees a lower payout than the contract template shows with no reason. Fix: appendActivityLogEntry when the modifier applies: 'Corruption shaved ${reducedAmount} Marks from the payout.' Acceptance: every corruption-reduced payout produces a log entry naming the amount.

title: Add currency helper formatMarks() and use everywhere
description: Money is displayed as 'X Mk', 'X Marks', 'X marks', or just a number depending on screen. Activity log entries say 'money' instead of 'Marks'. The currency name is a repeated string literal scattered across 15+ files. Fix: create src/domain/game/currency.ts exporting CURRENCY_UNIT = 'Marks', CURRENCY_ABBREV = 'Mk', formatMarks(amount): string, formatMarksAbbrev(amount): string. All UI components and activity log strings use these. Acceptance: the string literal 'Marks' does not appear outside currency.ts; all money display uses formatMarks or formatMarksAbbrev.

title: Centralise repair discount logic — eliminate selector/reducer duplication
description: The quartermaster repair discount (20%) is applied in two independent places: ShopsScreen.tsx:322 (display) and gameSlice.ts:544 (actual deduction). If one is updated, displayed price and actual deducted price diverge. Fix: create pure function computeRepairCost(baseCost, hasQuartermaster): number in equipmentCatalog.ts or a new economics utility. Both call sites import and call it. Acceptance: repair discount logic exists in exactly one place; grep finds no other implementations.

title: Remove dashboard meta quick-route shortcuts
description: Dashboard currently exposes non-diegetic Quick Routes links for shops and ledger, undermining the location-first world model.
acceptance_criteria: Dashboard no longer renders Quick Routes or direct ledger/shop shortcuts; related tests updated; search completed for similar meta-shortcut copy and findings documented; pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build pass.
notes:
Why: breaks diegetic navigation and contradicts district/POI flow. What: remove Quick Routes block from dashboard and any tests asserting it. Why now: user flagged it as immersion-breaking and likely indicates similar meta shortcuts. Player impact: dashboard stops bypassing the world model. System impact: dashboard screen/tests only. Does not include broader navigation redesign. Finding coverage: user report 2026-05-15 dashboard quick routes.
Origin check: introduced in commit 125664c (feat: streamline ledger and shop navigation). Similarity audit: exact management shortcuts removed; remaining directive links are story/quest guidance only (Dashboard 'What next' and first-run 'Check the Work Board', ContractBoard 'Recommended Next Step').

title: Contextual authored event instances for NPC-specific narrative events
description:
## Why
Some authored events need NPC-specific prose, source context, and one-time instancing without multiplying static templates. The current event system renders template-only title/description and resolves by raw eventId, which is too thin for high-specificity narrative events like captivity pregnancy discovery.

## What
Add a minimal contextual authored-event instance layer on top of the existing event template system.

## Why now
Mandatory Discovery Moment (`destiny-jcrj`) depends on NPC-specific discovery source and trait-shaped prose. Without instance-aware event presentation and per-instance resolution tracking, `jcrj` would collapse into a generic modal and miss its fiction contract.

## Player impact
Players see authored consequence events that feel about a specific person and moment, not a recycled system template.

## System impact
Touches event instance usage, modal rendering, resolveEvent behavior, and one command path that queues contextual event instances.

## Does not include
No broader event system rewrite. No generic event authoring UI. No additional reward mechanics.

## Finding coverage
Covers the contextual-runtime gap identified while triaging `destiny-jcrj`.
acceptance_criteria: Event modal can render per-instance presentation text for a generic event template; resolveEvent resolves one pending instance at a time and records chosenOptionId/resolvedOnDay; captivity/pregnancy discovery can queue NPC-specific presentation without duplicating templates; pnpm test:run, pnpm typecheck, pnpm build pass.

title: New NPC: Elyn -- The Growing Ward arc, childhood through young-adult, shaped by the house
description:
## Why
The age milestone system (destiny-d0cc) and the ward schema (destiny-fzzo) define the mechanics for children growing up during gameplay. Elyn is the first authored instance — a specific named child who arrives through Petra Sunn's network, placed under the house's care, and grows from early childhood into a young person over the course of gameplay. She is not a game system. She is a child who exists in the house. The player watches her grow up.

She demonstrates that the longest-horizon investment in this game is not building a district influence network or mastering faction standing — it is being the kind of house that a child can grow up in, and getting to see who they become.

## What

### NPC: Elyn — A child, currently seven, no surname yet, brought through Petra's network

Background: Petra Sunn placed Elyn with the house four days after the game starts. She does not give many details. Elyn's situation was not safe where it was. It is safer here. Elyn does not talk much yet. She watches. She has her mother's hands — Petra does not say whose mother.

Elyn is seven. She likes to be near Irenne's herb bundles. She has not cried in front of anyone since she arrived.

Origin: The Warrens (believed)
Status: ward
Faction affinity: none
Age band: child (7 years = ~700 game days before teenager)
Sex: girl
Rarity: unique (one instance; this NPC does not appear in hire pools — she arrives via event)

Starting traits (genuinely unformed — childhood defaults):
- discipline: 20, ambition: 15, empathy: 45, ruthlessness: 8
- prudence: 28, curiosity: 62, dominance: 12, loyalty: 55, zeal: 18

Starting skills: all at 5 (baseline awareness only)
Base attributes: all at 20 (child physiological baseline)

Appearance: a small person who takes up much less space than she has opinions, always has something in her hands — a pebble, a piece of cord, someone else's forgotten button.

### Arc: arc-ward-growing

arcId: 'arc-ward-growing'
Starting stage: 'early-childhood' (age 7 = 700 game days to teenager at 1100 days)

This arc uses the destiny-d0cc age milestone system with specific authored beats for Elyn.

Stage: early-childhood (days 0–400 in house, age 7–11)
- Elyn is in the background. She appears in activity log entries when specific NPCs interact with her.
- Passive: high-empathy NPCs (>60) on the roster automatically generate "Elyn time" entries — she follows them, listens, asks questions that are logged.
- Authored milestone: at ~200 days in house, 'elyn-first-word' event: Elyn says something unprompted to a specific NPC. Which NPC is the first she speaks to unprompted is written to stageFlags. This is the beginning of her attachment arc.
- Trait drift: she absorbs from household adults at ×3 rate (children form faster). Her dominant household influences write into her slowly-forming traits.

Stage: childhood (days 400–900 in house, age 11–16)
- Elyn starts to be visible as a person. She has preferences. She notices things.
- Authored milestone: 'elyn-asks-to-help' event at ~500 days. She asks to be given something real to do. Player choices: assign her to a role (light scouting, herb gathering with Irenne, administrative observation), redirect her to study, or decline. Her response to each is authored and affects her trust in the player.
- If assigned to Irenne: empathy and medicine skill bonus path
- If assigned to investigation/scouting: curiosity and stealth path
- If assigned to administration with Marion: discipline and administration path
- Authored milestone at ~800 days: 'elyn-knows-something' — Elyn has observed something she should not have and comes to the player (or doesn't, depending on trust). A small operational event that involves her having information.

Stage: early-adolescence (days 900–1100, age 16–18)
- Elyn is a young person with opinions. She has formed attachments. She has questions about her history.
- Authored milestone: 'elyn-asks-about-her-mother' — the question is inevitable. What the player knows and how they answer shapes her final-stage traits significantly.
- If Petra Sunn is on roster: Petra has a parallel event. She decides how much to tell. Player can facilitate or stay out.
- The answer (or the evasion) is written to stageFlags.

Stage: young-adult (days 1100+, age 18+)
- Elyn is now a functional house member. She has skills, she has traits, she has a history.
- The traits she has are determined by:
  a) Trait drift from household adults over 1100 days
  b) Authored milestone responses
  c) The answer she received (or didn't) about her mother
- Authored event: 'elyn-chooses' — Elyn tells the player what she wants to do with her life. Four possible paths based on her developed dominant trait:
  - empathy dominant: she wants to work with Irenne or Petra — helping people move through the network
  - curiosity dominant: she wants to investigate something specific (connected to her own backstory)
  - discipline dominant: she wants formal responsibility — to be given a title and trusted with it
  - loyalty dominant: she tells the player she is staying. She does not need a reason beyond this house.

### Elyn and Petra:
If Petra Sunn is on the roster when Elyn arrives:
- A silent event fires: Petra watches from across the room when Elyn is shown her space.
- No text. Activity log: "Petra Sunn was near the door when Elyn arrived. She left before anyone spoke to her."
- Petra's trust with player +3 (she is watching whether the house keeps its word)
- Over the following 30 days, Petra visits Elyn once per week. These are logged silently.
- At 60 days: 'petra-elyn-understanding' event — Petra and Elyn have found an arrangement. Neither will explain it to anyone.

### Ward care system (from destiny-fzzo/d0cc):
Elyn needs:
- daily upkeep: 2 Marks (food, basic needs — not tracked as coin pressure but logged)
- a guardian NPC assigned (optional but affects trait drift: assigned guardian = primary influencer)
- medical access: if condition drops below 50, Cress or Irenne event fires

### Pre-existing relationships at arrival:
Elyn × Petra Sunn: affinity 28, trust 22 (Petra→Elyn), 24/18 (Elyn→Petra) — Petra moved her; Elyn trusts warily

All other NPCs: no existing relationship. Elyn builds from zero based on who she encounters.

## Why now
Elyn arrives in the first week of the game. She is a presence from the beginning — not a problem to solve, just a person who is there. Her growth is the longest arc in the game. The player who pays attention for 1100 days gets to see a child become a person. The player who doesn't still has Elyn in the background, logging her quiet observations.

She is also the proof of concept for destiny-fzzo/d0cc in a form that has emotional stakes. The mechanics work or they don't — and here, it matters whether they work.

## Player impact
Elyn represents the house as a place people grow up in, not just a platform for operations. Her presence asks the player a different question than any other NPC: not "what can you do with her?" but "what kind of house are you building, that a child is growing up in it?"

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json: 1 new entry (Elyn) with npcArc pre-populated
- data/definitions/npc-arcs.json: arc-ward-growing definition
- data/definitions/events.json: 8 authored events (first-word, asks-to-help, knows-something, asks-about-mother, petra-understanding, elyn-chooses ×4 variants)
- src/application/commands/applyNpcTraitDrift.ts: arc-ward-growing uses ×3 drift rate
- Elyn arrives via a specific trigger event on day 3-5 (not in hire pool; destiny-fzzo warrant type: 'placement')
- data/definitions/npc-starting-relationships.json: 1 entry (Petra→Elyn)

## Does not include
Elyn's specific backstory (who her mother is — this is narrative design for the Petra Sunn quest bead)
What happens if the house is destroyed or disbanded before Elyn reaches young-adult
Elyn as a combat NPC (not in this bead)

## Acceptance criteria
NPC definition parses. Arc initializes in early-childhood stage. Trait drift applies at ×3 rate. Guardian assignment writes primary influencer to stageFlags. All 8 authored events parse. Petra silent event fires on day 3-5. Age milestone stage transitions fire at correct day thresholds. pnpm test:run passes.

## Finding coverage
destiny-fzzo (child/ward schema). destiny-d0cc (age milestone system). destiny-529i (arc system). Design paper §5 (the long arc, the growing ward).
close_reason: Elyn authored: NPC definition, arc-ward-growing (4 stages), 8 milestone events, addNpcToRoster outcome type, ward status, child ageBand, x3 drift rate for ward arc. 985 tests passing.

title: New NPC: Bren Aldoth -- The Fractured arc, trauma progression, anchor NPC healing mechanic
description:
## Why
Combat and hardship in the current system affect morale states and relationship axes — but they do not change who an NPC is. Bren Aldoth is designed around the opposite premise: something already changed him before the player met him, and the player's house will determine whether that change becomes permanent damage or an eventual return. He is the first NPC whose arc can go badly — who can end up worse than he started, if the player ignores what he needs or pushes him into the wrong situations.

He also fills a specific world gap: a character who represents the personal cost of institutional loyalty. He did everything right for people who did not deserve his fidelity. Now he is figuring out what fidelity means when the institution is gone.

## What

### NPC: Bren Aldoth — Former garrison operative, fractured but holding, looking for something to hold onto

Background: Fourteen years in a specialized Compact enforcement unit — the kind of unit that is called in when the public version of enforcement has already tried and failed. He is good at it. He is very good at it. The last operation his unit ran ended badly in a way that was not his fault, not exactly, and that he cannot fully account for and will not try to. Three of his unit did not come back. He filed the report. He accepted the severance. He has not spoken to anyone from the unit since.

He applied to the house because it was a specific kind of work with a specific kind of structure and he needs both of those things right now more than he needs to admit he needs them.

Origin: Harbor Ward (garrison posted; not born there)
Status: mercenary
Faction affinity: none (formerly faction-civic-compact; the connection is severed in fact if not in record)
Age band: middle
Sex: man
Rarity: rare

Starting traits:
- discipline: 81, ambition: 28, empathy: 38, ruthlessness: 62
- prudence: 44, curiosity: 32, dominance: 58, loyalty: 68, zeal: 42

Starting skills:
- melee: 74, ranged: 66, stealth: 58, investigation: 52
- survival: 48, medicine: 26, administration: 24, scouting: 62, engineering: 14, negotiation: 18

Base attributes:
- might: 68, agility: 62, endurance: 71, intellect: 52, perception: 66, presence: 46, resolve: 56

Appearance: fills doorways without meaning to, makes eye contact slightly too long then breaks it, has not told anyone what the operation was called.

Quirks:
- counts exits and headcounts in any new location before relaxing (triggers: building, location, enter)
- asks the names of people who died in operations he was not part of (triggers: death, loss, mission gone wrong)

Motivation:
- publicGoal: 'To be useful in a way that does not require him to think about why.'
- privateNeed: 'To find out if he is still someone worth trusting.'
- immediateNeed: 'Work that demands enough focus that he does not have to be anywhere else in his own head.'

Allowed titles: ['title-trainer', 'title-quartermaster']

### Arc: arc-fractured

arcId: 'arc-fractured'
Starting stage: 'intact' — the fracture exists but is not visible yet

Stage: intact (starting stage)
- Bren performs normally. High discipline carries operations.
- Hidden flag: fractureVisible = false
- Transition conditions: Bren is deployed on 3+ missions rated 'brutal' OR is co-deployed with a high-ruthlessness NPC (>65) for 20+ days OR a specific trigger event fires (a mission goes badly and someone dies)
- Transition event: 'bren-fracture-visible' — a quiet, specific moment where something leaks through. Not a breakdown. A pause. A wrong response. The player sees something is there.

Stage: cracking (after fracture becomes visible)
- fractureVisible = true
- Bren's effectiveness in high-pressure operations -15% (reflected in combat outcome modifier)
- Transition conditions: TWO paths diverge here
  Path A (healing): Bren is assigned to low-risk roles for 30+ days AND a specific relationship develops (trust ≥ 40 with an empathy>60 NPC on the roster)
  Path B (breaking): Bren continues to be deployed in brutal operations OR is 30 days without meaningful interaction
  If Path A conditions met first: transition to 'healing'
  If Path B conditions met first: transition to 'broken'

Stage: healing (Path A)
- Effectiveness penalty removed
- Bren begins to talk. Specific NPC who holds high trust (the 'anchor' NPC) receives authored dialogue events
- Transition: 60+ days in healing + player responds supportively to anchor-NPC event
- Transition event: 'bren-returning' — the player witnesses Bren do something that indicates he is finding his way back. Small. Specific.
- Terminal sub-stage: 'returned' — Bren is not who he was before the operation. He is something else: someone who knows what broke and chose to not be broken by it. New motivation written: 'To be the person who does not walk away from the ones who come back changed.'

Stage: broken (Path B)
- Effectiveness penalty -30%
- Bren becomes a departure risk (npcDeparture system activates)
- Authored event: 'bren-leaving-warning' — Bren says something indirect that is a direct warning
- Player has 10 days to act: assign him to low-risk, increase pay, or have the anchor NPC (if one exists) speak to him
- If no action: 'bren-left' event fires; Bren departs. The house lives with what it made.
- If player acts and conditions met: advance to 'healing' (rare late rescue)

### Anchor NPC mechanic:
The first NPC on the roster with empathy > 60 who accumulates trust ≥ 40 with Bren becomes his 'anchor'. Written to stageFlags: { anchorNpcId: string }. The anchor NPC receives authored optional dialogue beats that give them the chance to reach Bren. Player can encourage or ignore. Vael, Oswin, Cress Aldmoor, and (if recruited) Irenne Brek or Petra Sunn are natural candidates.

### Pre-existing relationships (authored Tier 1):

Bren × Verek Holst:
- Bren->Holst: affinity 22, trust 18
- Holst->Bren: affinity 20, trust 14
- History: Both came out of Compact enforcement in different units. They know each other's names and reputation. Not friends — professional acknowledgment. Holst is one of the few people from that world Bren does not actively avoid. ~8 years of institutional overlap.

Bren × Halvard Senn (cross-bead, destiny-h4r8):
- Bren->Halvard: affinity 14, trust 10
- Halvard->Bren: affinity 18, trust 12
- History: Halvard's unit was senior to Bren's. Halvard knows something about the final operation but has never been asked. Bren does not know Halvard knows. Tense, unspoken proximity. ~4 years.

## Why now
Bren demonstrates the arc system's capacity for downward arcs, not just growth arcs. He is the first NPC who can leave because of what the house did to him — and whose departure, if it happens, is authored as the house's failure, not a random event. He also activates the empathy-NPC anchor mechanic, which makes existing high-empathy NPCs (Vael, Oswin, Irenne) feel like they serve a purpose beyond their skills.

## Player impact
Bren is a high-skill combat asset whose value comes with responsibility attached. Players who use him without attention lose him; players who pay attention get access to a character arc about trauma, return, and what the house owes the people who serve it. The player who lets Bren break is also the player who built a house that breaks people.

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json: 1 new NPC entry (Bren Aldoth) with npcArc pre-populated
- data/definitions/npc-arcs.json: arc-fractured definition added
- data/definitions/events.json: 6 authored events (fracture-visible, leaving-warning, bren-left, bren-returning, anchor dialogue ×2)
- src/application/commands/checkNpcArcTransitions.ts: arc-fractured path evaluation (Path A/B condition check, anchor detection)
- src/application/commands/npcDeparture.ts: integrate with broken stage departure risk
- data/definitions/npc-starting-relationships.json: 2 new Tier 1 edges

## Does not include
Bren's account of the final operation (separate authored quest event — only available in 'returned' stage)
Multiple simultaneous fractured NPCs (Bren is the authored instance; the arc system supports others but doesn't implement them)
Bren and Halvard confrontation event (separate authored event bead)

## Acceptance criteria
NPC definition parses. Arc initializes in intact stage. Fracture trigger conditions fire correctly. Path A and Path B evaluate correctly in cracking stage. Anchor NPC detection writes to stageFlags. Departure risk activates in broken stage. bren-left event fires after 10 days of no action in broken stage. pnpm test:run passes.

## Finding coverage
destiny-529i (arc system). Emergent NPC design §4 (The Fractured arc). npcDeparture integration.
close_reason: Bren Aldoth authored: NPC definition, arc-fractured reworked with intact/cracking/healing/broken/returned stages, Path A/B branching in checkFracturedArcBranching, anchor NPC detection, departure risk queueing, 6 events. 991 tests.

title: New NPC: Nessa Vain -- The Initiator arc, autonomous goal pursuit, weekly world moves
description:
## Why
Every NPC in the current system waits for the player. The player assigns them, deploys them, sends them. Nothing happens without player initiation. This is a fundamental limitation: the world feels static between player decisions. Nessa Vain changes this. She is an NPC who uses the house's platform to pursue her own agenda — and the player discovers what she has been doing, not just what they asked her to do. She generates situations the player did not create.

She also fills a specific world gap: the house has no one who thinks at the city planning and civic infrastructure level — who understands how districts work, what pressure points exist in the urban fabric, and how to move pieces into position before anyone notices you're playing.

## What

### NPC: Nessa Vain — Urban fixer, infrastructure reader, autonomous operator

Background: Spent eight years as a district resource coordinator for the Civic Compact — the person who managed which water distribution points got funding, which wall sections got inspected, which rebuild contracts went to which crews. She learned, in those eight years, exactly how the city's physical layer and its power layer interact. Who controls the infrastructure controls the flows. She left the Compact when she understood that the Compact's interest in this knowledge was narrower than her own.

She does not announce her plans. She makes moves. She tells you what she has accomplished when it is already done and cannot be undone.

Origin: Civic Quarter
Status: citizen
Faction affinity: none (formerly faction-civic-compact)
Age band: adult
Sex: woman
Rarity: rare

Starting traits:
- discipline: 72, ambition: 78, empathy: 34, ruthlessness: 58
- prudence: 66, curiosity: 74, dominance: 62, loyalty: 32, zeal: 28

Starting skills:
- administration: 78, investigation: 66, negotiation: 62, engineering: 52
- scouting: 44, stealth: 38, medicine: 11, melee: 18, ranged: 14, survival: 24

Base attributes:
- might: 34, agility: 44, endurance: 54, intellect: 74, perception: 68, presence: 62, resolve: 66

Appearance: carries annotated district maps she does not show people, addresses problems in terms of leverage points not solutions, remembers every piece of infrastructure she has personally assessed.

Quirks:
- mentally prices the replacement cost of every structure she enters (triggers: building, district, damage)
- does not ask for permission for small actions; reports their outcomes instead (triggers: decision, plan, ask)

Motivation:
- publicGoal: 'To make the house a durable presence in the city, not a temporary one.'
- privateNeed: 'To build something that will still be standing after she is not relevant to it.'
- immediateNeed: 'A principal with enough patience to let her work at the pace things actually move.'

Allowed titles: ['title-steward', 'title-quartermaster', 'title-chief-engineer']

### Arc: arc-initiator

The initiator arc extends applyNpcAgency with a weekly autonomous goal-pursuit check. This is a new AgencyAction type: 'initiative'.

```
// In applyNpcAgency, for NPCs with npcArc.arcId === 'arc-initiator':
// Additional weekly check (every 7 days based on state.day % 7 === npc.initiativeOffset):
if (npcArc.arcId === 'arc-initiator' && state.day % 7 === nessa.initiativeOffset) {
  const initiativeResult = evaluateInitiativeAction(state, npc, rng)
  // fires one of: district_lever, npc_approach, faction_position, resource_move
}
```

Initiative action types:

**district_lever:** Nessa applies pressure to or resolves a resource/tension node in a district she has been working in. Effect: districtTension ±5, or district resource modifier shift. Activity log: "Nessa Vain has been working a contact in [district]. [Observed outcome]."

**npc_approach:** Nessa initiates a relationship with another NPC on the house roster or a known contact, without player direction. Applies +3 affinity in both directions. Activity log: "Nessa Vain spent time with [NPC] this week. [Observation about why]."

**faction_position:** Nessa makes a move in the faction standing network — a small positive shift with one faction, small negative with a rival. Activity log: "Nessa Vain has been positioning the house with [faction]. This may have side effects."

**resource_move:** Nessa identifies and redirects a small resource stream. Coin balance +rng(15,40). Activity log: "Nessa Vain located an underused asset and redirected it. [Amount] Marks added."

### Initiative tone: the player is a partner, not a manager

Crucial design rule: Nessa's initiatives must always be presented as news, not requests. She does not ask "may I?" She reports "I have." The activity log entry always describes a completed action with its effect, not a proposed one needing approval.

The player can respond (via a follow-up choice event that fires 3 days after any district_lever or faction_position):
- Endorse: +1 morale to Nessa; locked-in effect
- Redirect: effect partially reversed; Nessa's trust in player -3
- Expand: spend coin to amplify the effect; Nessa's trust +2

If the player never responds, Nessa interprets this as endorsement. She is building regardless.

### Arc stages:

Stage: active (starting stage — Nessa begins taking initiatives immediately)
- 1 initiative action per week
- Transition condition: 5+ initiatives completed, player has endorsed or expanded 3+ times
- Transition: arc advances to 'aligned'

Stage: aligned
- 2 initiative actions per week
- Her actions are better calibrated to house interests (she has learned the player's patterns)
- Transition condition: player trust relationship ≥ 50 AND 10+ total initiatives
- Transition: arc advances to 'partner'

Stage: partner (near-terminal)
- Nessa now proposes one large initiative per month (a multi-week operation she needs player support for)
- These are authored events, not procedural — significant world effects
- 'partner' stage events: acquiring a district resource node, neutralizing a rival faction operation, establishing a durable information pipeline

### Pre-existing relationships (authored Tier 1):

Nessa × Marion Vale:
- Nessa->Marion: affinity 30, trust 22
- Marion->Nessa: affinity 26, trust 18
- History: Both moved in Compact administrative channels. Marion filed; Nessa coordinated. Professional wariness — Marion recognizes someone who operates the way she herself does, and finds it uncomfortable. ~6 years of institutional overlap.

Nessa × Orren Wex:
- Nessa->Orren: affinity 36, trust 28
- Orren->Nessa: affinity 32, trust 24
- History: Orren (archival quarter) and Nessa (district coordination) intersected on infrastructure documentation projects. He archives; she applies. Mutually useful. ~4 years.

Nessa × Dael Morw (cross-bead, destiny-h4r8):
- Nessa->Dael: affinity 22, trust 14
- Dael->Nessa: affinity 20, trust 16
- History: She processed districts; he processed people in those districts. Adjacent systems. He knows she was good at her job; she knows he cared too much about his. ~5 years of indirect overlap.

## Why now
Nessa is the most direct answer to a house that feels static between player actions. Once she is on the roster, something is always happening. She is also the first demonstration of what the arc-initiator system enables: an NPC who generates story without being prompted. The player's relationship with her is the most interesting player-NPC dynamic available — not romance, not loyalty, but mutual respect between two people who both think they know what needs to be done.

## Player impact
The house feels alive even when the player hasn't opened the game in three in-game days. Nessa's weekly initiative actions mean there is always a new entry in the activity log. Players who engage with her arcs gain access to district-level leverage and faction positioning at minimal coin cost. Players who ignore her get free resource trickles and mild faction shifts they didn't choose.

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json: 1 new NPC entry (Nessa Vain) with npcArc pre-populated (arc-initiator, stage: active)
- data/definitions/npc-arcs.json: arc-initiator definition added alongside arc-becoming
- src/application/commands/applyNpcAgency.ts: add 'initiative' action type; weekly check for arc-initiator NPCs; evaluateInitiativeAction function
- data/definitions/npc-starting-relationships.json: 3 new Tier 1 edges
- data/definitions/events.json: 3 authored 'partner' stage events + endorsement follow-up event structure

## Does not include
Nessa's personal quest line (what she is actually trying to build — separate narrative bead)
UI display of her initiative log (separate)
Conflict event if player redirects 3+ times (Nessa considers leaving — separate event bead)

## Acceptance criteria
NPC definition parses. Weekly initiative check fires correctly. All 4 initiative action types produce correct state changes and activity log entries. Stage transitions advance on conditions. Partner stage authored events parse. pnpm test:run passes.

## Finding coverage
destiny-529i (arc system). Emergent NPC design §3 (The Initiator). applyNpcAgency extension.
close_reason: Nessa Vain authored: NPC definition with defaultArcId, arc-initiator updated (active/aligned/partner), weekly initiative actions (4 types), defaultArcId on npcDefinitionSchema, recruitNpc arc initialization, 3 partner events, 6 relationship edges. 996 tests.

title: New NPC: Lissel Crane -- The Becoming arc, trait drift, crystallization into who she will be
description:
## Why
Most NPCs arrive at the house already formed. Lissel Crane arrives still becoming. She is not a blank slate — she has experiences that shaped her starting state — but she has not yet resolved into the person she will be. This is a design opportunity that no current NPC offers: a character whose identity is genuinely unfinished and whose trajectory is determined by what happens in the house. She is the first implementation of the arc-becoming system (destiny-529i).

She also fills a real functional gap: the house currently has no NPC who is genuinely young and inexperienced and growing. All others arrived at competence. Lissel arrives at potential.

## What

### NPC: Lissel Crane — Young, unformed, watching everything

Background: Came out of the Warrens food network periphery — not as an organizer like Oswin, not as someone with a plan, but as someone who was useful when people needed a quick pair of hands and learned by watching. She is perceptive in the way that people who have never been sure of their safety become perceptive: she reads rooms before she enters them, remembers conversations she wasn't supposed to be part of, and says less than she knows. She does not yet know what to do with any of this.

She is nineteen. She has good instincts and no framework for them yet.

Origin: The Warrens
Status: citizen
Faction affinity: none
Age band: young
Sex: woman
Rarity: uncommon

Starting traits (genuinely low — still forming):
- discipline: 32, ambition: 44, empathy: 52, ruthlessness: 28
- prudence: 38, curiosity: 68, dominance: 18, loyalty: 48, zeal: 24

Starting skills (low across the board — she is genuinely inexperienced):
- investigation: 38, stealth: 34, negotiation: 22, survival: 28
- melee: 14, ranged: 12, medicine: 16, administration: 18, engineering: 9, scouting: 32

Base attributes:
- might: 36, agility: 58, endurance: 48, intellect: 54, perception: 72, presence: 44, resolve: 42

Appearance: always positioned near the edge of a room, keeps a small notebook she fills with other people's words not her own, looks older than nineteen when she's focused and younger when she's not.

Quirks:
- writes down what people say in meetings she attends, then reads it back to herself later (triggers: decision, meeting, plan)
- identifies the person in any group who is most afraid and watches them (triggers: threat, tension, crowd)

Motivation:
- publicGoal: 'To be useful enough to stay.'
- privateNeed: 'To understand why people like Petra or Oswin choose to keep doing what costs them everything.'
- immediateNeed: 'Not to make a mistake that proves she wasn't ready.'

Allowed titles: ['title-quartermaster'] (becomes eligible for more as she develops)

### Arc: arc-becoming

arcId: 'arc-becoming'
Starting stage: 'forming'

Stage: forming (starting stage)
- Trait drift rate: ×2 (she absorbs from those around her faster than settled adults)
- Transition conditions: any single trait reaches 55+, after minimum 30 days
- Transition event: 'lissel-first-shape' — Lissel tells the player she has noticed something about herself changing. Not dramatic. Quiet. The player can respond in ways that either encourage or redirect.

Stage: crystallizing (after first transition)
- Trait drift rate: ×1.5 (still forming, but the core is setting)
- A dominant trait has emerged — the highest trait at transition defines the 'type' she is becoming. Written to stageFlags: { type: 'disciplined' | 'empathic' | 'ruthless' | 'curious' }
- The authored milestone event for each type is different
- Transition conditions: all core traits above 45, after minimum 60 more days
- Transition event: 'lissel-crystallized-[type]' — four different authored events, one per type

Stage: set (terminal)
- Trait drift stops
- Lissel now functions like a settled NPC — traits no longer drift from proximity
- She is who she became. The house made her. The player made her.
- Final event: 'lissel-settled' — Lissel acknowledges, in her way, what the house gave her. Not gratitude necessarily — truth.

### Authored event beats:

'lissel-first-shape':
- Title: "Something Is Settling"
- Lissel reports noticing that she reacts differently than she expected in a specific recent situation (auto-selected from recent activity log)
- Player outcomes: [acknowledge, redirect, ask what she wants to become]
- Mechanical effect: small trait nudge based on player response (+3 to trait associated with response)

'lissel-crystallized-disciplined' (if discipline emerged dominant):
- Title: "She Has Found Her Bearing"
- Lissel has developed structure. She requests more systematic responsibility.
- Unlock: title-quartermaster becomes fully eligible; training output increases
- The authored text reflects Halvard or Holst as likely influences (if either is on roster)

'lissel-crystallized-empathic' (empathy dominant):
- Title: "She Sees What Others Miss"
- Lissel has developed into a reader of people. She is beginning to use it intentionally.
- Unlock: investigation skill accelerated growth; access to social mission roles
- Reflects Vael, Oswin, or Petra as likely influences

'lissel-crystallized-ruthless' (ruthlessness dominant):
- Title: "The Softness Has Gone Out of Her"
- This is a harder outcome. Not evil — but the house showed her certain things and she adjusted.
- Unlock: security/combat roles; player receives a quiet note that this is the house they built
- Content note: this outcome requires the player to have used Lissel in high-ruthlessness contexts repeatedly

'lissel-crystallized-curious' (curiosity, already high, fully dominant):
- Title: "She Has Found Her Question"
- Lissel has become an investigator — someone who needs to understand things.
- Unlock: investigation lead role; rumor network access; Dael Morw relationship auto-improves

### Pre-existing relationships (authored Tier 1):

Lissel × Oswin Farr:
- Lissel->Oswin: affinity 28, trust 22
- Oswin->Lissel: affinity 32, trust 26
- History: She was on the periphery of his Warrens distribution network when she was fifteen. He gave her tasks when she showed up. He didn't formally recruit her; she just kept being useful. 4+ years of loose affiliation.

Lissel × Petra Sunn (cross-bead, destiny-3y0k):
- Lissel->Petra: affinity 36, trust 30
- Petra->Lissel: affinity 34, trust 28
- History: Petra moved Lissel through a safe-house chain when she was seventeen. Lissel does not know all of what Petra does; Petra does not know all of what Lissel saw. Mutual recognition of people who operate in the same spaces. 2+ years.

## Why now
Lissel is the first test of the arc-becoming system. She demonstrates that the system works narratively before more complex arcs (fractured, initiator) are built. She also provides the player with a meaningful long-term investment: the character they grow is different from the one someone else would grow.

## Player impact
