captivityState (destiny-4n2j) models the what. coercionRisk models the how fast and contributes to the aftermath probability. Without this, all captive NPCs degrade identically regardless of character — which removes the meaning of NPC traits in the survival layer.

## Player impact
- High-resolve NPCs resist captivity longer and return with shallower damage
- Resilience label on NPC detail panel: resilient / vulnerable / at risk (label only, no number)
- Assigning a vulnerable NPC to a high-exposure mission is a visible management risk
- Recovery time after rescue is visibly longer for vulnerable NPCs
- Pregnancy aftermath, if present: discovered through authored event, never predicted or optimized

## System impact
- src/application/selectors/npcs.ts: selectNpcCoercionRisk(npc) computed score
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts: captivity degradation uses coercionRisk as modifier
- src/application/commands/rescueNpc.ts: rare aftermath check uses coercionRisk as one input factor
- src/ui/: NPC detail panel shows resilience label only
- No schema additions (computed from existing trait fields)

## Does not include
- Player-triggered pregnancy
- Probability exposure or any UI around pregnancy likelihood
- coerced-consent as a player-facing label
- Any mechanic that rewards exploiting vulnerable NPCs
- Vulnerability as a stat players min-max for non-protective purposes

## Evidence
GD+ND+SA+TC panel round 1: protection system, not exploitation mechanic. Round 2 revision: world-generated aftermath is a valid dark fantasy state when non-optimizable, non-exposed, and emotionally restrained. TC: rare, brief, sober, no reward loop, no player instruction around causing it.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: resilience label on NPC detail panel; vulnerable NPCs degrade faster in captivity; protective assignment prevents field exposure; pregnancy aftermath discovered through authored event only
2. System / technical: selectNpcCoercionRisk computed from existing trait fields; endDay captivity tick uses modifier; rescueNpc aftermath check uses coercionRisk as one factor with probability never exposed; no new schema fields
3. Tests / verification: selectNpcCoercionRisk returns correct relative values; high-resolve NPC degrades slower than low-resolve in captivity; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-4n2j (captivityState + pregnancyState schema — coercionRisk modifies degradation pipeline and feeds aftermath check)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+TC+SA captivity panels (both rounds): stat-driven vulnerability, protection mechanic, revised pregnancy verdict

title: captivityState schema on npcRuntimeState
description:
## Why
NPCs can go missing, be captured during missions, be held by enemy factions, or return changed. Currently npcRuntimeState has no model for this. There is no captive status, no condition field, no compliance axis, no bond type, and no model for what captivity leaves behind. The Mira arc (destiny-qx1v) requires captivityState to model her discovery state. World NPC captivity during missions has no mechanical representation — outcomes are flat (rescued/not rescued) with no NPC-level consequence.

## What
Add captivityState and pregnancyState as optional nested objects on npcRuntimeStateSchema.

### captivityState

```ts
export const captivityStateSchema = z.object({
  status: z.enum(['missing', 'captive', 'rescued', 'returned', 'dead']),
  holderId: z.string().nullable().default(null),
  condition: z.enum(['healthy', 'hurt', 'broken', 'altered']).default('healthy'),
  compliance: z.enum(['resistant', 'conflicted', 'compliant']).default('resistant'),
  bondType: z.enum(['none', 'fear', 'dependency', 'affection', 'coercion']).default('none'),
  timeHeldDays: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
  questTag: z.string().nullable().default(null),
}).optional()
```

### pregnancyState

```ts
export const pregnancyStateSchema = z.object({
  context: z.enum(['consensual', 'unknown']),
  daysElapsed: z.number().int().nonneg().default(0),
  questTag: z.string().nullable().default(null),
}).optional()
```

context: 'unknown' is used when pregnancy results from captivity. The coercion linkage is never surfaced as a player-facing label — it lives only in hidden provenance (questTag or internal event log). context: 'consensual' is for freely chosen relationships.

### Recovery states after rescue
- trauma debuff: temporary skill penalties (existing modifier system)
- loyalty shift: trust axis drops
- fear shift: fear axis rises
- condition-to-modifier mapping: 'broken' = -20 all skills temporary; 'altered' = changed trait values

### Captivity degradation over time
Each day captive: condition can worsen at threshold (every 7 days without rescue = condition step down). At 'broken': rescue difficulty rises. Checked in endDay processing pipeline.

### Pregnancy as world-generated aftermath (PANEL DECISION)
pregnancyState may be set when an NPC is rescued from captivity, as a rare world-generated consequence — never as a player-triggered or player-optimizable outcome. Probability is NEVER exposed to the player. No UI or action increases it. No feedback loop where sending an NPC into captivity produces an expected pregnancy outcome. Discovery occurs through healer, guard report, or the NPC herself. Player choices on discovery: protect / investigate / conceal / confront captor.

pregnancyState is NEVER set by direct player action. It is set only by event resolution logic as a rare aftermath flag.

## Why now
destiny-qx1v (Mira arc) needs captivityState to model her discovery state. destiny-pws9 (world NPC runtime state) is the parent schema. Without this, any mission that results in NPC capture has no state to write to and no aftermath to model.

## Player impact
- Missing NPCs show a 'missing' badge with days elapsed
- Rescued NPCs carry visible recovery state (debuffs) with authored explanation
- Time pressure: the player sees condition worsen if rescue is delayed
- Pregnancy state, if present: discovered through authored event, not a UI flag. Player faces support/investigation/protection choices.
- No explicit simulation of captivity events — player sees state, not acts

## System impact
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts: add captivityStateSchema, pregnancyStateSchema, add both optional fields to npcRuntimeStateSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json: no change (both fields optional, default undefined)
- src/application/commands/endDay.ts: captivity degradation tick; coercionRisk modifier applied
- src/application/commands/rescueNpc.ts: clears captivityState, applies recovery modifiers, runs rare aftermath check (pregnancyState may be set here as world event)
- src/application/selectors/npcs.ts: selectCaptiveNpcs, selectNpcCaptivityState

## Does not include
- Player-triggered pregnancy mechanics
- Probability exposure or UI around pregnancy likelihood
- Detailed simulation of captivity acts
- Assault depicted graphically
- Any mechanic that rewards captivity or exploitation
- coerced-consent as a player-facing label or category

## Evidence
GD+ND+SA+TC captivity panel (round 2): world-generated captivity aftermath pregnancy is a valid dark fantasy state. Mechanic-driven pregnancy remains OUT. Schema: pregnancyState optional with context enum. Probability never exposed.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Player-visible: Missing NPCs show status and days elapsed; rescued NPCs show recovery debuffs with duration; pregnancy state discovered through authored event only, never a direct UI flag
2. System / technical: captivityStateSchema and pregnancyStateSchema added to npc/contracts.ts; both optional on npcRuntimeStateSchema; endDay ticks condition degradation using coercionRisk modifier; rescueNpc clears captivityState and applies recovery; pregnancyState set only by event resolution, never direct player action
3. Tests / verification: captivityState parses correctly; endDay degrades condition at threshold; rescueNpc clears state; pregnancyState requires context field; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-pws9 (world NPC runtime state — captivityState lives here)

## Finding coverage
GD+ND+TC+SA captivity panels (both rounds): captivityState schema, recovery states, degradation model, pregnancy as rare world-generated aftermath
notes:
## Update (2026-05-14): pregnancyState schema addition

This bead is extended to also deliver `pregnancyState` on `NpcRuntimeState`, in addition to `captivityState`.

**Reason:** The content policy (docs/content-policy.md, round 2 panel) now explicitly IN-lists pregnancy from both consensual and non-consensual relationships as a modeled world state. The pregnancyState schema must exist before the Breeding Register events (destiny-3vyy) or the Mandatory Discovery Moment (destiny-jcrj) can function.

**pregnancyState schema addition to NpcRuntimeState:**
```ts
pregnancyState: z.object({
  conceptionDay: z.number(),
  daysElapsed: z.number().default(0),
  otherParentId: z.string().nullable(),  // npcId, 'player', or null (unknown)
  context: z.enum(['consensual', 'unknown']),  // 'unknown' = captivity aftermath; never surfaced to player as label
  registrationStatus: z.enum(['unregistered', 'registered', 'concealed', 'assessed']).default('unregistered'),
  permitObtained: z.boolean().default(false),
}).nullable().default(null)
```

**Policy constraints on `context: 'unknown'`:**
- The string 'unknown' is an internal code. It must never be displayed to the player in any UI element, tooltip, or label.
- The Mandatory Discovery Moment (destiny-jcrj) is the only authored path through which the player learns about this state.
- No UI panel may show `pregnancyState` at all before the discovery event has fired for that NPC.

**Duration:** pregnancyState ticks `daysElapsed` each day via endDay. At daysElapsed = 270 (standard gestation at 1:1 day ratio) OR daysElapsed = 90 (compressed — configurable), the birth event fires.

**`registrationStatus` flow:**
unregistered → registered (player registers, first child free or permit paid)
unregistered → concealed (player chooses concealment)
concealed → registered (player pays after deciding to come clean)
concealed → assessed (Compact discovers; player fails to pay)
any → assessed (player chooses Option E — let be assessed)

**Schema change checklist applies.**

title: House Merrow: author rival lineage faction as world entry
description:
## Why
House Valdris is returning to a city that has redistributed its power. No faction currently models the specific resentment of a house that quietly claimed Valdris authority during the Breach and now sees that claim threatened. Harlen Voss is a creditor enemy and Lady Sorn is an old-money structural enemy — but neither models the 'quietly nursing a legitimacy claim' archetype. Without a named rival lineage, the ambient hostility the player faces has no coherent origin.

## What
Author House Merrow of Ash Quay as a world-level rival lineage entry. This is a CONTENT and WORLD BUILDING bead — no new faction schema, no new faction mechanics. House Merrow is authored as a named entity that existing and new NPCs can reference.

### House Merrow — Authored Definition

**Full name:** House Merrow of the Ash Quay
**Type:** Civic-mercantile dynasty, not old nobility
**Origin:** Once dock inspectors and berth-masters in Harbor Ward. Became customs brokers and marriage witnesses after the Breach.
**Claim:** During the Breach, a city emergency order recognized the Merrow seal as the lawful replacement for Valdris authority over district tolls and succession disputes. They have held that de facto authority for years.
**Why they haven't moved openly:** The claim is shaky without the right records. A returning House Valdris means the original authority documents may still exist. They prefer delay, purchase, and sabotage over direct confrontation.
**Methods:** Debt acquisition, document purchase, quiet discrediting, arranged marriages into Compact positions, and — when necessary — contracts with people like Harlen Voss.
**What they fear:** That Orven Pell's ledger, Torvald Messe's copy, or Brannic Thule's testimony exist and can be authenticated.

### Authored world entries to create/update:
1. Add a `factionNote` or world lore entry for House Merrow (location: docs or data/definitions/)
2. Update Harlen Voss's bonds: add { type:'handler', targetId:'house-merrow', note:'Voss debt operations serve Merrow interests — not always by contract, but by alignment' }
3. Update Lady Sorn's bonds: add { type:'rival', targetId:'house-merrow', note:'Sorn and Merrow compete for the same legitimacy space. They cooperate only when Valdris is the target.' }
4. Optional: add one minor Merrow-affiliated NPC as a 'face' contact (not a named enemy — a functionary who delivers Merrow pressure indirectly)

## Why now
Several new NPCs (Orven Pell, Alis Vey, Veyran Malk) reference Merrow-adjacent pressure without naming it. destiny-4hiq (exteriorState faction reactions) needs a named faction to react to house tier advances. House Merrow is the named source of the 'faction visitor at tier 3' milestone.

## Player impact
- The ambient hostility the player faces is legible: House Merrow is the quiet hand behind several setbacks
- Faction visitor at exteriorState tier 3 is now authored: a Merrow functionary, not a generic envoy
- The player can investigate and eventually confront Merrow's claim directly

## System impact
- data/definitions/ or docs/ — House Merrow world entry (JSON lore entry or markdown)
- data/definitions/npcs.json — bonds updates for Harlen Voss and Lady Sorn (Merrow-aligned)
- No new faction schema required unless Merrow is added to factions.json (evaluate at implementation)

## Does not include
- Merrow as a full faction with faction mechanics (future bead if Merrow becomes a major faction)
- Merrow combat encounters
- Full Merrow NPC roster
- Resolution of the Merrow claim (late-game quest content)

## Evidence
WB panel section 6 (rival lineage): 'House Merrow of the Ash Quay — prefers delay, purchase, and sabotage over direct confrontation.' ND synthesis: 'the faction visitor at tier 3 now has a name.' Panel consensus: the ambient hostility needs a coherent origin.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** House Merrow referenced by name in at least one authored world entry or NPC background; Merrow's claim described in accessible lore
2. **System / technical:** Merrow world entry authored; Voss and Sorn bonds updated with Merrow references; no schema changes required
3. **Tests / verification:** npcs.json parses without error after bond updates; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
Lineage panel — WB section 6 (rival lineage problem), ND synthesis (ambient hostility origin)

## Fiction contract
House Merrow is not a villain faction. They are a bureaucratic dynasty that filled a legitimacy vacuum and now cannot afford to give it back. They do not hate the player — they simply cannot afford for the player to succeed.

title: Author lineage backstories for 9 existing NPCs in npcs.json
description:
## Why
Nine existing NPCs have backgrounds that imply generational depth but state nothing specific. Their `motivation` fields have generic publicGoal/privateNeed text that doesn't reference their connection to House Valdris, the Breach, or each other. The lineage panel produced specific, authoritative backstory decisions for each of them. These decisions must be written into npcs.json now — before destiny-nw4m (bond authoring) is implemented — because the bonds reference these backstories directly.

## What
Update the following 9 NPCs in data/definitions/npcs.json:

### 1. Old Maret (npc-old-maret)
- background: Update to specify she was lower household accounts keeper for House Valdris. Survived by knowing who was starving before they admitted it. Falsified grain ledgers during a pre-Breach winter riot to keep the house fed. Last living witness to the Valdris servants hall.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To give her granddaughter the ledger before she dies — without admitting she knows she has it.'
- quirks: retain existing, optionally add: 'refers to the old Valdris hall rooms by name as if she still works there'

### 2. Sister Vael (npc-sister-vael)
- background: Update to specify her 'foreknowledge' comes from reading confessions, not visions. Has a dead sister erased from temple records after the Breach — smuggled out under plague blankets. Her information network is built entirely on confessions she was never meant to keep.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To find the name of the patron who arranged her sister's erasure before she runs out of time to matter.'

### 3. Lira Ashcroft (npc-lira-ashcroft)
- background: Had Lirien outside a sanctioned marriage. The Ashcroft name was kept through a debt-forgiveness bargain with a surviving noble patron. Left the old district to protect Lirien from whatever the family owes. Knows why the Ashcrofts were allowed to keep their name when others were stripped.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To die before Lirien finds out what name they were almost given instead.'

### 4. Lirien Ashcroft (npc-lirien-ashcroft)
- background: Lira's daughter, born outside sanctioned marriage. The wrist-marking is a survival tally — dates of deaths in her family, debt notices, or names she is not supposed to remember. Refuses family discussion in groups because she knows the real scandal is not the name but the blood.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To find out whether she is heiress or evidence — and whether it matters either way.'

### 5. Maret Sunne (npc-maret-sunne)
- background: Bastard granddaughter of Old Maret, raised under a different surname to hide paternity. Became a clerk/archivist because she inherited Old Maret's memory for prices, names, and omissions. Holds a ledger that proves who profited from the fall of House Valdris. Does not know Old Maret knows she has it.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To understand what the ledger means before using it — and who she would become if she did.'

### 6. Garet Doyle (npc-garet-doyle)
- background: Son of an innkeeper whose establishment served Valdris retainers before the Breach. The locked box behind the bar contains service tokens, ring fragments, and unpaid writs from the old house. Memorises orders because his father memorised noble table habits for a Valdris patron who later vanished.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To return the box to someone who will know what to do with it without making him responsible for what happens next.'

### 7. Marion Vale (npc-marion-vale)
- background: 'Before the docks' means she survived the taking of the old harbor. Her family handled illicit cargo for a noble house and knows which house shipped people — not goods — out of Valdenmoor during the first panic. The dock work is legitimate cover for a network built on that original contract.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To never be the person who made that cargo run again — and to never admit she still could.'

### 8. Torvald Messe (npc-torvald-messe)
- background: Son of a municipal notary who copied all noble grants before the Breach. Three copies of every document exists because his family lost everything to forged records. Knows which legal form erased House Valdris the first time — and has kept a copy of that form.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To hand the copy to someone who can use it before his nerve fails. He has been waiting for the right person for years.'

### 9. Harlen Voss (npc-enemy-harlen-voss)
- background: Generational creditor lineage. His house loaned into ruin before the Breach and survived by purchasing Valdris arrears at collapse price. He is the descendant of the man who purchased the Valdris default and turned it into a standing claim. His wealth began with a coerced signature under duress — making his entire claim legally void if anyone can prove the circumstances.
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To complete the foreclosure before anyone finds the coercion papers.'

Lady Sorn update is intentionally NOT in this bead — her background is already hostile and specific. A separate enemy content bead should handle her if needed.

## Why now
destiny-nw4m (bond authoring) references these backstories directly — the bonds only make sense if the backing narrative is in the NPC definition. These updates must precede bond authoring.

## Player impact
- NPC backgrounds in the detail panel carry specific, credible history
- Old Maret's connection to House Valdris becomes discoverable through dialogue
- The Ashcroft mother/daughter relationship is readable from the detail panel
- Torvald Messe's copy of the Valdris erasure document becomes a quest hook

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json — background, motivation.privateNeed, and optionally quirks updates for 9 NPCs
- No schema changes
- No runtime changes

## Does not include
- Bond array authoring (destiny-nw4m)
- New NPC creation (destiny-h2vi)
- Dialogue authoring for these NPCs
- Any changes to stats or traits

## Evidence
Lineage panel ND Part 1 (existing NPC lineage audit): specific authored backstory decisions for each of the 9 NPCs listed. All confirmed as authoritative decisions, not suggestions.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** NPC detail panel backgrounds read with specific Valdenmoor history; Old Maret's Valdris connection is present; Lira/Lirien relationship is readable
2. **System / technical:** All 9 NPC definitions updated in npcs.json; npcDefinitionSchema.parse() passes on full file; no field removals
3. **Tests / verification:** npcs.json parses without errors; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
Lineage panel — ND Part 1 (Old Maret, Sister Vael, Lira Ashcroft, Lirien Ashcroft, Maret Sunne, Garet Doyle, Marion Vale, Torvald Messe, Harlen Voss)

title: Author 5 new lineage NPCs: Orven Pell, Alis Vey, Brannic Thule, Veyran Malk, Cessa Rill
description:
## Why
Five specific archetypes are absent from the current NPC roster that a generationally credible Valdenmoor requires. These are not generic additions — each one holds a piece of the city's written, oral, legal, or grudge-memory that no existing NPC covers. Without them, the Mira lineage quest has no supporting cast, the legal erasure of House Valdris has no witness, and the generational enemy logic has no named face.

## What
Add 5 new NPCs to data/definitions/npcs.json with full authored definitions (all required schema fields including ageBand, sex, appearanceTags, quirks, motivation, bonds):

### 1. Orven Pell — The Archivist Who Kept the Ledger
- id: npc-orven-pell
- ageBand: elder | sex: man
- origin: Archive district (municipal quarter)
- npcType: world
- background: A bent record-keeper who can recite old property lines and burial permissions from memory. Survived the Breach by being too useful to remove.
- appearanceTags: ['ink-yellowed fingers', 'coat with too many interior pockets', 'never closes a door completely']
- quirks: ['answers questions about records with the filing date first', 'has memorised which ledgers burned and which merely vanished']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'Retirement. Obscurity. One more quiet season.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To give the ledger to someone who will use it before he dies.'
- bonds: [{ type:'witness', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Kept the one unburned ledger naming which house benefited from the Valdris erasure' }]
- Connection: Knows exactly how House Valdris was legally erased. Holds the unburned ledger.

### 2. Alis Vey — The Dispossessed Ally
- id: npc-alis-vey
- ageBand: middle | sex: woman
- origin: Former noble quarter
- npcType: world
- background: Her house once served as a cadet ally to House Valdris. Sold Valdris property under coercion during the Breach. Now lives like a clerk and claims no servants. Wants restitution before she can confess.
- appearanceTags: ['widow black without mourning status', 'good boots worn past their use', 'a crest ring worn face-inward']
- quirks: ['speaks of her house in the past tense without affect', 'keeps a record of every favor she has received since the Breach']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'To clear a name she no longer uses.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'Restitution before confession. She must give something back before she can speak.'
- bonds: [{ type:'rival', targetId:'npc-enemy-lady-sorn', note:'Sorn pressured the sale. Alis has not forgiven it.' }, { type:'witness', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Sold Valdris property under coercion. Holds the original transfer documents.' }]

### 3. Brannic Thule — The Old Steward
- id: npc-brannic-thule
- ageBand: elder | sex: man
- origin: Old household quarter
- npcType: world
- background: Former steward's assistant at House Valdris. Now works as a repairman who recognises silver by touch and timber by smell. The only living person who served in the Valdris halls and is still in Valdenmoor.
- appearanceTags: ['heavy hands that move carefully around anything old', 'a steward badge worn under his shirt', 'stops to assess every room he enters']
- quirks: ['addresses the player by the correct honorific from the first meeting', 'names every room in a building by its old function before its current one']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'Useful work. A dry room. Nothing owed.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To deliver the artifacts before he dies. He swore an oath.'
- bonds: [{ type:'witness', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Served in Valdris halls before the fall. Knows where family seals and heir artifacts were hidden.' }]
- Connection: The only living person who knows where the Valdris heir artifacts are hidden.

### 4. Ser Veyran Malk — The Polite Creditor
- id: npc-veyran-malk
- ageBand: middle | sex: man
- npcType: enemy (or world — escalates to enemy)
- origin: Credit-house district
- background: A debt enforcer from a family ruined by House Valdris in pre-Breach power games. Hates the return of Valdris as historical revenge, not ideology. Polite always. Never loud. His father forged the paper trail that destroyed the old house — which means if anyone finds that paper, Malk's grievance becomes an embarrassment.
- appearanceTags: ['clean coat, no badge', 'speaks with pauses that feel practised', 'always faces the door']
- quirks: ['thanks people before delivering ultimatums', 'never repeats a threat — once is sufficient']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'Collection of legitimate outstanding accounts.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To see House Valdris fail again, quietly, with paperwork.'
- bonds: [{ type:'rival', targetId:'house-valdris-fall', note:'Family ruined by Valdris in pre-Breach dealings. Father forged the paper trail that destroyed the house — making the grudge a liability if exposed.' }]

### 5. Cessa Rill — The Registrar of the Pale
- id: npc-cessa-rill
- ageBand: middle | sex: woman
- districtId: district-the-pale
- npcType: world
- background: Quiet keeper of baptisms, adoptions, and death names in the Pale archive chapel. Can prove or erase a maternal lineage. She altered one noble birth entry years ago to save a child from inheritance law — a decision she has lived with since.
- appearanceTags: ['archive dust on dark robes', 'ink that does not wash from her left thumb', 'reads people like entries']
- quirks: ['corrects names silently and never aloud', 'knows which records have been reviewed and by whom, and when']
- motivation.publicGoal: 'Accuracy. Completeness. The record as it should be.'
- motivation.privateNeed: 'To know whether the alteration she made saved or condemned.'
- bonds: [{ type:'witness', targetId:'npc-mira', note:'Holds or altered the birth record Mira was tracking. May be the final link in the maternal lineage chain.' }]

## Why now
The Mira quest arc (the game's central story) requires NPCs who hold physical evidence of the lineage chain. Brannic Thule (artifacts) and Cessa Rill (birth records) are the two most directly quest-critical. Orven Pell and Alis Vey provide the legal and moral flanks of the Valdris erasure story. Veyran Malk gives the ambient hostility a face that isn't Voss or Sorn.

## Player impact
- Orven Pell: discoverable contact who can explain the legal erasure of House Valdris
- Alis Vey: potential ally or confession source for the property transfer documents
- Brannic Thule: quest-critical NPC who delivers heir artifacts when trust threshold is met
- Veyran Malk: escalating enemy who provides a 'polite threat' register distinct from Voss
- Cessa Rill: the final gatekeeper of the maternal lineage record Mira was seeking

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json — add 5 new full NPC definitions
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json — if any of these are world NPCs with dialogue, add districtId entries
- NPC count goes from 31 to 36
- No schema changes required (all fields already in npcDefinitionSchema)

## Does not include
- Authored dialogue scripts (separate content bead per NPC)
- Quest triggers or discovery conditions (separate quest bead)
- Runtime relationship arcs for these NPCs
- House Merrow as a faction entry (separate bead)

## Evidence
Lineage panel ND Part 3: 5 specific NPC gaps with authored pitches. WB section 5: matching institutional roles (archive, oral memory, debt, grudge, birth records).

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** All 5 NPCs appear in their correct districts in the world view; Brannic Thule is locatable in the old household quarter
2. **System / technical:** All 5 NPC definitions parse against npcDefinitionSchema without errors; all required fields present; bonds reference valid targetIds or canonical ghost-IDs
3. **Tests / verification:** npcs.json parses cleanly; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
Lineage panel — ND Part 3 (NPC gaps: Orven Pell, Alis Vey, Brannic Thule, Ser Veyran Malk, Cessa Rill), WB section 5 (generational fabric gaps)

title: House prestige: derived selector, faction gating, and renown split
description:
## Why
'Prestige' and 'renown' are conceptually distinct but not mechanically separated. `renown` on `playerCharacter` conflates personal reputation with house standing. There is no `housePrestige` field or selector. Content cannot be gated on house standing. Faction contracts, NPC recruitment quality, and social access are ungated. The house growing from ruin to legitimacy has no mechanical output that the rest of the game reads.

## What
House prestige is a **derived metric** — never stored, always computed.

```
housePrestige = f(
  repaired rooms × tier weight,
  roomFunctions assigned (count + type weight),
  exteriorState tier value,
  installedHouseModules count,
)
```

Prestige score maps to 5 descriptive tiers: Collapsed / Occupied / Established / Recognized / Prominent.

Renown remains stored on `playerCharacter` — it is the *player's* personal reputation. Prestige is the *house's* standing. They are separate axes.

Content gating by prestige tier:
- Collapsed: no faction contracts, no specialist NPCs available for recruitment
- Occupied: petty work, faction envoys begin to notice
- Established: mid-tier faction contracts, reputation-sensitive NPCs willing to talk
- Recognized: high-tier contracts, social scenes unlocked, political leverage in negotiation
- Prominent: senior guild contacts, faction dependencies, social scene access; also maximum raid pressure

selectHousePrestige(state): { score: number, tier: HousePrestigeTier, label: string }
selectContentGates(state): { canRecruit: string[], canAccessContracts: ContractTier[], socialScenesUnlocked: boolean }

## Why now
Prestige gating is the economic backbone of Phase 3. Without it, all content is available from day 1 and house progression has no mechanical output. This bead is the selector layer that makes all other house progression beads meaningful to the rest of the game.

## Player impact
- The house panel shows prestige tier and score with a breakdown
- Certain NPCs, missions, and faction contacts are clearly gated on house tier (not hidden — communicated)
- The player understands: fixing and equipping the house opens more of the world

## System impact
- src/application/selectors/house.ts — selectHousePrestige, selectContentGates (new selectors, no schema change)
- Content gating in: NPC recruitment filter, faction contract filter, social scene unlock conditions
- src/ui/ — house panel shows prestige tier with readable breakdown

## Does not include
- Storing prestige (always derived)
- Collapsing renown and prestige into one field
- Prestige decay mechanics (future bead if ever needed)
- Automatic prestige-triggered scripted events (those are in destiny-4hiq exteriorState bead)

## Evidence
GD/SA panel: prestige is derived, not stored. ND: separate renown (personal) from prestige (house). Panel consensus: prestige unlocks better NPC quality, faction contracts, social scenes, political leverage. Every prestige upgrade must also carry friction (at least one authored NPC/faction provocation) — authored in destiny-4hiq.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** House panel shows prestige tier label and contributing factors; gated content is visibly blocked with a reason (e.g., 'Requires Established standing')
2. **System / technical:** selectHousePrestige returns correct score and tier for all 5 tier thresholds; selectContentGates returns correct unlock list; no prestige stored on GameState
3. **Tests / verification:** selectHousePrestige tested for each tier boundary; selectContentGates returns correct gates at each tier; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-4wlm (room functions contribute to prestige score)
destiny-4hiq (exteriorState contributes to prestige score)
destiny-f3ti (installedHouseModules contribute to prestige score)

## Finding coverage
House progression panel — prestige model and renown/prestige split

title: House fortificationLevel and raid event system
description:
## Why
The house has no defense model. Factions and criminals cannot threaten it mechanically. `fortificationLevel` does not exist on `houseStateSchema`. NPCs have no 'defense duty' assignment. As house prestige grows (exteriorState), it attracts more attention — but there is no system to model, resolve, or give weight to that threat. The risk of building something is currently invisible.

## What
Add `fortificationLevel: z.number().int().min(0).max(5).default(0)` to `houseStateSchema`.

Defense rating formula:
```
defenseRating = fortificationLevel × hardwareWeight + (NPCs on defense duty) × crewWeight + renown deterrence modifier
```

Guard assignment: reuse `NpcRuntimeState.assignment` — NPCs assigned to 'defense' duty count toward defenseRating. No new schema needed for guards.

Three authored raid types:
1. **Faction enforcement** — collectors, envoys, legal predators. Target: ledgers, vault documents, proof of ownership. Arrives with a warrant and hired blades.
2. **Criminal opportunists** — night thieves who believe a rebuilding house still hides silver. Scale with exteriorState (more visible → more frequent, but also easier to deter).
3. **The Remainder** — a curse, witness, or hungry thing tied to the original house collapse. Returns when the house resumes breathing. Not a burglar — a *remainder*.

Authored raid scene: 'The Notary's Knock' — a debt-office clerk with a sealed warrant and three hired blades. They target the vault and ledgers. Defense here means protecting *legitimacy*, not HP.

Defense cost: NPCs on guard duty cannot go on missions (crew opportunity cost). Fortification improvements cost materials/gold and may require specific room functions (vault, barracks).

Defense feedback loop: higher exteriorState → more raid probability → defense choices matter more → crew on guard = crew unavailable → strategic tension.

## Why now
Without a defense system, growing the house has no downside cost. Prestige without threat is just reward accumulation. This bead gives the 'contested legitimacy' phase of house progression its mechanical teeth.

## Player impact
- The player sees raid risk increase as the house becomes more visible
- Guard assignment creates a genuine crew trade-off: you can't raid and defend at once
- A successful defense protects the vault, ledgers, and legitimacy; a failed defense costs resources and opens plot threads
- The Remainder raid is a horror beat tied to the house's origin — it cannot be deterred by fortification alone

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts — add fortificationLevel to houseStateSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json — add fortificationLevel: 0
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts — ensure 'defense' is a valid assignment value
- src/application/selectors/house.ts — selectDefenseRating: fortificationLevel + guards on duty + renown modifier
- src/application/commands/resolveRaid.ts — new command: compares defenseRating vs raid strength, produces outcome
- Authored raid events: faction enforcement, criminal opportunists, The Remainder (3 authored event scripts)
- Raid probability tied to exteriorState (selector)

## Does not include
- Tower/wall placement or spatial defense layout
- Per-room breach mechanics
- Full combat resolution inside the house (use existing combat system outcome hooks)
- Raid scheduling beyond probability + threshold triggers

## Evidence
WB panel: 3 specific raid types with authored identities. ND panel: 'The Notary's Knock' as canonical legal raid. GD panel: defense must cost crew slots to create real trade-off. WB: 'The Remainder' as the horror thread tied to original house collapse.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Raid risk visible in house view; guard assignment depletes available crew; raid events fire with authored text; outcomes affect vault/ledger/legitimacy resources
2. **System / technical:** fortificationLevel on houseStateSchema and initial-game-state.json; selectDefenseRating selector; resolveRaid command with 3 raid type branches; 'defense' valid NPC assignment
3. **Tests / verification:** selectDefenseRating computes correctly; resolveRaid produces loss on 0 defense vs high-strength raid; resolveRaid produces win on high defenseRating; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-4wlm (room functions — barracks/vault contribute to fortification)
destiny-4hiq (exteriorState — raid probability scales with exterior tier)

## Finding coverage
House progression panel — fortification model, raid types, defense cost loop

## Fiction contract
Defense is not a tower-defense game. It is a question of whether the house has enough legitimacy, personnel, and physical presence to make an attack too costly. The Notary's Knock is a legal raid — the enemy is paperwork and a warrant, not a siege engine. The Remainder is not defeated by fortification.

title: House exteriorState: world-legible tiers and faction reactions
description:
## Why
The house has no outward-facing state. The district cannot 'see' House Valdris improve. Factions have no trigger point to notice the house or react to it. Prestige upgrades produce no friction — no authored NPC or faction scene responds to the house becoming more visible. The absence of an exterior model means the house exists only for the player, not in the world.

## What
Add `exteriorState: z.enum(['ruined','patched','maintained','restored','grand']).default('ruined')` to `houseStateSchema`.

Four world-legible milestone thresholds:
1. **patched** — district stops calling it a ruin. Lights on at night.
2. **maintained** — petty criminals stop using the courtyard. The lane clears when someone from the house appears.
3. **restored** — a faction sends a 'visitor' (not an ambassador, not a thug). Someone who measures and remembers names.
4. **grand** — 'near Valdris', 'under Valdris eyes' — the house is now part of the district's working power map.

exteriorState advances when: room repair + assigned functions cross a threshold (selector-computed, not manual). It does not advance by spending money alone.

Every tier advance triggers at least one authored reaction:
- The Painted Door: restoring heraldry increases renown but provokes a creditor, a servant, and a faction note
- The Velvet Room: restoring the master's chamber causes minor nobles to treat the house as political; kitchen staff still sleep on stone

exteriorState feeds into: faction visit quality, suspicion/inspection frequency, prestige selector, raid probability.

## Why now
exteriorState is the missing link between interior house work (rooms, modules) and the world reacting to the house. Without it, prestige has no hook into faction behavior, and the 'lights on → name on the map' arc has no mechanical backbone.

## Player impact
- The district reacts visibly as the house improves: dialogue shifts, faction contact quality increases, threats change in character
- Prestige upgrades feel consequential because someone always notices — and not always approvingly
- The player faces authored friction: improving the exterior invites attention from creditors, factions, and enemies

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts — add exteriorState to houseStateSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json — add exteriorState: 'ruined'
- src/application/selectors/house.ts — selectExteriorTier: derives from room states + assigned functions; selectHousePrestige uses exteriorState as a factor
- src/application/commands/advanceExteriorState.ts — called by selector-gated trigger when threshold crossed
- Authored faction/NPC reaction events — minimum 1 authored scene per tier transition (content)
- src/domain/events/ or equivalent — milestone event system (if not yet present)

## Does not include
- Freeform cosmetic choices for the exterior
- Per-room visible-from-outside state
- Exterior combat fortifications (separate bead: fortificationLevel)
- Full faction standing overhaul

## Evidence
WB panel: four world-legible tiers — 'lights on → criminals leave → faction sends visitor → name on the map.' ND panel: 'The Painted Door' and 'The Velvet Room' as canonical prestige friction events. Every prestige upgrade must have at least one NPC or faction reaction that treats it as a provocation.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** exteriorState visible in house view; faction/NPC dialogue acknowledges house tier; at least 1 authored scene fires per tier transition
2. **System / technical:** exteriorState on houseStateSchema and initial-game-state.json; selectExteriorTier derives from rooms + functions; advanceExteriorState command; selectHousePrestige incorporates exteriorState
3. **Tests / verification:** selectExteriorTier returns correct tier for given room states; tier advance triggers milestone event; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-4wlm (room functions — exteriorState threshold includes assigned functions)

## Finding coverage
House progression panel — exteriorState and faction friction model

## Fiction contract
The house is not a building site — it is a statement about whether House Valdris intends to survive. Every visible improvement is also a provocation. The world is watching, and it has opinions.

title: Room function schema: assign purpose to repaired rooms
description:
## Why
houseRoomSchema has `state` (repair status) but no concept of what a room *does* after repair. All rooms are mechanically equivalent once intact. Crew capacity is derived only from `rosterBonus` — a single opaque integer with no room-level origin. The house cannot model specialization: a barracks, a study, and a kitchen are indistinguishable in the domain.

## What
Add `roomFunction: RoomFunction | null` to `houseRoomSchema` (nullable, default null). Define a `roomFunctionSchema` enum with 9 values. Wire room functions into a crew capacity formula replacing the opaque `rosterBonus` as the primary expansion driver.

Crew capacity formula:
```
capacity = base(4) + rosterBonus + sum(roomFunction bonuses)
```

Room function bonuses:
- quarters / barracks: +2 roster capacity each
- kitchen: morale recovery rate modifier
- study: training skill multiplier
- workshop: unlocks gear repair action
- archive: document storage limit + evidence tracking bonus
- infirmary: NPC injury recovery speed modifier
- vault: valuables protection (raid defense bonus)
- reception: prestige modifier for faction visits

roomFunction can only be set when `room.state === 'intact'`. Assigning a function to a damaged room is rejected by the command.

## Why now
destiny-f3ti (house modules) and destiny-xqa6 (carry limits) both need a house capacity model that goes beyond a single integer. Without room functions, expanding the house means editing `rosterBonus` by hand — an opaque field with no player-facing explanation. Room functions also unblock the prestige selector (house prestige needs to count assigned functions).

## Player impact
- After repairing a room, the player assigns its function from a short menu
- Crew capacity is displayed as a live formula: '6 base (4 + quarters + barracks) — 3 filled'
- The house view shows each room's function badge next to its repair state
- Certain missions or events are gated on having a specific room function (e.g., 'your infirmary can treat this wound')

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts — add roomFunctionSchema enum and roomFunction nullable field to houseRoomSchema
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json — all 11 rooms get roomFunction: null
- src/application/commands/assignRoomFunction.ts — new command: validates intact state, sets function
- src/application/selectors/house.ts — selectCrewCapacity: computes base + rosterBonus + function bonuses
- src/ui/screens/Dashboard or house panel — room function assignment UI (menu per intact room)

## Does not include
- Room adjacency or placement mechanics
- More than 9 defined room functions
- Automatic function assignment (always player-initiated)
- Visual floor plan rendering

## Evidence
GD/SA panel: current `rosterBonus` is an opaque bucket with no origin or ceiling. Room functions give crew capacity a spatial, explainable basis. SA: nullable with default(null) is low save risk — additive field on .strict() schema.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** Intact rooms show an 'Assign Function' option; crew capacity in the house view updates after assignment; rooms display their assigned function badge
2. **System / technical:** roomFunctionSchema enum in contracts.ts; houseRoomSchema has roomFunction nullable field; all 11 rooms in initial-game-state.json have roomFunction: null; assignRoomFunction command rejects non-intact rooms; selectCrewCapacity returns correct sum
3. **Tests / verification:** assignRoomFunction sets function on intact room; rejects damaged/stripped room; selectCrewCapacity sums bonuses correctly; test count does not drop below 403

## Finding coverage
House progression panel — room function mechanic (Phase 2 core)

## Fiction contract
A room's function is a choice about what kind of house this will be, not a passive unlock. The barracks says we expect violence. The study says we intend to be here long enough to learn something. The infirmary says someone here is worth saving.

title: Consumable field use in missions: injury choice, scarcity moment, named consequence
description:
## Why
Consumables can be packed into NPC loadout slots (3 per NPC) before a mission, but they are never used during or after a mission. An NPC can return injured regardless of whether healing salves were packed. The most narratively charged moment in the item system — 'do I use the last salve now, or save it?' — does not exist. Consumables are inert cargo.

## What
Consumables packed in loadout slots become usable during mission resolution:
- After a mission encounter where an NPC is injured, a 'use consumable' decision point is offered if the NPC has a heal-type consumable in their loadout
- Player chooses: apply salve now (NPC healed, item consumed, quantity decremented) or save it (NPC remains injured, item preserved)
- The choice and its outcome are recorded in the activity log with authored prose
- Consumable with a stat_mod effect (e.g. stimulant) can be pre-used before a mission phase, applying the modifier for that phase
- Consumed items are removed from ownedItems (quantity decremented to zero removes the entry)

## Why now
This bead makes the entire item system feel real to the player. destiny-5zru provides the typed effects and useItem pipeline. This bead provides the first authored decision moment where an item has weight — a specific NPC, a specific injury, a specific cost. Without this, consumables remain expensive tokens that sit in loadout slots forever.

## Player impact
- After a fight, the game prompts: 'Mara is bleeding. You have 1 Bitter Salve. Use it now?'
- Using it: 'You unstopped the salve. Mara steadied. The last of the herb smell faded.' Mara's injury state decreases; item is consumed.
- Not using it: 'You kept the salve. Mara's wound tightened overnight.' Mara carries the injury forward.
- The player feels the arithmetic of survival — not a stat screen, a named person and a dwindling resource.

## System impact
- src/application/commands/expedition.ts or a new resolveExpeditionOutcome.ts — add consumable decision step to mission resolution
- src/application/commands/useItem.ts (from destiny-5zru) — called here to resolve heal effect and decrement quantity
- src/ui/screens/ — a decision prompt component during mission resolution (inline, not a full modal)
- Activity log: authored prose for 'salve used on [NPC name]' and 'salve saved, [NPC name] wounded'

## Does not include
- Mid-mission real-time inventory browsing
- Using all consumable effect types (heal only in this bead)
- NPC-initiated consumable use (NPCs don't self-medicate)
- Consumable use outside of mission context (rest/camp use is a separate bead)

## Fiction contract
The salve is not 'spent as HP.' It is the smell of bitter herbs over blood and wet stone. The player chose who gets to continue being real. The activity log must reflect the named NPC, not a generic 'consumable used' message.

## Evidence
ND panel: 'The inventory makes it meaningful if the item is scarce enough to hurt, the target is named, and the consequence is visible later.' 'The player should feel they did not spend a consumable. They chose who gets to continue being real.' GD panel: consumables in field are the core tension of expedition carry. UX panel: 'Allow pause/turn-based inventory use during mission resolution. Use only items already in that NPC's 3 slots.'

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** After a mission encounter with an injured NPC who has a heal consumable, a decision prompt appears; choosing to use it applies the heal and removes the item; the activity log shows authored prose with the NPC's name
2. **System / technical:** useItem called with heal effect during mission resolution; quantity decremented; ownedItem removed when quantity reaches 0; decision prompt only fires when NPC has applicable consumable in loadout
3. **Tests / verification:** useItem heal path decrements quantity; quantity-0 removes entry from ownedItems; mission with no consumable produces no prompt; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-5zru (useItem command + typed heal effect)
destiny-jcer (ownedItems model — quantity decrement and removal)

## Finding coverage
ND panel finding (salve decision scene — named NPC, scarcity, visible consequence), GD finding (consumables in field as core expedition tension), UX finding (mid-mission consumable use from loadout slots only)
acceptance_criteria:
- Decision prompt fires after mission encounter when injured NPC has heal consumable in loadout
- Using it applies heal effect and decrements/removes item from ownedItems
- Activity log shows authored prose with NPC name
- Tests cover: apply + decrement, no prompt without consumable, quantity-0 removal
- Test count does not drop below 403; typecheck clean; build clean

title: Item management UI: HouseStoragePanel, MissionPackPanel, ItemCard with contextual actions
description:
## Why
There is no inventory screen. Items appear only in the shop/purchase flow and the NPC loadout. Players cannot see what they own, where it is stored, what they can do with it, or what they are bringing on a mission. The typed item effects (destiny-5zru) and unified item model (destiny-jcer) exist in the backend with no player-facing surface.

## What
A two-context inventory UI is added across existing screens — not a new standalone inventory screen:
- HouseStoragePanel: tab or collapsible section on Dashboard, shows items with location:'house_storage' + capacity bar
- MissionPackPanel: module inside MissionPrepScreen, shows items being packed with category CarryLimitBars
- ItemCard component: icon, name, category badge, primary action CTA, overflow menu (⋯) for secondary actions
- Primary actions by category: consumable→Use, document→Open (then File/Present/Burn), gift→Give, tool→Equip, householdModule→Install in House, trade good→Sell
- TargetPickerModal: opens when a use/give action requires selecting an NPC target
- InventoryDrawer: compact global access icon in nav header, opens a slide-over or modal with both house/pack views

## Why now
The typed effects and unified ownership model are only player-facing if there is a UI surface for them. This bead closes the loop: items can be used, items are visible, item actions are accessible from the right context. Without this bead, destiny-5zru and destiny-jcer deliver zero visible player value.

## Player impact
- Player opens the house view and sees what is stored, how much space remains, and installed modules
- Before a mission, the player sees the mission pack with carry bars per category
- On an ItemCard, one tap/click shows the primary action; rare actions are hidden behind ⋯
- Giving a gift opens a target picker showing available NPCs — the player chooses Marion or Verek
- The global nav shows a small inventory icon the player can tap any time for quick access

## System impact
- src/ui/components/ItemCard.tsx — new reusable component
- src/ui/components/ItemActionMenu.tsx — primary + secondary actions driven by item category
- src/ui/components/TargetPickerModal.tsx — NPC target selection for use/give actions
- src/ui/components/CarryLimitBar.tsx — from expedition carry bead; reused here
- src/ui/components/InstalledModulesPanel.tsx — from house storage bead; referenced here
- src/ui/components/InventoryDrawer.tsx — global compact access
- src/ui/screens/Dashboard.tsx — add HouseStoragePanel tab/section
- src/ui/screens/MissionPrepScreen.tsx — add MissionPackPanel module
- src/application/selectors/inventory.ts — selectItemsByLocation, selectItemActions(itemId): UseActionType[]

## Does not include
- Drag-and-drop (buttons are the minimum viable interaction)
- Animated inventory transitions
- Item comparison UI
- Crafting or repair UI (separate bead)
- Full NPC equipment management screen (separate bead)

## Evidence
UX panel: 'Primary home: Dashboard tab for house inventory; MissionPrepScreen module for mission carry. No standalone inventory screen competing with the core loop.' 'One primary action per item type — consumable→Use, document→Open, gift→Give, tool→Equip. Secondary actions behind ⋯.' 'Highest priority: MissionPrepScreen item/carry module — touches the highest-impact loop.' UX component set: InventoryDrawer, HouseStoragePanel, MissionPackPanel, ItemCard, ItemActionMenu, TargetPickerModal, CarryLimitBar, InstalledModulesPanel.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** House storage visible on Dashboard with capacity bar; MissionPrepScreen shows packing module with category carry bars; ItemCard shows primary action for at least consumable, document, and gift categories; TargetPickerModal opens for give/use-on-NPC actions
2. **System / technical:** ItemCard, ItemActionMenu, TargetPickerModal, InventoryDrawer components exist; selectItemsByLocation and selectItemActions selectors exist; HouseStoragePanel and MissionPackPanel integrated into their parent screens
3. **Tests / verification:** ItemCard renders primary action from category; selectItemsByLocation returns correct items for 'inventory' and 'house_storage'; TargetPickerModal renders NPC list; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-jcer (unified owned-item model — UI reads from ownedItems with location)
destiny-5zru (typed effects — actions are driven by effect types)
destiny-xqa6 (expedition carry limits — CarryLimitBar and MissionPackPanel reuse this bead's component)
destiny-f3ti (house storage — HouseStoragePanel reuses InstalledModulesPanel from this bead)

## Finding coverage
UX panel findings: inventory screen location (Dashboard tab), two-view pattern (HouseStoragePanel + MissionPackPanel), item action model (primary + overflow), carry limit feedback, highest-priority screen (MissionPrepScreen)
acceptance_criteria:
- HouseStoragePanel on Dashboard with capacity bar
- MissionPackPanel in MissionPrepScreen with category carry bars
- ItemCard with primary action for consumable/document/gift categories
- TargetPickerModal for use/give actions
- InventoryDrawer in global nav
- Tests pass; count does not drop below 403; typecheck clean; build clean

title: House storage capacity and household module installation
description:
## Why
The player's base is a collapsed noble house with no modeled storage capacity. Inventory has no house storage layer — items are in a flat unowned pile. The 3 householdModule items in the catalog (and any future modules) have no installation mechanic and produce no effect. The house as a progression space is inert.

## What
House storage becomes a capacity-limited, module-expandable layer:
- houseStorageCapacity: number on GameState (default: 40 slots)
- installedHouseModules: z.array({ moduleItemId, installedAtDay }).default([]) on GameState
- Installing a module: item moves from ownedItems (location:'inventory') to installedHouseModules; item is no longer in the storable pool
- Module effects are resolved via typedEffects (storage_expand, training_bonus, rest_quality_bonus) — read at compute time, not stored
- Authored fiction + effects for all 3 existing householdModule items (see below)
- InstalledModulesPanel shows installed modules and their active effects in the house/Dashboard view

## Why now
houseStorageCapacity gives the unified item model (destiny-jcer) a meaningful constraint at home. Without it, location:'house_storage' has no limit and the house is still just a bigger pocket. Module installation is the progression mechanic that makes the base feel like a place being reclaimed rather than a generic storage screen.

## Player impact
- The house has a visible storage limit; when it fills, the player must choose what to store vs sell/use
- Installing a salvaged strongbox expands storage — the player sees the capacity increase immediately
- Installed modules are listed in the house view with their active effects: '+12 house storage · Chamberlain's Chest installed Day 4'
- The house improves visibly over time as modules are installed

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts — add houseStorageCapacity: z.number().int().positive().default(40) and installedHouseModules: z.array(installedModuleSchema).default([])
- src/domain/items/contracts.ts — installedModuleSchema: { moduleItemId, installedAtDay }
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json — add houseStorageCapacity: 40, installedHouseModules: []
- src/application/commands/installModule.ts — new command: moves item from inventory to installedHouseModules, validates it is a householdModule category
- src/application/selectors/house.ts — selectHouseStorageInfo(state): { capacity, used, installedModules with effects }
- src/ui/screens/Dashboard (or house panel) — InstalledModulesPanel component
- data/definitions/items.json — all 3 householdModule items get authored fiction + typed storage_expand or training_bonus effects

## Fiction contracts for the 3 existing householdModule items
- Salvaged Strongbox — a heavy iron chest from the dead lord's counting room; now hides paper trails and surplus gear. Effect: storage_expand +12.
- Repaired Apothecary Cabinet — a damp cabinet from a ruined clinic, its labels still legible. Holds salves behind locked glass. Effect: storage_expand +8, rest_quality_bonus (NPC morale recovery +5%).
- Hidden Archive Drawer — a false drawer from the original library desk, unknown to the previous heirs. Used for writs, blackmail, and family secrets. Effect: storage_expand +6 (documents only), training_bonus (academics skill +10% multiplier).

## Does not include
- Raw weight tracking
- Per-NPC carry weight
- More than the 3 existing householdModule items (new content is a separate bead)
- Social/faction effects of installed modules (future bead)
- Workshop or repair station functionality

## Evidence
GD panel: modules should be 'functional, progression-bearing, house identity-defining.' ND panel: 'modules are discoveries and claims, not upgrades — they say we found this place, we repaired this place, we are making it ours.' SA panel: installedHouseModules on GameState (not derived from inventory) to avoid brittle bonus resolution. UX panel: InstalledModulesPanel on Dashboard showing name, effect, and install day.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** House storage capacity shown as current/max in house view; installing a module increases displayed capacity; InstalledModulesPanel lists active modules with their effects
2. **System / technical:** houseStorageCapacity and installedHouseModules on GameState and initial-game-state.json; installModule command validates category and moves item; selectHouseStorageInfo returns correct used/capacity; all 3 householdModule items have typed effects in items.json
3. **Tests / verification:** installModule moves item to installedHouseModules and removes from ownedItems; storage_expand effect increases effective capacity; installing non-module item is rejected; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-jcer (unified owned-item model — installation moves from ownedItems)
destiny-5zru (typed effects — module effects are typed ItemEffect instances)

## Finding coverage
Panel finding I-7 (house storage capacity), I-8 (module installation mechanic), I-9 (fiction contracts for 3 existing modules), ND fiction contract (modules as discovery and claim)
acceptance_criteria:
- houseStorageCapacity and installedHouseModules on GameState and initial-game-state.json
- installModule command validates category and transitions item correctly
- All 3 householdModule items have typed effects and authored descriptions in items.json
- InstalledModulesPanel shows installed modules and effects
- Tests pass; count does not drop below 403; typecheck clean; build clean

title: Expedition carry limits: slot/category caps enforced at mission departure
description:
## Why
Missions and expeditions have no carry limit. The player can pack an unlimited number of items for any mission. This removes meaningful expedition planning decisions, makes the item system irrelevant to the mission loop, and prevents the tension of choosing what to bring and what to leave. It is also inconsistent with the fiction: the crew hauls salvage through narrow alleys and past watchmen checkpoints.

## What
Slot/category-based expedition carry limits are added to the mission layer:
- MissionPrepScreen shows a carry summary per expedition
- Category limits (not raw weight): documents/evidence (5 slots), trade goods/materials (8 slots), tools (2 slots), consumables (governed by existing loadout slots per NPC)
- CarryLimitBar component shows current/max with yellow near limit, red when over
- Hard gate at mission departure: cannot launch if over limit
- timeCostSlots on missions (from destiny-dcj0) already exists by the time this lands

## Why now
Carry limits only create meaningful decisions if the item use pipeline (destiny-5zru) and unified item model (destiny-jcer) exist first — players need things worth choosing between. This bead implements the constraint layer that makes expedition planning a real decision.

## Player impact
- Before launching a mission, the player sees: 'Documents: 3/5 · Trade Goods: 8/8 · Tools: 1/2'
- Trying to over-pack shows a yellow warning and then a hard block
- The player must choose: take the evidence or take the salvage, not both
- The crew feels like a real squad with real limits, not an infinite luggage cart

## System impact
- src/domain/game/contracts.ts or separate expeditionSchema — expeditionCarryConfig: category limits (authored constants, not GameState fields)
- src/application/commands/expedition.ts — validate carry limits before departure
- src/ui/screens/MissionPrepScreen.tsx — CarryLimitBar per category, hard gate on launch
- src/ui/components/CarryLimitBar.tsx — new reusable component
- src/application/selectors/ — selectExpeditionCarryLoad(state, missionId): by-category summary

## Does not include
- Raw weight tracking (slot/category model only)
- Per-NPC carry weight on NpcRuntimeState
- Social/concealment consequences of carrying specific item types (separate narrative bead)
- House storage limits (separate bead)

## Evidence
GD panel: 'Use slot/category limits, not raw weight. The meaningful question is take this OR that — not whether you have 4.7 of 5.0 kg.' ND panel: 'The system should express bulk, concealment, and attention. Bulk is the implementation target.' SA panel: category slots fit the existing architecture; weight field exists but becomes accounting noise. UX panel: 'CarryLimitBar on MissionPrepScreen — soft warning near limit, hard block at departure. Never a surprise after starting.'

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** MissionPrepScreen shows carry summary by category; category bar turns yellow near limit; launch is blocked if over limit with clear message
2. **System / technical:** carry limits defined as constants by category; selectExpeditionCarryLoad selector returns per-category current/max; departure validation rejects over-limit state; CarryLimitBar component renders correctly for all states
3. **Tests / verification:** expedition within limits launches; expedition over document limit is blocked; CarryLimitBar renders correct color states; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-jcer (unified owned-item model — carry is computed from ownedItems with location:'mission_pack')
destiny-5zru (typed effects — item categories are typed before limits make sense)

## Finding coverage
Panel finding I-6 (expedition carry limits — slot/category model), UX finding (CarryLimitBar on MissionPrepScreen), GD finding (carry management adds expedition planning without weight sim)
acceptance_criteria:
- Carry limits enforced by category at mission departure
- MissionPrepScreen shows CarryLimitBar per category
- Hard block prevents launching over-limit expedition
- selectExpeditionCarryLoad selector exists
- Tests pass; count does not drop below 403; typecheck clean; build clean

title: Remove dead factionRelationships field from NpcRuntimeState
description:
NpcRuntimeState contains factionRelationships: FactionRelationship[] which is written in recruitment.ts (lines 60-62) but never read by any command, selector, query, or UI component in the entire codebase.

This was confirmed during the session audit (grep confirmed: zero read sites) and endorsed for removal by all four panel roles.

CURRENT STATE:
- Written: recruitment.ts sets factionRelationships when an NPC joins
- Read: nowhere
- Schema: included in npcRuntimeStateSchema under .strict()
- Initial state: present in data/runtime/initial-game-state.json with an empty array default

WHY NOW:
Every NpcRuntimeState schema change (memory, grief, directed bonds) requires updating all test fixtures. Removing dead fields first reduces maintenance cost. The field is a distraction when reading the schema and a silent source of false data confidence.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Remove factionRelationships from npcRuntimeStateSchema in src/domain/npc/contracts.ts
- Remove the write in recruitment.ts
- Remove from data/runtime/initial-game-state.json NPC entries (if present)
- Update any test fixtures containing NpcRuntimeState (idaRhysRosterEntry and related fixtures in testFixtures.ts)
- Remove FactionRelationship import if it becomes unused

DOES NOT INCLUDE:
- Any faction relationship mechanics (those can be designed fresh when needed, with proper read consumers)
- Changes to FactionRelationship type itself (may be used elsewhere — check before deleting)
- Any behavioral change; this is pure dead-code removal

EVIDENCE:
Session audit: grep confirmed factionRelationships written in recruitment.ts:60-62, zero reads elsewhere.
Panel consensus: DROP — all four roles agreed. SA: 'Dead weight in every endDay cycle.'

FINDING COVERAGE:
Session audit finding D-1 (confirmed dead field)
acceptance_criteria:
- factionRelationships absent from npcRuntimeStateSchema
- No write to factionRelationships anywhere in src/
- data/runtime/initial-game-state.json does not include factionRelationships in NPC entries
- All test fixtures updated (idaRhysRosterEntry and any full NpcRuntimeState snapshots)
- TypeScript: no unused imports; zero errors
- All tests pass; test count does not drop below 403
- Build clean

title: Quirk tag schema: replace plain strings with typed Quirk objects and add first trigger pipeline
description:
quirks on NpcDefinition are plain strings (e.g. 'obsessively checks structural integrity before trusting a building'). There is no mechanism to match a quirk to a game context, no way to trigger a mechanical or narrative consequence, and no way for the character signature or memory system to reference quirk activation semantically.

This bead introduces a typed schema — the contract that enables everything quirk-related downstream.

WHAT CHANGES:

1. New schema in src/domain/npc/contracts.ts:
   - QuirkTag enum: 'cautious' | 'superstitious' | 'protective' | 'vengeful' | 'compulsive' | 'loyal' | 'secretive' | 'volatile' | 'grief-prone' (extend as needed)
   - npcQuirkSchema: { text: z.string(), tags: z.array(QuirkTagSchema), triggerKeywords: z.string().array() }
   - npcDefinitionSchema.quirks: z.array(npcQuirkSchema).default([]) (was z.array(z.string()))

2. Migrate all 31 NPCs in data/definitions/npcs.json:
   - Convert each string quirk to a { text, tags, triggerKeywords } object
   - Minimum: 1–2 tags and 2–3 trigger keywords per quirk (migration script acceptable)

3. New helper: matchQuirkToContext(npc: NpcDefinition, contextTags: string[]): NpcQuirk | null
   - Returns the first quirk whose triggerKeywords overlap with contextTags
   - Used by: applyNpcAgency (agency action context), lineTheyWontCross check, character signature derivation

4. First trigger callsite:
   - In applyNpcAgency or evaluateEvents: pass a minimal context array for at least one action type
   - When a cautious quirk matches a structural/hazard context: apply a small bonus (skill output modifier or reliability bump) or write a text event
   - Outcome must be visible in activityLog or NPC state

SYSTEM IMPACT:
- src/domain/npc/contracts.ts (QuirkTag enum, npcQuirkSchema)
- data/definitions/npcs.json (all 31 NPCs — migration)
- New helper matchQuirkToContext in src/domain/npc/ or src/application/commands/
- One callsite in applyNpcAgency or evaluateEvents
- src/ui/screens/NpcDetailPanel.tsx (quirk chips already rendered — may need tag display update)

DOES NOT INCLUDE:
- Full quirk simulation or behavior trees
- NPC-to-NPC quirk interaction
- UI quirk trigger animations (separate polish bead)
- Memory recording of quirk fires (needs destiny-2qz4 — add the write point but leave it as a no-op until memory lands)
- More than one trigger callsite at this stage

EVIDENCE:
SA panel: 'Plain strings won't scale. Propose tagged quirk system with tags and triggerKeywords. Full behavior tree is overkill; tagged keywords are the middle path.'
ND panel: Three concrete authored examples with trigger condition → voiced line → small consequence.
GD panel: 'Quirks are the highest-value identity layer. Wire them to trait-driven event modifiers and action preferences.'

FINDING COVERAGE:
Panel finding Q-1 (typed quirk tag schema), Q-2 (first trigger pipeline)
acceptance_criteria:
- NpcQuirk type exists with { text, tags: QuirkTag[], triggerKeywords: string[] }
- All 31 NPCs in npcs.json have quirks as typed objects (not plain strings); schema validation passes on catalog load
- matchQuirkToContext(npc, contextTags) function with unit tests (match found; no match; empty context)
- At least one trigger callsite in applyNpcAgency or evaluateEvents
- Trigger outcome is observable (activityLog entry, state modifier, or event text)
- NpcDetailPanel still renders quirks correctly (pill chips show quirk text)
- Test count does not drop below 403
- Typecheck clean; build clean; no save invalidation (quirks live on NpcDefinition, not runtime state)

title: Character Signature: one living line per NPC surfaced on roster and detail panel
description:
NPCs currently present a static skill list and a motivation string. There is no visible summary of who this NPC is today — how events, states, and identity combine into a single readable impression. Players who have enriched NPCs with quirks, motivation, and ageBand see none of that personality at a glance.

The UX Designer panel review named this 'the cheapest high-value addition in the NPC system.'

WHAT IT IS:
A single derived string per NPC — computed from:
1. Dominant quirk text (if any)
2. Current emotional/operational state (stressed, grieving, fearful, healthy)
3. Most recent significant memory summary (once destiny-2qz4 exists — initially absent)
4. Relationship highlight (once directed bonds exist — initially absent)

Examples:
- 'Checks every beam before stepping forward.' (from quirk, day 3)
- 'Now speaks only after Marion does.' (from dependency bond, day 12)
- 'Keeps Ida's tool charm in her pocket.' (from grief memory, after Ida's death)

WHY NOW:
The selector is small, the UI surface already exists (NpcDetailPanel, RosterScreen). Starting with quirk + current state gives immediate visible payoff and establishes the pattern that deepens automatically as memory and relationship systems land. If deferred, the enriched schema continues to be invisible to players.

VOICE RULE:
The signature reads like a witness observation, not a game status message.
- CORRECT: 'Checks every beam before stepping forward.'
- WRONG: 'Quirk: cautious. Status: stressed.'

SYSTEM IMPACT:
- New selector: selectCharacterSignature(state, npcId): string
- src/application/selectors/roster.ts (add field to selectRosterDetail output)
- src/ui/screens/NpcDetailPanel.tsx (display at top of character section)
- src/ui/screens/RosterScreen.tsx (compact one-liner under NPC name on roster cards)

DOES NOT INCLUDE:
- Dynamic dialogue (separate bead)
- Memory-driven signature text (needs destiny-2qz4 — feature-flagged until then)
- Relationship-driven signature text (needs destiny-13si)
- Art/portrait changes
- Authored per-NPC signature strings — derived only, not hand-authored fields

EVIDENCE:
UX Designer panel: 'One string per NPC, updated by existing events, surfaces in an existing component. Makes memory visible before the memory system even exists.' All 4 roles endorsed it as highest-ratio investment.

FINDING COVERAGE:
UX panel finding CS-1 — Character Signature concept
acceptance_criteria:
- selectCharacterSignature(state, npcId) returns a non-empty string for any NPC with at least one quirk or active state
- Signature appears in NpcDetailPanel above the skills/motivation section
- Signature appears as a compact line on roster cards (or under the NPC name)
- Signature reflects current operational state (a stressed NPC shows a different signature than a healthy one)
- Selector has unit tests: at least 3 scenarios (quirk only; active negative state; both)
- Test count does not drop below 403
- No schema changes; no save invalidation

title: lineTheyWontCross: inline refusal prompt when player order conflicts with NPC moral boundary
description:
lineTheyWontCross is authored on all 31 NPCs in npcs.json (e.g. 'won't betray a sworn guest', 'won't profane the dead', 'won't harm children') but is never read by any game logic. When a player sends an NPC on a morally contradicting task, no warning fires. The content exists; the consumer does not.

The NPC panel review (Game Designer, Narrative Director, UX Designer, Systems Architect) unanimously identified this as high narrative value at relatively low implementation cost.

WHY NOW:
lineTheyWontCross is the clearest authored moral-boundary in the game. Not building the check means the authored content is false advertising — players who read it expect it to matter.

WHAT CHANGES:
- New helper: checkLineTheyWontCross(npcId, actionContext: string[]): string | null
  Returns the violated line text if the action context contains a conflicting keyword, null otherwise
- Action context: derived from mission type, target type, or explicit action tags
- When a player initiates an assignment that triggers the check: show inline red-tinted callout near the confirmation button ('Marion: I won't betray a sworn guest.')
- Player choices: Change order / Proceed anyway
- If player proceeds: NPC complies with reluctance (state penalty) OR refuses (no action taken) — behaviour driven by relationship strength and intensity field
- NOT a modal for routine conflicts; modal reserved for permanent rupture events

SYSTEM IMPACT:
- New helper in src/application/commands/ or src/domain/npc/
- MissionPrepScreen.tsx or equivalent assignment UI (inline callout)
- Action taxonomy (minimal — string tag array on mission/action types is sufficient)

DOES NOT INCLUDE:
- NPC departure as consequence of line-crossing (separate bead — needs grief/relationship depth)
- Memory recording of compliance/refusal (needs destiny-2qz4 first)
- Quest branching on refusal
- Relationship axis impact on refusal (defer until directed bonds land)

EVIDENCE:
All 4 panel roles named lineTheyWontCross explicitly. ND: 'When Marion is asked to betray a sworn guest, she does not simply refuse. She looks at you for a long moment.' UX: 'Inline red callout, not a modal.' SA: 'No new schema needed — field is already on NpcDefinition.'

FINDING COVERAGE:
Panel finding F5 — lineTheyWontCross as player-facing moral tension
acceptance_criteria:
- checkLineTheyWontCross function exists and returns violated line text for at least one NPC+action pair
- Inline callout rendered in the assignment/mission prep UI — red-tinted, below or near the confirm button
- Player can proceed or cancel from the callout
- At least one authored end-to-end scenario tested: Bead Ida or Marion + a conflicting assignment type triggers the warning
- No new schema changes; no save invalidation
- Tests cover: match returns string; no-match returns null; UI conditionally renders callout
- Test count does not drop below 403

title: Wire ageBand to decay/recovery modifiers in applyStateDecay
description:
ageBand (young/adult/middle/elder) is authored on all 31 NPCs and exposed in the UI, but has zero mechanical effect. Elder NPCs tire at the same rate as young NPCs — directly contradicting the fiction and the design intent confirmed by the Game Designer and Systems Architect in the NPC panel review.

WHY NOW:
The field is in schema, content is authored, UI already shows it. Adding the modifier costs 3 lines of code. Deferring it means the enriched content continues to mislead players who read 'elder' and expect some difference.

WHAT CHANGES:
- Add an ageBand modifier lookup in applyStateDecay (or equivalent decay step)
- elder: +8% faster negative state accumulation, -5% slower recovery
- middle: baseline (0%)
- adult: baseline (0%)
