- young: +5% faster negative accumulation (volatility), +8% faster recovery
- Modifier applies to at least: stress accumulation, fatigue/morale recovery rate
- No new schema fields required — ageBand is read from NpcDefinition via contentCatalog

SYSTEM IMPACT:
- src/application/commands/applyStateDecay.ts (primary change)
- src/application/content/contentCatalog.ts (NpcDefinition lookup, already accessible)
- One modifier table constant (5 lines)

DOES NOT INCLUDE:
- Training speed modifiers (separate bead)
- Injury risk changes (combat bead)
- Retirement/death-by-age mechanics
- UI display of the modifier value
- Combat performance modifiers

EVIDENCE:
Panel consensus: GD and SA agreed ±5-10% on decay/recovery. Very low engineering risk. ageBand is the one identity field where the mechanical implementation is a near-trivial extension of an existing pipeline step.

FINDING COVERAGE:
Panel finding F4 — ageBand as mechanical modifier (±5-10% decay/recovery, not a stat block)
acceptance_criteria:
- ageBand read from NpcDefinition inside applyStateDecay (or called helper)
- elder NPCs accumulate negative state faster OR recover slower than adult baseline
- young NPCs recover faster OR accumulate state faster (volatility interpretation)
- Unit tests verify modifier is applied for each ageBand value
- No schema changes required; build and typecheck clean
- Test count does not drop below 403

title: Author explicit NPC bonds in npcs.json: sibling, lover, rival, creditor, dependent, dead spouse
description:
## Why
Every NPC in npcs.json has bonds: [] — the entire roster of 31 characters has no authored connections to each other, to the city's past, or to House Valdris. Without named bonds, the household feels staffed not entangled. Love, hate, kinship, grudges, sworn loyalty, and dependency are implied in quirks and backgrounds but never embodied in the data. The directed bond graph (destiny-13si) will read these bonds at runtime — but they need to exist first.

## What
Author explicit bonds for all starting roster NPCs (minimum 1 bond each) and all world NPCs in npcs.json. Bonds follow the schema added in destiny-gxl5:

```
bonds: [{ type: BondType, targetId: string, note?: string }]
```

Bond types: sibling | ex-lover | creditor | rival | fosterling | handler | dead-spouse | old-comrade | hated-foreman | missing-child | favored-animal | parent | child | bastard-kin | witness | debtor

## Authored bonds — panel-confirmed lineage (authoritative list)

### Family / kin bonds
- **Old Maret → Maret Sunne**: type:'bastard-kin', note:'Granddaughter raised under a different surname to hide paternity. Old Maret knows she holds the Valdris ledger.'
- **Maret Sunne → Old Maret**: type:'bastard-kin', note:'Does not know Old Maret knows. Carries a ledger proving who profited from the fall of House Valdris.'
- **Lira Ashcroft → Lirien Ashcroft**: type:'parent', note:'Had Lirien outside a sanctioned marriage. Ashcroft name kept through a debt-forgiveness bargain with a surviving noble patron.'
- **Lirien Ashcroft → Lira Ashcroft**: type:'parent', note:'Knows the name is not the real scandal — the blood is. May be heiress or evidence.'

### House Valdris witnesses
- **Old Maret → house-valdris-fall**: type:'witness', note:'Was lower household accounts keeper. Falsified grain ledgers during pre-Breach winter riot. Last living witness to the servants hall.'
- **Garet Doyle → npc-valdris-retainer-patron**: type:'witness', note:'Father ran inn serving Valdris retainers. Locked box contains service tokens and unpaid writs from the old house.'
- **Torvald Messe → house-valdris-fall**: type:'witness', note:'Father copied all noble grants before the Breach. Holds copy of the legal form that erased Valdris the first time.'
- **Sister Vael → npc-dead-sister-vael**: type:'dead-spouse', note:'Sister erased from temple records after the Breach — smuggled out under plague blankets. Source of Vael's information network: confessions, not visions.'

### Enemy bonds
- **Harlen Voss → house-valdris-fall**: type:'creditor', note:'Grandfather purchased Valdris arrears after the Breach. Wealth began with a coerced default. Claim is legally void if duress can be proven.'
- **Lady Sorn → house-valdris-fall**: type:'rival', note:'House stood by while Valdris was stripped. Legitimacy structurally depends on Valdris staying fallen. Keeps the list of who was taken.'

### Hidden lineage
- **Marion Vale → npc-valdris-cargo-patron**: type:'handler', note:'Family handled illicit cargo for a noble house. Knows which house shipped people out of Valdenmoor during the first panic.'

## Why now
destiny-13si (directed bond graph) needs authored bonds to test with real data. destiny-nw4m is the content bead that produces that data. Without it, the relationship system has no authored starting state and all relationship-gated dialogue fires on empty.

## Player impact
- NPCs in the detail panel show their bonds with brief authored notes
- The roster feels entangled: connections visible, consequences implied
- Bond-gated dialogue and relationship arc entry points become possible

## System impact
- data/definitions/npcs.json — bonds array populated for all 31 NPCs
- No schema changes (schema exists from destiny-gxl5)
- Minimum: 1 bond per roster NPC, 2 bonds per world NPC with dialogue

## Does not include
- Runtime bond graph computation (destiny-13si)
- New bond type schema additions beyond the 16 listed
- Bond-triggered events or dialogue (future content beads)
- The 5 new lineage NPCs (separate bead: destiny-lineage-new-npcs)

## Evidence
Lineage panel (ND + WB): every key NPC has an authored lineage role confirmed in panel synthesis. bonds: [] across all 31 NPCs is the critical gap. destiny-13si reads these bonds at runtime.

## Acceptance Criteria
1. **Player-visible:** NPC detail panel shows bonds list; at least one NPC in the roster shows a family bond and one shows an enemy bond
2. **System / technical:** bonds field non-empty for all 31 NPCs; all targetIds either reference existing NPC IDs or use canonical ghost-IDs (prefixed npc-ghost-) for pre-Breach dead/absent persons
3. **Tests / verification:** npcs.json parses against npcDefinitionSchema without errors; test count does not drop below 403

## Dependencies
destiny-gxl5 (bond schema — already completed)
destiny-13si (directed bond graph reads these bonds at runtime)

## Finding coverage
Lineage panel — ND Part 1 (existing NPC lineage audit), Part 2 (Ashcroft resolution), WB section 2 (family survival types)

title: NPC attachment moment: redesign roster first impression for emotional hook
description:
UX/Emotional Designer finding: 'The player never has an attachment moment. The roster leads with numbers, not people. The player has no reason to care about an NPC before they lose them.'

WHAT TO CHANGE:
- RosterScreen NPC entry: lead with name, role/background phrase, and one authored personality detail (quirk or mood), NOT stats
- Stats visible but visually secondary (reveal on expand or smaller)
- On first meeting / recruitment: brief one-line authored greeting or character moment displayed before assignment/stats
- Consider: when viewing an NPC for the first time (no previouslyViewed flag), surface their most distinctive authored field prominently

NOTES:
- Depends on destiny-gxl5 (NPC identity schema) to have quirks and appearance fields available
- The goal is the 'dog with a name' principle: a character with a quirk and a face cannot be treated as a unit
- This is explicitly a UX bead — implementation may be CSS/layout only if content exists

ACCEPTANCE:
- RosterScreen NPC entries lead with human identity (name, background phrase, one quirk) not stats
- At least one authored personality detail visible without expanding an NPC
- Stats visible but not the primary visual hierarchy

title: Grief and bereavement: named loss state, grief modifier, narrative hooks on NPC death or departure
description:
Narrative Designer (strong language): 'Grief is the irreducible core of dark fantasy and of this game. It is non-negotiable.' UX Designer: 'The player must witness grief and feel its mechanical weight.'

WHAT TO ADD:
- When an NPC is lost (dies, defects, is expelled), write a memory entry on every NPC with a significant relationship to them: { day, event: 'loss', lostNpcId }
- GriefState: { lostNpcId, lostOnDay, intensity: number } — derive from memory entries
- Grief modifier: NPC with active grief has reduced effectiveness (loyalty contribution, skill output, or morale recovery rate)
- Narrative hook: when viewing a grieving NPC in the detail panel, surface a visible grief indicator and authored or templated text ('Lost Verek. Has not spoken since.')
- Grief fades over days (intensity decay in applyStateDecay or similar)

DEPENDS ON:
- destiny-2qz4 (npcMemory foundation — grief reads from memory)
- destiny-gxl5 (NPC identity schema — grief text appears in extended character section)

ACCEPTANCE:
- NPC loss event writes loss memory entry on related NPCs (trust > threshold)
- deriveGriefState() function returns active grief for NPCs with recent loss memory
- Grief modifier applied in at least one gameplay output (loyalty, activity, morale)
- Grief displayed visibly in NPC detail panel

title: Named relationship states: derive love, hate, rivalry, grief, dependency from axes
description:
Game Designer, UX Designer, and World Builder all called for human-readable relationship summaries rather than raw axis numbers. 'trust: 62, fear: 10' is debugger output, not world-building.

WHAT TO ADD:
- RelationshipState enum or tagged union: 'bonded' | 'trusted' | 'neutral' | 'tense' | 'hostile' | 'rival' | 'dependent' | 'grieving' | 'estranged'
- deriveRelationshipState(edge: DirectedRelationshipEdge): RelationshipState function based on axis thresholds
  Examples: trust > 70 + affection > 50 → 'bonded'; fear > 60 + trust < 20 → 'dependent'; conflict > 70 + rivalry > 60 → 'rival'
- Display the derived state label in RosterScreen and NpcDetailPanel as a readable badge/tag
- Named states should also be searchable/filterable in roster

NOTES:
- This depends on destiny-13si (directed bond graph) being complete first
- Named states are DERIVED not stored — they are computed from axes at display time
- Grief state requires npcMemory (destiny-2qz4) to detect recent loss events

ACCEPTANCE:
- deriveRelationshipState() function with at least 6 named states
- State displayed as readable label in at least one UI panel
- All named state derivations covered by unit tests

title: Author quirks, secrets, humor, and sensory district texture for NPCs
description:
### Objective
Give Valdenmoor its slightly-wrong dark fantasy texture through quirks, habits, secrets, dark comedy, and sensory place-writing.

### Scope
In scope:
- NPC quirks, habits, and secrets
- sensory district and POI micro-texture
- dark-comic texture notes and rumors consistent with tone

Out of scope:
- broad narrative arc rewrites
- slapstick or genre-breaking comedy

### Ownership
- data/definitions/npcs.json
- data/definitions/districts.json
- data/definitions/pois.json
- data/definitions/rumors.json
- relevant docs if standards need clarification

### Inputs
- docs/narrative.md
- docs/art-direction.md
- user review criteria from destiny-3fwp

### Expected Output
- authored oddities and sensory details that deepen place
- dark-comedy guidance embodied in content
- validation for any schema changes

### Validation
- pnpm test:run
- pnpm typecheck
- pnpm build

### Architecture Constraints
- quirks must reinforce dark fantasy specificity, not generic whimsy

### TDD Expectation
- tests first if schema/contracts change

### Stop Conditions
- stop if content additions require unresolved rating/content-policy decisions
acceptance_criteria: NPCs and districts gain memorable oddities without breaking tone

title: Add quest-funnel playthrough coverage for discovery, travel, execution, and aftermath
description: Why: The current playthrough-harness backlog covers broad branch scenarios, but not a dedicated regression suite for the quest funnel itself. Given the confirmed issues around lead discovery, contract acceptance, travel-to-incident flow, mission prep context, and objective-specific execution, we need end-to-end scenario coverage that proves the player can follow a coherent quest path through the world after the upcoming refactors. What: add command-level and browser-smoke playthrough coverage for the core quest funnel from lead discovery or surfacing, through acceptance, travel or location gating, execution, and aftermath visibility.
design: The scenarios should validate world guidance as well as state transitions. At minimum cover one combat contract, one investigation contract, and one non-combat contract once those handlers are stateful enough. Prefer assertions on route/context identity, next-step guidance, and aftermath persistence rather than only on rewards.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The playthrough backlog includes a dedicated quest-funnel regression scenario pack. 2. At least one scenario verifies a discovered lead becomes accepted work, then becomes an on-site execution flow with explicit location context. 3. At least one scenario verifies aftermath or follow-up state remains visible after resolution. 4. Scenario assertions catch regressions where the player can bypass location/context or lose quest identity between screens.

title: Extract quest lifecycle out of gameSlice into testable quest services
description: Why: quest acceptance, completion, failure, expiry, and investigation resolution are currently implemented as open-coded reducers inside gameSlice with repeated template lookups and side-effect mutation. That makes the quest system hard to extend, hard to reason about, and easy to desynchronize across objective types. What: move quest lifecycle rules into explicit application/domain helpers so reducers become thin dispatch points and new objective handlers can reuse tested settlement and progress logic.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Quest lifecycle rules are implemented in dedicated helpers or services rather than duplicated reducer branches. 2. gameSlice reducers delegate to those helpers and stay thin. 3. Shared tests cover acceptance, completion, failure, expiry, and at least one objective-specific resolution path. 4. Adding a new objective type no longer requires copying settlement logic into multiple reducers.
close_reason: Extracted quest lifecycle into src/application/commands/questLifecycle.ts: canDiscoverQuest, addQuestLeadIfNew, acceptQuestFromLead, expireTimedQuestsOnState, resolveSimpleContractObjective. gameSlice reducers now delegate to these helpers. Fixed standing-penalty bug in expireTimedQuests (settleQuestFailure was called after quests were already removed). Eliminated duplicated quest-expiry code in endDay and advanceTimeSlot. 15 new tests added. 378 total pass, zero typecheck errors, clean build.

title: Replace instant delivery and survival resolution with stateful objective handlers
description: Why: delivery and survival quests currently resolve from the Contracts screen with one button press as soon as the player is in the matching district. They do not consume goods, model transit risk, represent on-site completion steps, or leave room for branching outcomes. That is a dead-end mechanic for both immersion and extensibility. What: implement objective-specific handlers and runtime state so delivery and survival work can represent prerequisites, progress, and outcome checks instead of immediate district-gated completion.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Delivery and survival objectives no longer complete instantly from a single district check. 2. Objective handlers can express prerequisites, progress, and at least one failure or complication path. 3. Contract UI shows what remains to be done instead of a generic complete button. 4. Tests cover at least one delivery and one survival progression path.

title: Turn investigations into location-aware quest operations with guided setup and debrief
description: Why: Investigation quests currently collapse into a roster checkbox screen with a district mismatch warning, then an abstract success state. They communicate stakes, but not enough procedural context: where the investigation happens, why selected operatives matter, what the player is actually doing in-world, or what the outcome changes next. What: redesign the investigation flow so it reads as a grounded operation with location-aware setup, clearer personnel reasoning, and a stronger debrief/aftermath step.
design: Keep the systemic core if useful, but wrap it in stronger world-facing guidance and consequence presentation.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Investigation setup explains the location, action, and personnel rationale in player-facing terms. 2. If the player is in the wrong district, the flow points them toward the required place instead of only blocking. 3. The debrief communicates what was learned, lost, or unlocked beyond raw reward numbers. 4. The investigation flow feels like a quest operation rather than a detached skill check form.

title: Split diegetic ledger fiction from global accounts access
description: Why: The current Ledger screen is framed as a singular physical house artifact while also functioning as a globally accessible management screen. That creates friction between fiction and usability. What: separate the diegetic ledger object from the always-available accounts interface, so the player keeps practical access to debt, wages, and obligations without confusing the fiction of where the physical ledger resides.
design: A clean solution is to keep a globally accessible accounts or house-summary surface while reserving the more diegetic ledger framing for house scenes, clues, and narrative beats. Do not remove player access to critical economic information while traveling or adventuring; instead, clarify what is abstract system access versus what is literally the physical ledger in the manor.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A globally accessible accounts surface (e.g. House overview or HUD summary) shows debt amount, wages owed, and days remaining — always reachable regardless of player location. 2. The diegetic Ledger screen is framed as a house-contextual object: accessible from the House or Bureau room, not from the district map or city overview sidebar. 3. The UI copy distinguishes between 'your accounts' (abstract system access) and 'the ledger' (physical object in the manor) where the distinction matters narratively. 4. No critical economic information is hidden from a player who is traveling or in a district.

title: Reframe Marion's room as a habitable salvaged room, not a privileged personal preserve
description: Why: It is coherent that Marion has been living in the house and using one intact room, but the current presentation makes it read as if she uniquely retained a special untouched personal chamber. That feels arbitrary and can undermine trust in the fiction. What: reframe the room so Marion clearly occupies one of the few salvaged habitable rooms, explain why it remains usable, and avoid implying that it is a narratively privileged preserve without cause.
design: Keep Marion's presence materially grounded: she stayed, consolidated salvage, and kept a few spaces functional. The room should read as practical habitation under strain, not sentimental exemption. If the player can search or interact with the room, the framing should respect privacy and state why only certain things are visible or accessible.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The room no longer reads as an implausible special preserve. 2. The UI or text explains why this room remains habitable. 3. Marion can still be housed there, but the fiction presents it as one salvaged room rather than 'her special room'. 4. Search/privacy framing remains coherent.

title: Retune vault gating copy and clue pacing
description: Why: The current vault copy over-explains future structure and compresses too much plot significance into a single locked-room beat. The player should understand that the vault is sealed without being prematurely told how the entire progression will work. What: simplify vault gating copy, make the immediate requirement clearer, and pace follow-up clues so the vault can seed later investigation rather than dumping a full quest payload at once.
design: Prefer immediate concrete language such as a key requirement over meta phrasing like 'or a quest'. Let narrative escalation emerge through search discoveries, notes, and follow-up hooks instead of pre-announcing the quest structure in the room state itself.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Vault copy is simpler and more concrete. 2. The vault no longer advertises quest structure prematurely. 3. Unlocking the vault can feed into later clues or investigation beats without over-compressing payoff.

title: Clarify recruit quality labels and rarity meaning
description: Why: Labels such as Rare or Uncommon currently communicate hidden progression meaning without explaining their gameplay impact. Players cannot tell whether the label is flavor, power, training ceiling, or social status. What: clarify or reframe recruit quality labels so the player understands what they change mechanically and why a given candidate carries that label.
design: The label can remain rarity-based if the game explains it well, or it can be reframed in more operational language. The important part is that the player learns the actual effect, such as training cap or long-term growth potential, without reading source code. Keep the explanation compact and legible in the recruitment flow.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Recruit quality labels are understandable without external documentation. 2. The UI states or tooltips the actual mechanical meaning. 3. The presentation helps players compare candidates rather than mystifying them.

title: Add playthrough execution commands and CI-ready suite entrypoints
description: Why: A proper harness should be easy to run locally and later in automation. Without stable entrypoints, even good scenarios will remain underused. What: add dedicated project commands and suite entrypoints for playthrough execution so contributors and future CI can run the golden path, branch pack, and browser smoke subsets predictably.
design: Keep execution surfaces simple and explicit: a baseline suite, a branch suite, and a browser-smoke suite are enough initially. The important part is that command names are stable and outputs are consistent with the report format from the reporting bead.
acceptance_criteria: 1. The project has stable commands or scripts for running playthrough suites. 2. Golden-path, branch-pack, and browser-smoke execution surfaces are distinct. 3. The commands align with the report format and failure output used by the harness. 4. The setup is ready to be wired into CI later without rethinking invocation structure.

title: Document playthrough strategy and scenario inventory
description: Why: Once the harness and scenario pack exist, contributors need a stable source of truth for what scenarios exist, what each path is meant to prove, and how to run or extend them. What: document the playthrough strategy, scenario inventory, naming rules, seeds, invariants, and extension workflow for future agents and contributors.
design: This should be project-facing documentation, not just inline test notes. It should explain why command-level playthroughs are primary, how browser smoke playthroughs relate to them, and how to add new branch scenarios without duplicating existing coverage.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A playthrough strategy document exists. 2. The scenario inventory lists current paths, seeds, and the purpose of each path. 3. The documentation explains how to add new scenarios and branch comparisons. 4. The relationship between command-level and browser-level playthroughs is explicit.

title: Add browser-level smoke playthroughs for critical routes
description: Why: Command-level playthroughs prove state correctness, but they do not prove that the same routes remain reachable through the actual UI. We need a thin browser layer for the most important paths. What: add browser smoke playthroughs for a small critical set of routes, starting with the golden path and one divergent branch, so the UI can be validated against the same scenario intent without rebuilding the full harness in the browser.
design: Keep browser playthroughs thinner and fewer than command-level runs. They should focus on route reachability, action wiring, and visible consequence checkpoints, not on exhaustive state comparison. Reuse scenario terminology and expected checkpoints from the command-level harness wherever practical.
acceptance_criteria: 1. At least one golden-path browser smoke playthrough exists. 2. At least one non-golden branch is covered in the browser layer. 3. Browser playthroughs validate key route transitions and visible consequences. 4. The browser layer reuses command-level scenario intent instead of inventing unrelated flows.

title: Add playthrough report and branch-diff output
description: Why: Once multiple scenarios exist, the project needs a readable way to inspect what changed between runs. Raw state dumps will not scale. What: add structured reporting for playthrough runs, including summary output, named checkpoints, invariant failures, and branch-diff views for key metrics such as money, roster readiness, faction standing, quest progress, and combat outcomes.
design: Keep reports human-readable first, machine-consumable second. The branch diff should highlight game-meaningful changes rather than object-level noise. Prefer stable sorted output and named metrics so future CI or snapshot tooling can consume the same structure.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Playthrough runs produce a readable summary report. 2. Branch comparisons highlight meaningful metric deltas. 3. Failure output points to the failing step or invariant clearly. 4. The report format is stable enough to support future CI integration.

title: Remove activeQuestIds and enforce one canonical active-quest runtime model
description: Why: activeQuestIds still exists in the game schema and fixtures, but live quest behavior is implemented against activeQuests and completedQuestIds. That leaves a parallel quest-state representation in saves that can drift and confuse future integrations, including combat-linked quest settlement. What: remove activeQuestIds from runtime state, fixtures, and tests or make it a strictly derived compatibility field during migration, then enforce one canonical active-quest model across selectors, commands, and saves.
acceptance_criteria: 1. active quest runtime state has one canonical representation. 2. activeQuestIds is removed or reduced to an explicit compatibility/migration mechanism with invariant coverage. 3. quest selectors, combat settlement, and fixtures all use the same canonical runtime model. 4. save validation/tests are updated accordingly.
close_reason: Removed activeQuestIds from gameStateSchema, initial-game-state.json, and all test fixtures. activeQuests is now the single canonical active-quest model. All 363 tests pass, zero typecheck errors, clean build.

title: Bridge or suppress expedition combat until source-integrated encounters exist
description: Why: Expedition logic can currently generate combat markers and destination data includes encounterEnemyIds, but those markers are not cleanly bridged into ActiveCombatState and do not share combat provenance or aftermath consistently. What: either wire expedition combat into the standard combat entry flow using the explicit provenance model, or suppress unresolved expedition-combat markers until the bridge exists. Also align expedition attrition and dangerous-deployment aftermath with the chosen combat-health and recovery rules where appropriate.
acceptance_criteria: 1. expedition combat is either fully routed into standard combat or intentionally suppressed with no dangling pseudo-combat markers. 2. encounterEnemyIds or equivalent destination enemy data is either consumed or removed from the current flow. 3. expedition aftermath is consistent with the chosen dangerous-deployment recovery model. 4. tests cover the chosen behavior explicitly.

title: Persist combat aftermath summary and mission context
description: Why: Combat currently clears activeCombat on conclusion, which weakens aftermath visibility in the dashboard and event log. The combat screen also drops too much of the mission or quest context once the fight starts. What: persist a lastEncounterSummary or equivalent aftermath record, surface mission stakes and source context during combat, and keep resolved consequences visible after the encounter is concluded.
acceptance_criteria: 1. the combat screen shows compact mission or encounter stakes while combat is active. 2. concluding combat does not erase the only readable recap of what happened. 3. dashboard and event log can show the latest resolved encounter summary until replaced by a newer one. 4. tests cover active-context visibility and post-conclusion recap visibility.

title: Character background: ID not stored, no post-creation mechanical reference
description: Player picks a background (Blade/Schemer/Voice) at character creation which sets initial stats, but the background ID is never stored in playerCharacter state. After game start there is no way to know which background was chosen — no background-specific dialogue, no background bonuses in endDay, no background shown on character sheet. Traits ARE stored and used throughout. Fix: add background: z.string().optional() to playerCharacter schema, store it on setPlayerCharacter, and use it for at least 1-2 background-specific perks.

title: NPC states: anger, intoxication, hygiene defined but never read or written
description: Three NPC state attributes are defined in statesSchema (anger, intoxication, hygiene), initialized in all NPCs, and displayed/tracked in state — but are never modified by any command and never used for any decision. applyStateDecay.ts only decays hunger/fatigue/stress/health. These three are dead schema weight. Either: (a) implement them (anger affects combat/morale, intoxication from tavern events, hygiene from neglect→disease risk) or (b) remove them to reduce confusion.

title: NPC training focus: useful feature but players rarely discover it
description: trainingFocus selector in NpcDetailPanel only appears when assignment='training', and the 1.5x skill gain multiplier it provides is the only specialization option. But the feature is invisible until a player assigns training AND knows to look. Additionally, trainingFocus resets to null if assignment changes, so planning a training track requires re-selecting each time. Consider: (a) persist trainingFocus between assignment changes, (b) surface focus selection more prominently, (c) add a 'training plan' view to roster.

title: Stash: items can be bought but not sold back
description: ShopsScreen allows adding weapons/armor to stash (addToStash action) but there is no sell/discard action. Once items are in stash they can only be equipped to NPCs — no sell-back, no drop. Players who over-buy are stuck with cluttered stashes and no refund path. Add a sell-back option at a discount (e.g. 50% of shopPrice) and/or a discard option. Wire into shop offer rows for owned items.

title: Missing relatives lore: field defined but never populated or shown
description: householdLore.missingRelatives (z.array(z.string())) is defined in gameStateSchema and initializes empty. No quest, event, or reducer ever populates it. No UI ever reads or displays it. The narrative concept (the player searching for missing family) is central but the data model to track it is a dead stub. Either: populate it via main quest progression (Mira's name added on rescue), use it for NPC dialogue callbacks, or remove it if replaced by mainQuest.stage.

title: Title assignment synergy: wire NPC skills to title bonuses
description: Title assignment is mechanical but not strategic — no synergy between NPC skills and title effects. Add synergy bonuses e.g. Steward with high administration skill gets +X% working income, Commander with high tactics gets +X combat bonus.

title: Verify Ida Rhys removed from NPC roster data
description: Ida Rhys was removed from opening lore in OpeningScreen.tsx. Confirm she is also flagged as non-recruitable or removed from npcs.json so she cannot appear in hire offers.

title: Seeded RNG for deterministic endDay
description: 16 Math.random() calls in endDay.ts make game state non-deterministic and impossible to replay or debug. Inject a seeded RNG from state (e.g. state.rngSeed) so endDay outcomes are reproducible and unit-testable.

title: Audit and remove dead selectors and routes
description: reputation.ts and household.ts selectors may be unreachable. HouseScreen.tsx route may be disconnected from navigation. Audit import graph, remove unreachable selectors, and fix or remove dead routes.

title: Fix TypeScript hygiene: unsafe casts and contentCatalog null safety
description: Two issues: (1) roster.ts uses (def as any), combat tests use 'as unknown as unknown' double-cast bypassing TypeScript — fix BASE_GAME_STATE fixture typing and remove casts. (2) contentCatalog.npcsById.get() is called in 28+ places without null checks — add a safeGetNpc() helper and audit all lookup sites.

title: Disabled button explanations and button style audit
description: Disabled buttons give no explanation for why they are disabled — players are blocked with no feedback. Add tooltip/inline reason to all disabled buttons (insufficient gold, renown too low, etc.). Audit button styles across all screens — inconsistent styles create trust issues. Standardize to a 3-variant system (primary, secondary, danger). Also surface equipment stash above repair section.

title: District-specific events and encounter hooks
description: Districts have great narrative identity in JSON data but visiting triggers nothing district-specific. Add 3-5 district-specific random events per district that reflect their identity (e.g., Dockside smuggling tip, Highspire noble intrigue). Wire events to city dials and faction control so districts feel alive and variable.

title: Add hiring affordability and burn rate UI feedback
description: Players cannot see burn rate or affordability when making hiring decisions — no feedback loop. Add: weekly salary burn rate display, affordability indicator on hire cards (can/cannot afford ongoing cost), projected weeks of runway at current income.

title: Move hard-coded content to data files
description: endDay.ts contains hard-coded district-faction maps, rumors, and other content that should live in data files. Extract to data/ directory and load via content catalog. Decouples content authoring from engine code.

title: Story NPCs: 5 former House Valdris staff
description: Add 5 story NPCs to npcs.json: (1) Dalen Morke — betrayer, now Gilded Court agent. Exposable via quest-morke-confrontation. Can be flipped with high Gilded Court standing or blackmail. Marion loyalty cap to him: -20. (2) Orren Wex — former steward/accountant, Compact custody in district-the-hollows. Rescue quest unlocks Debts Owed section of Ledger. Marion-Orren NPC-NPC: trust+55 affinity+40. (3) Tessaly Ash — believed dead, alive in The Pale under alias as Wren informant. Knows Mira's disappearance was not fully involuntary. Only reachable via Wren questchain. (4) Sanna Veld — fled to Ironworks, simple reunion, loyalty starts 65. (5) Brand — fighter hiding in The Warrens. Each needs full story NPC schema fields + NPC-NPC relationship seeds.

title: WorkBoard/Contracts redesign — kill Available Contracts from Deployment
description: Remove Available Contracts section from Deployment tab entirely. Contracts discovered IN districts (each district POI has a Leads tab). Work Board = only accepted/active quests. Deployment tab = squad prep / formation only. Add quest fields: discoverySource, discoveryDistrictId, sourceNpcId, riskLevel, flavorNote. Contracts from Bar, Guild, Court, Events — not a global list. Player must go to the district to pick up work.

title: District POI system — 2-level navigation
description: Add pois.json data file (8-12 POIs per district). Add adjacentDistrictIds and borderType to districts schema. Border types: open/compact_checkpoint/ring_toll/condemned_barrier/restricted_gate. Create DistrictInteriorScreen.tsx showing POIs as clickable node map (not text list). Harbor+The Pale+Ironworks adjacent; Gilded Heights guarded access. POI types: guild, tavern, shop, court, residence, market, faction_hq, black_market. District travel resolves via adjacency graph.

title: Ledger Screen — House Valdris founding document
description: Dedicated LedgerScreen.tsx accessible from main HUD (persistent button). Shows: Debt Remaining + PAY DEBT action, daily income/expense P&L, active faction obligations, contract registry (all signed contracts). The Ledger is the legal identity of House Valdris — not an inventory item. item-ledger-bureau in inventory is a separate bureaucratic prop. Opening scene says player takes over the Ledger — this screen is the payoff. Wire to debt state, quests, and faction standings.

title: House Valdris: HouseScreen + room state system
description: Add house field to initial-game-state.json and houseStateSchema to contracts.ts. 8 rooms: Entrance Hall (intact), Marion Quarters (intact), Bureau (damaged,15M), Kitchen (damaged,20M), Study (stripped,35M), Master Chamber (stripped,45M), Cellar/Vault (locked), Garret (collapsed,130M). House in district-the-pale. Create HouseScreen.tsx with floor-plan room grid showing room state, repair cost, and available actions. Wire Vault unlock to mainQuest advancement (contains Mira note). Closes destiny-uq3.

title: Marion-Arc: Mira-Queststatus auf Dashboard verknüpfen
description: Mira-Quest-Widget auf Dashboard zeigt Status, aber mainQuest.stage ändert sich nie durch Spieleraktionen. Quest braucht Triggerpunkte: Fraktionsstatus, Distrikt-Erkundung, Dialoge.

title: NPCs gestalten Welt aktiv mit (NPC-Agency erweitern)
description: Aktuelle Agency: 8% Chance auf Aktion/Tag. Needs: NPCs beeinflussen Fraktionen, kaufen Items, initiieren Beziehungen mit anderen NPCs, kommentieren Weltereignisse. Betrifft: endDay.ts NPC-Agency-Sektion.

title: Marion Beziehungswerte: Tag 1 zu kalt
description: Marion blieb durch den Kollaps. trust:25, loyalty:30, affinity:15 sind Fremden-Werte. Sollte: trust~55, loyalty~70, affinity~40. Betrifft: initial-game-state.json Marion roster-Eintrag.

title: Weltweite Events: dayMin-Guards fehlen für Day-1-Events
description: Lore-Critic: event-restored-appeal (minUnrest:40, Start:55) feuert Tag 1. event-ring-smuggler-contact feuert Tag 1. event-npc-marion-warning (minUnrest:50) feuert Tag 1 aber Beziehungswerte passen nicht. Betrifft: events.json dayMin-Guards setzen.

title: Ida-Ankunft als Story-Event (Tag 3-4)
description: Ida Rhys soll über ein Story-Event ankommen, nicht einfach im Hire-Pool erscheinen. Event: 'Eine Frau klopft an die Tür...' Entscheidung: Anheuern oder ablehnen. Betrifft: events.json neues event-ida-arrives, gameSlice.ts recruitNpc-Trigger.

title: Zeitfortschritt — 'Morning' schreitet nicht voran
description: Tageszeit bleibt auf 'morning'. Nachmittag/Abend-Slots bei Aktionen konsumieren. Betrifft: endDay.ts timeSlot-Logik, Aktivitäten-System.

title: Shop nicht nach aktuellem Distrikt gefiltert
description: ShopsScreen zeigt alle Items unabhängig von currentDistrictId. Sollte nur Shops des aktuellen Distrikts zeigen (districts.json shopTypes). Betrifft: ShopsScreen.tsx.

title: Haus hat keinen eigenen Screen
description: houseDistrictId ist nur ein Badge. Braucht /house Route: Stash-Panel, Personalübersicht, Marion-Briefing, Hausstatus. Betrifft: App.tsx Route, neuer HouseScreen.tsx.

title: World rumors — 1-2 daily log entries unrelated to player
description: Each endDay generate 1-2 ambient world rumors from a authored template pool. Examples: 'Whispers in the Pale: Lord Aldren was seen near the Ashfields.' / 'Iron Docks: Three warehouses burned overnight.' / 'A Collectors ledger-keeper was found dead in The Tangle.' Rumors can foreshadow events, hint at Mira's location, or reflect faction activity. Stored in eventTemplates with type:'rumor', isAutoResolved:true.
close_reason: 15 authored world rumors fire via endDay; world feels alive without player action

title: Dialogue system — NPC conversations with choice branches
description: No dialogue exists. Implement minimal dialogue: (1) DialogueScreen with NPC portrait + text + 2-3 choice buttons, (2) dialogue trees in data/definitions/dialogues.json, (3) choices can: start quest, change faction standing, unlock contract, trigger event, (4) Marion and key world NPCs have dialogue. This is the RPG interaction layer.
close_reason: Dialogue system with 2 NPC trees; Marion Vale and Verek Holst; Talk button in NpcDetailPanel

title: Enemy NPCs — named antagonists with motivation
description: Enemies in combat are anonymous stats. Implement: (1) enemy NPCs in data (Collectors enforcer, Iron Docks gang leader, Pale Court spy), (2) enemies have faction, motivation, optional negotiation flag, (3) MissionPrepScreen shows enemy portrait/name for contracts with named antagonists, (4) defeating named enemy has faction consequence.
close_reason: 3 named enemy NPCs (Harlen Voss, The Dockmaster, Lady Sorn); enemyNpcId field on quest schema; threat panel on MissionPrepScreen; 333 tests pass

title: Equipment stash — track weapon/armor ownership
description: Currently weapons and armor have no ownership tracking — players can equip any item from the full catalog. Add an equipment stash (separate from consumable inventory) so gear must be purchased or looted before equipping. Integrates with shop purchase flow and future loot drops. The ItemSelectionModal should filter to stash contents only.
close_reason: Stash system tracks owned equipment; NPCs equip from stash only

title: NPC personal events — authored voice per character
description:
NPCs appear in generic events but have no character-specific events. Marion never speaks in her own voice. Events don't distinguish between characters.

NARRATIVE DESIGNER: 'NPCs should have authored voice. Marion's identity as a dock broker who kept crews loyal should surface in how she responds when loyalty is tested.'
GAME DESIGNER: 'Character-specific events are what make NPCs feel like people not tools.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Add 'npcId' condition to event schema — event only fires if that NPC is on the roster
2. Create 2-3 Marion-specific events:
   - 'Marion's Assessment' — fires around day 5: Marion reviews the ledger and gives her honest read of the situation. Choice: listen to her analysis (gain intel) or dismiss her (morale hit for her)
   - 'The Dock Broker's Contact' — fires after first successful contract: a Harbor Ward contact of Marion's makes an offer. Her background becomes a mechanical asset.
3. Create 1 Ida-specific event:
   - 'What Ida Knows' — fires around day 7: Ida reveals she has information about something. Was she sent? Or does she genuinely want to help? Choice affects her relationship arc.
4. These events check npcId is on roster, fire once (repeatable:false), and write to lastFiredDay

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- At least 3 NPC-specific events in events.json
- Marion event fires around day 5 if she is on roster
- Events produce lasting state change (faction standing, log entry, relationship delta)
close_reason: Personal events added for all 12 NPCs — 2 events each for Marion, Ida, Verek, Rutha; 1 for each of the remaining 8 NPCs. Authored log text in each character's distinct voice. isAutoResolved, repeatable: false, staggered dayMin triggers. 333 tests pass, typecheck clean.

title: Status bar visual hierarchy: zone grouping, amber accent, display font values
description:
GlobalStatusBar renders all items (Day, Marks, Rep, District, Deployed, End Day) at equal visual weight. Player cannot identify critical vs contextual at a glance.

UX: Group into 3 zones with visual separation:
[identity | Day X · morning] | [Marks: 450 | Rep: Established] | [⚠ 2 events] → [End Day →]

Art Director: Add amber-gold top border accent to status bar. Resource values in display font italic. Icons before values. End Day button in stamped style (matches destiny-dml shape language bead).

IMPLEMENTATION:
- CSS: status bar gets border-top: 2px solid var(--gold-warm)
- Three flex groups with background-tint separator between them
- Marks and Day values use --font-display italic
- Pending events badge already has pulse — keep, extend pattern to deployed count > 0
- End Day: square corners, amber border (not blue)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Day and Marks scannable in under 1 second
- End Day is visually the primary action
- Pending events badge-only when > 0
close_reason: Three-zone status bar: identity | resources | actions. Amber top border, display font for Day/Marks values, End Day as primary stamped CTA.

title: District narrative hooks: route each hook to one authored entry event or quest
description:
Every district already has a narrativeHook in districts.json pointing to potential content. None of these hooks connect to missions or quests. The story is there; the doors have not been built.

EXISTING HOOKS TO ACTIVATE (one entry event or quest per district):
- Harbor Ward: 'Bandits own the trade roads after the third milestone' → quest or event about road bandit situation
- Gilded Heights: Highland passes nominally under Compact — 'nominally' → event: smuggling through passes
- Ironworks: 'The mines east of Ironworks still produce. Most are haunted.' → mission: escort/salvage to live mine
- The Pale: 'The old garrison road leads to the ruins of the previous city. Nobody comes back unchanged.' → mystery arc entry event
- The Warrens: 'The sewers connect to an undercroft. Older than the city.' → Tallow Ring quest hook
- The Hollows: 'There are doors in that wall. Unofficial ones.' → late-game expedition gate

Each hook becomes: one event that fires on first district visit (not every visit), opening a quest thread or adding a log entry that makes the district feel inhabited.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Each district has at least one authored first-visit event
- Events fire once (first visit only, firedEventIds)
- At least 3 hooks connect to actual quest entries
close_reason: Each district has 1-2 authored narrative hooks displayed on the map

title: Update GDD: art direction section, player character framing, Marion romantic arc
description:
GDD currently has no art direction section. Several design decisions from this specialist panel session need permanent record.

SECTIONS TO ADD:
1. Art Direction: warm-dark palette, IM Fell English + Crimson Pro fonts, ink-and-wash portrait style, ledger-as-administrative-record aesthetic metaphor
2. Player Character: male, manager-not-combatant framing, named (protagonist), no combat stats
3. Marion Vale: the one loyal remaining retainer, female, romantic interest potential — framed through restraint not overt mechanics
4. Ida Rhys: mercenary pragmatist, second starting NPC, arrived 3 days after Marion, different relationship arc
5. Opening narrative intent: grief + circumstances + one active goal (Mira) + one emotional anchor (Marion)
6. Debt-claim as early game tension arc: 30-day countdown, mechanical not just narrative
7. House Valdris HQ: The Pale district, Threadneedle Lane

This bead depends on destiny-d8d (existing GDD update bead) — merge or sequence accordingly.
close_reason: GDD updated with art direction, player character framing, Marion romantic arc

title: Faction 5 SVG symbols in icons.svg
description:
Factions have zero visual identity. Art Director: 5 SVG symbols, all buildable as symbol elements in public/icons.svg. No illustration budget needed.

SYMBOLS (all geometric/heraldic, SVG path work):
- Civic Compact: balanced scales icon (civic authority)
- Gilded Court: stylized crown (wealth/status)
- Foundry League: crossed hammer and tong (labor/craft)
- Tallow Ring: candle flame in a circle (underground/smuggling)
- The Restored: broken chain (liberation/ideology)

Each as a <symbol id='faction-[id]'> in icons.svg. Used in: faction standing rows, NPC affiliation badges, contract board employer column, district control indicators.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- 5 SVG symbols in icons.svg
- Used consistently across all faction references
- Each symbol readable at 16px and 32px
close_reason: 5 faction SVG symbols added to icons.svg and rendered on FactionsScreen

title: District visual identity: accent color + glyph watermark per district (CSS only)
description:
Districts are text cards with no visual identity. Art Director: District identity is achievable through CSS without illustration.

PER-DISTRICT IDENTITY:
- Harbor Ward: teal accent (#2a5c6e), anchor glyph (⚓)
- Gilded Heights: gold accent (#8a6e3a), crown glyph (♛)  
- Ironworks: rust accent (#6e3a2a), gear glyph (⚙)
- The Pale: grey accent (#4a5568), skull glyph (☽)
- The Warrens: brown accent (#5c4a2a), rat glyph (≋)
- The Hollows: near-black accent (#2a2020), eye glyph (◉)

Each district card gets: colored left-border (4px solid accent), glyph as large watermark (opacity 0.08, positioned right), district name in display font. CSS classes: .district--harbor, .district--gilded-heights etc.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Each district card visually distinct at a glance
- Color coding consistent across all references to that district
- Danger level expressed through accent color warmth/coldness
close_reason: Each district has unique accent color and glyph watermark via CSS

title: Opening screen: atmospheric art — Valdenmoor skyline SVG + Marion portrait slot
description:
Opening screen is plain text on dark background. First impression is a form. Art Director: 'The opening is seen first and sets the perceptual contract. It must establish the world exists.'

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Add Valdenmoor skyline SVG behind the opening text — silhouette of towers, walls, a broken spire. CSS-positioned, low opacity, atmospheric. Pure SVG, no illustration budget needed.
2. Reserve portrait slot for Marion Vale (right side of opening panel) — initially the CSS silhouette placeholder, later her commissioned portrait
3. Opening prose renders over the art — the text is the foreground

Art Director minimum: even a single candlelit window in CSS box-shadow and a radial warm glow behind the text transforms the emotional tone. Zero asset budget required for the atmospheric layer.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Opening screen has atmospheric visual element (SVG or CSS)
- Marion Vale portrait slot visible from game start
- No plain white text on black background as the sole visual
close_reason: Skyline SVG and Marion portrait slot added to opening screen

title: NPC portrait: CSS silhouette placeholder + faction-colored frame
description:
Current placeholder: letter initial in gradient box. Worst possible MVP. Art Director: 'A letter initial does not make an NPC feel like a person.'

PHASE 1 (CSS only, this sprint):
Replace .npc-portrait-placeholder with: dark gradient bg + hooded/cloaked silhouette SVG + faction-colored border-left accent + name in display font below. No illustration needed.

PHASE 2 (SVG, next sprint):
Anonymous figure placeholder with gender-coded silhouette. 6-8 variants. Faction border color per NPC.

PHASE 3 (commissioned):
Real ink-and-wash portraits for Marion Vale (priority 1 — opening screen anchor and romantic interest), then core 6 starting NPCs. Target style: 18th-century engraving aesthetic, dark backgrounds, 3/4 lighting, hatching in shadows. Size: 140x190px.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (Phase 1):
- No letter-in-a-box anywhere
- Every NPC slot shows silhouette + faction accent color
- Marion Vale visually distinct (as primary character) from generic NPCs
close_reason: Letter-initial replaced with hooded SVG silhouette. Faction-colored border-left accent per NPC. Marion Vale visually distinct with primary class.

title: Art: warm-dark color palette — replace cold navy with amber-candlelight tokens
description:
Current palette: cold navy/black (#111827, #0b1020) with white-opacity borders. Art Director: 'Cold dark signals sci-fi or noir. Warm dark — candlelight, aged parchment, tarnished gold — signals dark fantasy.'

NEW TOKEN SET:
--bg-deep: #0d0a07 (near-black with brown undertone)
--bg-mid: #161009
--bg-surface: #1e160d
--bg-raised: #261c11
--gold-warm: #c9a84c
--gold-muted: #8a6e3a
--text-primary: #e8dcc8 (warm off-white, not blue-white)
--text-secondary: #a89070
--accent-blood: #7a2020 (danger/injury)
--accent-pale: #4a5568 (neutral/unknown)

Replace: rgba(255,255,255,0.X) borders → rgba(201,168,76,0.2) borders
Replace: blue CTA (#3b82f6) → amber-gold (#c9a84c)

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- No cold-blue tones in primary UI surfaces
- All borders use warm amber token
- Text is warm off-white not blue-white
- CSS custom properties used throughout (one token swap propagates everywhere)
close_reason: Warm-dark palette applied, cold navy replaced

title: Nav restructure: 11 items to 7, Market inside The City, Engagement transient
description:
11 nav items is too many. Beyond 7 items navigation becomes invisible. UX Specialist proposed restructure.

PROPOSED STRUCTURE (7 primary + 1 sub):
HOUSE: Operations, The Roster, Recruitment
VALDENMOOR: The City (Market as tab inside), Factions
OPERATIONS: Work Board → Deployment (indented sub-item), Expeditions
TRANSIENT (no nav link): Engagement, Investigation, The Record (widget on Operations)

RATIONALE:
- Market is geographically inside the city — tab not top-level item
- The Record is reference material — surface as widget/link not primary destination
- Engagement is transient — no idle state worth navigating to
- Recruitment stays top-level as a distinct player action

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Max 8 nav items visible at once
- Market accessible as tab within The City screen
- Engagement removed from sidebar
- The Record accessible from Operations dashboard widget
close_reason: Nav trimmed to 7 items; Market accessible via Districts; Engagement removed from nav

title: House Valdris: place in The Pale, add HQ marker to district map
description: House Valdris exists only in lore. No district, no location on the map, no home base the player anchors to. Narrative Designer proposes The Pale: grey-zone district, contested by 3 factions, narratively exact for a fallen house. Add houseLocation to game state, mark on district map as named location, add opening flavor: 'two rooms, a locked basement, a view of three competing flags'.
close_reason: houseDistrictId:district-the-pale in game state. District map marks The Pale with HQ badge. selectHouseDistrictId selector added.

title: P2: EventModal uses untyped react-redux hooks instead of useAppDispatch/useAppSelector
description: EventModal.tsx uses useDispatch and useSelector directly from react-redux instead of the project's typed useAppDispatch and useAppSelector hooks. This creates a type safety gap — action misuse won't be caught at compile time.

title: P2: RecruitmentScreen recruit button missing action-button className
description: The recruit button in RecruitmentScreen.tsx has no className attribute. It renders as an unstyled browser default button, inconsistent with all other action buttons in the game. Add className='action-button'.
close_reason: action-button className added to recruit button.

title: P2: Combat-recruited NPCs show raw npcId in roster instead of name
description: selectRosterEntries resolves name from contentCatalog.npcsById. Enemy NPCs (recruited via combat) are in enemyNpcsById not npcsById, so their name falls back to raw ID string like 'enemy-ash-raider-1'. Fix: check enemyNpcsById as fallback in the selector.
close_reason: selectRosterEntries falls back to enemyNpcsById; enemyNpcsById in contentCatalog.

title: Clarity: GlobalStatusBar shows both assignedSquadCount and deployedCount
description: GlobalStatusBar.tsx shows 'X in the field' (assignedSquadCount) and separately 'X deployed' badge (deployedCount). These are the same concept. Remove one. Keep only 'X deployed' badge when deployedCount > 0, remove the always-visible 'in the field' text.

title: Clarity: narrativeSummary shown twice in DistrictMapScreen
description: DistrictMapScreen.tsx: district.narrativeSummary is used as the title tooltip attribute (line 87) AND rendered as a full paragraph (line 120-122). Remove the title attribute tooltip or use a shorter summary for it. The paragraph is the primary display.
close_reason: title tooltip removed from district card

title: Clarity: Dashboard duplicates GlobalStatusBar info
description: DashboardScreen shows Day and Marks in stat cards that are already in GlobalStatusBar. Remove the redundant stat cards or replace them with more useful info (e.g. active quests count, NPC stress average, days until next hire refresh). Also the 'End Day' button should be visually primary and separate from Save/Load utility buttons.

title: UX: Remove duplicate Districts screen — merge into The City
description: screenCatalog has /district-map (The City - interactive travel) and /districts (Districts - survey/overview). These are redundant. Remove /districts from nav and merge any unique content into DistrictMapScreen. Check DistrictsScreen.tsx for any content not in DistrictMapScreen before removing.

title: UX: Merge Contracts and Missions nav items or clarify sequence
description: screenCatalog has both /contracts (ContractBoardScreen - accept work) and /missions (MissionPrepScreen - assemble squad). Players have no mental model for the difference. Options: (A) merge into one screen with two tabs, (B) rename clearly as 'Work Board' and 'Deployment' with arrows showing sequence, (C) remove /missions from nav and only reach it via ContractBoardScreen button.
close_reason: Nav renamed Work Board/Deployment with step indicator

title: Content expansion: 10 more quests, 20 more armor pieces, NPC-specific event triggers
description:
GDD content targets not yet met:
- Quests: 5 exist, target 10-15. Need 5-10 more with dynamic trigger conditions (NPC state-based, faction standing-based, district-based)
- Armor: 10 exist, target 40-60. Need 20+ more pieces covering light/medium/heavy across all 6 districts
- Events: 8 exist, target 20-30. Need 12+ more including: NPC stress crisis, NPC loyalty threshold, courtship/attraction (trait-driven), internal house dispute, theft/sabotage attempt, rival org provocation

Quest design notes: New quests should use dynamic conditions (e.g., 'available only when Tallow Ring standing > 10', 'available only after completing quest-harborwatch'). Mix investigation and combat objectives. At least 2 should chain (quest A unlocks quest B).

Event design notes: NPC-specific events should trigger based on npcState thresholds — stress > 80, loyalty < 20, morale < 15. These exist in npcStateThresholds.ts but only log a message; no event fires. Wire them to pendingEvents.
close_reason: 10 quests, 20 armor, 3 NPC events added. All Valdenmoor voice.

title: Architecture cleanup: layer violations, hardcoded ID constants, duplicate export
description:
Three medium-priority architecture issues from code audit:

1. NpcDetailPanel.tsx imports getDurabilityTier from application/commands/durability.ts directly — UI should only use selectors. Move getDurabilityTier result into a selector (selectDurabilityTierForNpc) so UI only imports from application/index.ts.

2. npcStateModifiers.test.ts imports from application/ layer (circular dependency: domain test → application command). Move test to src/application/ or inject the test state differently.

3. cityResources is exported twice in application/index.ts (duplicate export * from './selectors/cityResources'). Remove the duplicate.

4. Hardcoded faction/npc/title ID strings scattered throughout codebase. Create src/application/constants/ids.ts with FACTION_IDS, NPC_IDS, TITLE_IDS objects to prevent typo-silent-failures.
close_reason: Circular dep removed, durability selector, duplicate export removed, ID constants file.

title: UI polish: recruitment feedback, focus rings, ShopsScreen empty state
description:
Three UX gaps from UI audit:

1. RecruitmentScreen shows no confirmation after 'Take them on' — the offer disappears silently. Add a brief success line in the screen (not just the activity log): 'Name has joined the house.'

2. ShopsScreen shows nothing when no shops exist in current district — add empty state message: 'No traders operate in this district.'

3. No visible focus indicator on any interactive element — keyboard navigation is invisible. Add :focus-visible styles in App.css for buttons, nav links, and interactive cards. Dark theme appropriate: outline: 2px solid #8b7355 or similar muted gold.
close_reason: Recruitment feedback, ShopsScreen empty state, focus ring.

title: Faction consequences: quest gating, district pressure, NPC standing reactions
description:
Faction standings change numerically but have no consequential gameplay effects.

MISSING CONSEQUENCES:
1. Quest gating: some quests should require minimum faction standing to be visible (e.g., Gilded Court quest needs standing > -20 with Court — currently all quests always visible)
2. District pressure: low standing with controlling faction should add danger to travel (log warning, chance of encounter event)
3. Shop access: hostile standing (-50 or below) should block access to that faction's shops
4. NPC reactions: NPCs with strong faction affiliation (factionAffiliation field in definitions) should react to their faction's standing — loyalty drops if player works against their faction
5. activePressure field in factionStates exists but never updates — wire pressure escalation when standing is very low (< -40): chance of a hostile encounter event per day

These consequences don't require new UI — they plug into existing selectors and the event/travel systems.
close_reason: Faction consequences: quest gating, hostile district pressure, shop blocking, NPC loyalty reactions, pressure escalation.

title: Event triggers on district travel and quest acceptance
description:
The event evaluation engine (evaluateEvents) only fires at end-of-day. This means players see zero events on Day 1 (they must end the day first), and location-specific events never fire on arrival.

NEEDED:
1. After travelToDistrict: call evaluateEvents filtered to districtId-specific events (conditions include currentDistrictId)
2. After acceptQuest: call evaluateEvents to check if any events are triggered by the new quest state
3. After concludeCombatEncounter (victory): call evaluateEvents to check faction-standing-triggered events

evaluateEvents is a pure function (takes state, returns new events) — it can be called from the travelToDistrict thunk, not just endDay. The only concern is deduplication: don't re-fire events that already fired this day.

Add a firedEventIds: string[] field to game state that resets each day — evaluateEvents skips templates already in this list.
close_reason: Events fire on travel and quest acceptance. firedEventIds deduplication. District conditions added.

title: Investigation quest resolution: skill roll system for non-combat objectives
description:
3 of 5 quests (ledger-recovery, ring-debt, restored-appeal) have objectiveType: 'investigation' but there is no investigation mechanic. These quests sit permanently in the active list with no way to complete them other than the manual completeQuest action.

NEEDED:
1. Investigation mission type in MissionPrepScreen — when active quest has objectiveType: 'investigation', the player should be able to 'run the investigation' without entering full combat
2. Resolution screen: shows the investigation objective, a skill roll option (primary skill from NPC definitions — intrigue, security, administration, etc.), and outcome text
3. Skill roll: take the best matching skill among deployed NPCs, compare to a difficulty threshold, determine success/partial/failure
4. Outcomes: success = completeQuest + rewards; partial = partial reward + complication event; failure = failQuest + consequence
5. District context: investigation quests are anchored to a districtId — player must be in or travel to that district first

This can be a lightweight screen, not a full turn-based system. The key is that investigation objectives have an interactive resolution path that uses existing NPC skills.
close_reason: Investigation resolution: skill roll system, InvestigationScreen, ContractBoardScreen wired.

title: District shop expansion: shops in all 6 districts with district-appropriate inventory
description:
Only 1 of 6 districts (Harbor Ward) has a shop. Players in The Pale, The Warrens, The Hollows, Foundry Row, or the Civic Quarter cannot access any shop at all. The district travel mechanic exists but has no meaningful shop consequence.

NEEDED:
1. Add shops to all 6 districts in data/definitions/shops or extend current shop definitions
2. Each district should have 1-2 shops appropriate to its character:
   - The Pale (Gilded Court): luxury goods, court documents, fine weapons
   - The Warrens (contested): black market items, cheap provisions, hired muscle info
   - The Hollows (Tallow Ring / black_market): contraband, cheap bound labor contacts, dangerous goods
   - Foundry Row (League): tools, armor repair materials, engineering components
   - Civic Quarter (Compact): standard provisions, permits/documents, basic weapons
3. Shops should reflect faction control and district dangerLevel in their inventory character
4. The selectShopsByDistrict selector (or equivalent) should return shops for current district
5. ShopsScreen should show 'No traders in this district' for districts with no shops (empty state)
close_reason: Shops in all 6 districts with faction-appropriate inventory, district filtering, empty state, standing gate.

title: City council and institutional governance: faction power, council seats, and institutional control
description: Valdenmoor is governed by a council of five permanent seats: Compact (3), Gilded Court (2), Foundry League (2), Common Wards (1 rotating). No seat for Tallow Ring or Restored — they operate outside the institutional framework. Each faction's institutional control translates to concrete game mechanics: Compact controls water permits and policing (can restrict player movement or shut down operations), Court controls debt adjudication and social legitimacy (can foreclose on properties, blacklist names), League controls production licenses and wall repair contracts (can raise costs or withhold services). House Valdric held three ward-council seats — stripped during the intrigue. Regaining institutional standing is a long-term power game goal. The player can attend council events, build allies within factions to influence votes, or work entirely outside the system.
design: Add to game state: councilSeats: Record<factionId, number> (initialized from world state). Add: institutionalStanding: Record<factionId, 'allied'|'neutral'|'hostile'|'blacklisted'>. Blacklisted = Compact can arrest player NPCs on sight; Court = debt instruments filed against player; League = service refusals at shops. Player can gain ward-council seat at high power standing (unlocks council event participation). Council vote events: periodic events where factions push a policy (raise Register fees, expand field licenses) — player can influence the outcome through faction standing and aligned NPCs.
acceptance_criteria: Faction institutional standing is tracked separately from general standing. Being blacklisted by Compact shows a warning on district travel. Council vote events fire periodically and player can influence them through faction standing. Ward council seat is an achievable milestone that unlocks council event participation.
close_reason: City council governance: institutionalStanding tiers, councilSeats, council vote events, FactionsScreen integration.

title: Expedition loop: outside-the-walls gameplay as a second major game loop
description: The world outside Valdenmoor (the Waste, the Ashfields, the Deep Ruin, the Green Corridor) is a defined part of the world now. The Adventure protagonist path depends on it. But there is no expedition game loop — only the city management loop exists. An expedition is structurally different from a city mission: longer, more resource-intensive, no faction safety net, monster enemies not human, environmental hazards, supply consumption. The expedition loop: (1) PREPARE — buy supplies, check gear durability, select squad (fewer than full roster, others stay on city duties), choose destination and route. (2) TRAVEL — encounter chain: series of encounters (combat, exploration, event) consuming days and supplies. (3) DISCOVERY — find loot, pre-Breach knowledge (unlocks things in the city), rare recruits, or evidence about the Breach/the sister Mira. (4) RETURN — bring back what you found, treat injuries, debrief. Return state affects the city: days passed means wages owed, city events that fired without you, rival organizations that moved.
design: New game state: expeditionState (idle / preparing / traveling / returned). New screen: ExpeditionPrepScreen. New encounter type: WasteEncounter — uses same combat system but enemy types from enemies.json with monster tags. Supply resource: rations consumed per NPC per expedition day. Supplies run out → fatigue/hunger crisis. Expedition length: 2-7 days depending on destination. City continues ticking during expedition (end-of-day runs without player input). Return screen shows what you found + what happened in the city while you were gone.
acceptance_criteria: Player can initiate an expedition to at least one destination (the Ashfields). Travel uses at least 2 encounter events. At least one discovery type (pre-Breach document, rare item). City state advances during expedition. Return shows both expedition results and city changes.
close_reason: Full expedition loop: 3 destinations, encounter tables, discovery rolls, city ticking while away. 5 tests.

title: Relationship progression: how NPC relationships change through actions, proximity, and events
description: Relationship data exists (affinity, respect, fear, loyalty, trust axes between NPCs and between player and NPCs) but relationships never change. The bead 4x3 covers relationships affecting combat — this bead covers how they grow or deteriorate in the first place. Relationship progression is an MVP must-have: it is the core of the NPC simulation. Changes should come from: (1) ACTIONS — giving gifts, assigning beneficial titles, paying wages reliably increases affinity/trust. Harsh dismissals, spending NPCs recklessly, or siding against them in events decreases it. (2) PROXIMITY — NPCs deployed together on missions build relationships with each other over time (positive or negative based on trait compatibility). (3) EVENTS — specific event outcomes can sharply change a relationship axis. (4) TIME — passive drift: relationships slowly decay toward neutral unless maintained.
design: Extend game state: relationships: Record<'player-'+npcId | npcId+'-'+npcId, RelationshipAxes>. RelationshipAxes: { affinity, respect, fear, loyalty, trust } all -100 to 100. Commands: adjustRelationship(fromId, toId, axis, delta). End-of-day tick: apply passive drift (-1 to affinity/trust per day if no positive interaction). Proximity: NPCs in same squad gain +1 affinity/day (trait-gated: high empathy NPCs gain more). Log notable relationship changes in activity log ('Marion seems to trust you more after the harbor job').
acceptance_criteria: Relationship state exists in Redux. Completing a mission with two NPCs increases their mutual affinity. Paying wages on time increases player-NPC loyalty. Activity log notes significant relationship changes. Relationship values are visible on the NPC detail panel.
close_reason: Relationship system: 5 axes, passive drift, proximity gains, combat hooks, NpcDetailPanel display.

title: Equipment durability: weapons and armor degrade, require repair, create management pressure
description: Equipment durability is an explicit MVP must-have in the GDD. Currently items have no durability and never degrade. Durability creates a management loop: combat damages gear, damaged gear performs worse, repair costs money, and the quartermaster title reduces repair costs. This connects shops, the economy, and combat in a tangible way. Without durability, equipment is a one-time purchase with no ongoing cost — it removes all maintenance pressure from the economy.
design: Add durabilityMax and durabilityCurrent to each equipped weapon and armor piece in NPC state (not the item definition — the instance). Each combat round has a chance to degrade equipped items (base 5% per action, higher for heavy use). At 50% durability: -10% accuracy or +5% damage reduction for armor. At 20%: item is 'damaged' — visible badge on roster, -25% effectiveness. At 0%: item is 'broken' — no effect, must be repaired before use. Repair action in shops: costs repairCost from item definition. Quartermaster title reduces repair costs by stated %. End-of-day summary notes damaged items.
acceptance_criteria: NPC equipped items have durability values visible on the NPC panel. Combat degrades durability. Damaged items show a badge. Shop has a repair action. Quartermaster title shows price reduction. Broken items cannot be used until repaired.
close_reason: Equipment durability: degradation in combat, tier modifiers, repair in ShopsScreen, end-of-day warnings.

title: Weapon and armor content: full data catalog for 40-60 pieces
description: The GDD calls for 40-60 weapons and armor pieces in MVP. We have 0 weapons.json and 0 armor.json. The combat equipment bead (afc) implements the mechanical resolution — this bead creates the actual content. Without content the combat system has nothing to draw from. Needed: data/definitions/weapons.json with at least 12 weapon entries covering all weapon classes (dagger, sword, spear, hammer, crossbow, rifle, shotgun, pistol, staff, special). Each weapon: id, name, category, weaponClass, tier, value, weight, rarity, tags, damageMin, damageMax, accuracy, armorPiercing, speed, rangeType, rangeModifierClose, rangeModifierDistant, critChance, staggerChance, durabilityMax, description. data/definitions/armor.json with at least 8 entries covering light/medium/heavy/specialized, each with: soak, evasionPenalty, speedPenalty, durabilityMax, repairCost, slotCoverage, resistances, description.
design: Start with 3 tiers of 4 weapon classes: daggers (fast/crit), swords (balanced), rifles (Distant+), hammers (stagger/armor-breaking). Add 2 crossbows and 2 pistols for flexibility. For armor: leather (light), chainmail (medium), plate (heavy), reinforced coat (specialized). All items should have world-grounded names and descriptions from the city's material culture — not generic fantasy names.
acceptance_criteria: data/definitions/weapons.json exists with 12+ entries. data/definitions/armor.json exists with 8+ entries. Each entry has full combat stat fields. NPC loadout can reference these weapon ids. Shop inventory can offer them.
close_reason: weapons.json (19 entries) and armor.json (10 entries) created with full combat stats and Valdenmoor-grounded names.

title: Quest and contract framework: structured missions with briefing, objectives, and rewards
description: The GDD calls for 10-15 quests in MVP. Mission stakes bead covers returning from combat with consequences but not the quest framework itself — the structure of how missions are found, accepted, tracked, and resolved. Needed: a contract board where active and available missions are listed, a mission briefing showing employer, risk, reward, and faction context, an objective tracker (combat victory, deliver item, reach district, survive N turns), and a resolution system that marks the quest complete or failed and applies rewards/consequences. Without this framework missions are just combat encounters — they have no narrative purpose and no campaign feel.
design: Quest template schema: { id, title, employerFactionId, enemyFactionId, districtId, briefing (text), objectiveType (combat/delivery/investigation), rewardCredits, rewardStanding, penaltyStanding, timeLimit (days, optional) }. Seed 3 quest templates. New screen: ContractBoardScreen — shows available and active quests. Quest state in Redux: availableQuests[], activeQuests[], completedQuestIds[]. Command: acceptQuest, completeQuest, failQuest. Quest completion triggers reward dispatch and faction standing adjustments.
acceptance_criteria: Player can see available contracts on a contract board screen. Accepting a quest adds it to active. Completing combat tied to a quest resolves the quest. Rewards and standing changes are logged. Completed quests are archived.
close_reason: Quest framework: 5 templates, accept/complete/fail actions, ContractBoardScreen, timed quest expiry.

title: NPC departure and betrayal: the other end of the retention loop
description: Recruitment without departure is not a loop — it is accumulation. The threat of losing people is what makes keeping them meaningful. Departure types to implement: (1) VOLUNTARY DEPARTURE — loyalty below threshold, NPC gives a warning event, then leaves if unaddressed. They take their skills and may take information to rivals. (2) RIVAL POACHING — a rival organization targets a dissatisfied NPC with a better offer. Player gets an intelligence warning if they have a spymaster assigned, otherwise it happens silently. (3) BETRAYAL — a deeply disloyal NPC (loyalty < 15, fear high) may actively betray the player: selling faction standing information to rivals, unlocking a district for an enemy operation, or triggering a surprise combat encounter. (4) DEATH — permanent loss in combat if health reaches 0. Death of a high-loyalty NPC should produce grief states in close relationship partners on the roster. (5) ARREST — a high-criminal-exposure NPC gets arrested by the Civic Compact. Player can bribe, negotiate, or abandon them.
design: End-of-day tick evaluates departure risk for each NPC: loyalty < 30 → departure_warning event added to log. Loyalty < 15 → rival poaching check (rival org attempts offer, player can counter). Loyalty < 10 → betrayal roll based on NPC ruthlessness trait. Betrayal outcome determined by NPC skills: high intrigue → information leak. high negotiation → rival faction deal. high combat → ambush event. Rival orgs track which player NPCs are dissatisfied and prioritize poaching them.
acceptance_criteria: An NPC with loyalty below 30 triggers a warning in the activity log. If unaddressed over 3 days, they depart. Departure is logged with reason. Rival orgs can poach unhappy NPCs. At least one NPC departure type has been tested end-to-end.
close_reason: Implemented in Wave 9 npc-departure-agent: evaluateNpcDeparture pure function, wired into endDay, betrayal fires faction boost + modal event. 4 tests.

title: Reputation as recruitment attractor: how your name shapes who wants to work for you
description: Your reputation in the city should determine who is willing to be found by you, and on what terms. A player known for treating their people well gets approached by better candidates. A player who executes surrendered enemies gets harder, more desperate recruits but loses access to principled ones. A player with high Foundry League standing gets first access to League-affiliated engineers. A player with criminal standing unlocks black-market contacts who would never appear in a legal context.
